content:
BINARY BASICS
Base: 2
Digits: 0, 1
1 bit = a single binary digit
4 bits = 1 nibble
8 bits = 1 byte
POSITION VALUES (RIGHT TO LEFT)
Bit positions:  ... 5  4  3  2  1  0
Powers of 2:    ...32 16  8  4  2  1
BINARY → DECIMAL
Multiply each bit by its power of 2 and add.
Example:
Binary: 1011
= 1*8 + 0*4 + 1*2 + 1*1
= 11
DECIMAL → BINARY
Divide by 2 repeatedly OR subtract highest powers of 2.
Example:
Decimal: 13
13 = 8 + 4 + 1
Binary: 1101
BINARY ↔ HEXADECIMAL
1 hex digit = 4 bits
Hex to Binary:
0 = 0000
1 = 0001
2 = 0010
3 = 0011
4 = 0100
5 = 0101
6 = 0110
7 = 0111
8 = 1000
9 = 1001
A = 1010
B = 1011
C = 1100
D = 1101
E = 1110
F = 1111
Binary to Hex:
Group bits in sets of 4 from the right.
Example:
Binary: 11010111
Groups: 1101 0111
Hex: D7
COMMON BINARY SIZES
8 bits  = 1 byte
16 bits = 2 bytes
32 bits = 4 bytes
64 bits = 8 bytes
SIGNED INTEGERS (TWO'S COMPLEMENT)
Most significant bit (leftmost) = sign bit
0 = positive
1 = negative
To negate a number:
1) Invert bits
2) Add 1
Example:
00000101 = +5
Invert: 11111010
Add 1: 11111011 = -5
BITWISE OPERATIONS

content:
1 AND 1 = 1, else 0
OR (|):
1 OR 1 = 1, 1 OR 0 = 1, else 0
XOR (^):
1 XOR 0 = 1, 0 XOR 1 = 1, else 0
NOT (~):
Flips all bits
Left Shift (<<):
Moves bits left, fills with 0
Right Shift (>>):
Moves bits right, fills with sign bit (arithmetic) or 0 (logical)
ASCII QUICK REFERENCE (DEC → CHAR)
32 = space
48 = 0
49 = 1
50 = 2
51 = 3
52 = 4
53 = 5
54 = 6
55 = 7
56 = 8
57 = 9
65 = A
66 = B
67 = C
68 = D
69 = E
70 = F
97 = a
98 = b
99 = c
100 = d
101 = e
102 = f
END OF CHEAT SHEET

enable                    # Enter privileged EXEC mode (# prompt)
configure terminal        # Enter global configuration mode

exit                      # Exit current mode
interface FastEthernet0/0 # Enter interface config mode
interface FastEthernet0/1 # Enter interface config mode
interface GigabitEthernet0/0 # Enter gigabit interface config mode
interface GigabitEthernet0/1 # Enter gigabit interface config mode
ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0  # Assign IP address to interface
no shutdown               # Enable interface
shutdown                  # Disable interface
show ip interface brief   # Show all interfaces with IP and status

vlan 20                   # Create VLAN 20

name HR                   # Name VLAN
switchport mode access    # Set port to access mode

line vty 0 4

show ip ssh                  # Show SSH status

show ip interface brief        # Verify ACL applied
debug arp                     # Debug ARP activity
show cdp neighbors detail     # Show detailed neighbor info
show ip ospf neighbor          # Show OSPF neighbors

show ip route rip               # Show RIP routes

show ip nat translations        # Show NAT table

interface FastEthernet0/0

show spanning-tree              # Verify STP

content:
Create VLAN:
enable                        # Enter privileged EXEC mode (# prompt)
configure terminal            # Enter global configuration mode
hostname SW1                  # Set switch hostname to SW1
# --- Set passwords ---
enable secret cisco123        # Set privileged EXEC mode password
line console 0                # Enter console line config
password cisco123             # Set console password
login                         # Require login on console
exit
line vty 0 4                  # Enter VTY lines for Telnet/SSH
password cisco123             # Set VTY password
login                         # Require login on VTY
exit
# --- Set login banner ---
banner motd "Welcome to SW1! Authorized users only!" # Set message of the day banner
# --- Create VLANs ---
vlan 10                       # Create VLAN 10
name Sales                     # Name VLAN 10 as "Sales"
vlan 20                       # Create VLAN 20
name HR                        # Name VLAN 20 as "HR"
exit                           # Exit VLAN config mode
# --- Assign access ports ---
interface FastEthernet0/1      # Enter interface Fa0/1
switchport mode access         # Set port as access port
switchport access vlan 10      # Assign to VLAN 10 (Sales)
description Sales PC           # Add description
exit
interface FastEthernet0/2      # Enter interface Fa0/2
switchport mode access         # Set port as access port
switchport access vlan 20      # Assign to VLAN 20 (HR)
description HR PC              # Add description
exit
# --- Configure trunk port (for inter-switch link) ---
interface FastEthernet0/24     # Port connecting to another switch
switchport mode trunk           # Set as trunk port
switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20 # Allow VLAN 10 and 20 on trunk
description Trunk to SW2       # Add description
exit
# --- Verification ---
show vlan brief                # Verify VLANs and port assignments
show interfaces status         # Verify interface statuses
show running-config            # Verify entire configuration
# --- Save configuration ---
copy running-config startup-config # Save configuration
write memory                   # Shortcut to save configuration

# View all A records in zone
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName "BMC.test" -RRType A | Select-Object HostName, @{N="IP";E={$_.RecordData.IPv4Address}}
# Remove stale DNS records
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName "BMC.test" -RRType A -Name "@" -RecordData "192.168.1.1" -Force
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName "BMC.test" -RRType A -Name "@" -RecordData "192.168.127.177" -Force
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName "BMC.test" -RRType A -Name "DomainDnsZones" -RecordData "192.168.1.1" -Force
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName "BMC.test" -RRType A -Name "ForestDnsZones" -RecordData "192.168.1.1" -Force
# Disable NAT adapter from registering in DNS
Set-DnsClient -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet1" -RegisterThisConnectionsAddress $false
# Re-register DNS
ipconfig /registerdns
ipconfig /flushdns
Restart-Service Netlogon

Invoke-Command -ComputerName W2022Core2 -ScriptBlock {
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\ServerManager\ServicingStorage\ServerComponentCache\DHCPServer" -Name "ConfigurationState" -Value 2

# Set DHCP DNS credentials (fixes error 20079)
$cred = Get-Credential BMC\Administrator
Set-DhcpServerDnsCredential -Credential $cred

section: 4. DHCP SCOPE CONFIGURATION (DC1 - managing Core remotely)
content:
# Create scope
Add-DhcpServerv4Scope -ComputerName W2022Core2 `
-Name "BMC Scope" `
-StartRange 192.168.14.100 `
-EndRange 192.168.14.199 `
-SubnetMask 255.255.255.0 `
-State Active
# Option 003 - Router/Gateway (RFC 2132)
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ComputerName W2022Core2 -ScopeId 192.168.14.0 -OptionId 3 -Value 192.168.14.1
# Option 006 - DNS Server (RFC 2132)
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ComputerName W2022Core2 -ScopeId 192.168.14.0 -OptionId 6 -Value 192.168.14.2
# Option 015 - Domain Name (RFC 2132)
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ComputerName W2022Core2 -ScopeId 192.168.14.0 -OptionId 15 -Value "bmc.test"
# Verify
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope -ComputerName W2022Core2
Get-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ComputerName W2022Core2 -ScopeId 192.168.14.0

# Check zone files
sudo named-checkzone bmc.example /var/named/bmc.example.zone
sudo named-checkzone 15.168.192.in-addr.arpa /var/named/bmc.example.reverse
# Check full config as named user
sudo -u named named-checkconf -z
# Set correct ownership
sudo chown named:named /var/named/bmc.example.zone
sudo chown named:named /var/named/bmc.example.reverse
# Restart BIND
sudo systemctl restart named
sudo systemctl status named
# Open firewall
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dns --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

DC1 (Windows Server GUI)
Ethernet0   192.168.14.1    (LAN)
Ethernet1   192.168.127.177 (NAT - DNS reg disabled)
W2022Core2 (Windows Core - DHCP)
Ethernet0   192.168.14.4
Rocky1/roshan.hu (Linux - BIND DNS)
ens160      192.168.15.1    (Linux segment)
ens224      192.168.127.x   (NAT)
Windows 10 Client
Windows Segment  DHCP 192.168.14.x  DNS: 192.168.14.1
Linux Segment    DHCP 192.168.15.x  DNS: 192.168.15.1
DHCP Scope (W2022Core2):
Range:   192.168.14.100-199
Gateway: 192.168.14.1 (opt 003)
DNS:     192.168.14.2 (opt 006)
Domain:  bmc.test     (opt 015)
DNS Zones:
bmc.test    -> Windows DNS DC1    (192.168.14.1)
bmc.example -> Linux BIND Rocky1  (192.168.15.1)

content:
====================  BULK CREATE AD USERS FROM CSV  =================
# Path to your CSV
$csv = Import-Csv "C:\Users\Administrator\Documents\BIPL_Users\BIPL_Users\patienten4.csv"
foreach ($user in $csv) {
# Extract CN and OU from DN
$dnParts = $user.DN -split ","
$cn = $dnParts[0] -replace "^CN=", ""
$ouPath = ($dnParts[1..($dnParts.Count - 1)]) -join ","
# Build AD user parameters
$params = @{
Name               = $cn
GivenName          = $user.givenName
Surname            = $user.sn
SamAccountName     = $user.sAMAccountName
UserPrincipalName  = $user.userPrincipalName
DisplayName        = $user.displayName
Description        = $user.Description
Path               = $ouPath
Enabled            = $true
AccountPassword    = (ConvertTo-SecureString "Welkom123!" -AsPlainText -Force)
}
# Create the user
New-ADUser @params
}
=======================  SCRIPT 1: LIST PROGRAMS  ====================
Get-ItemProperty HKLM:\Software\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\* |
Select-Object DisplayName, DisplayVersion, Publisher, InstallDate |
Format-Table –AutoSize
====================  SCRIPT 2: CHECK DISK SPACE  ====================
Get-PSDrive -PSProvider FileSystem |
Select-Object Name, Used, Free, @{Name="TotalGB";Expression={[math]::round($_.Used/1GB + $_.Free/1GB,2)}}
====================  SCRIPT 3: FIND LARGE FILES  ====================
Get-ChildItem C:\ -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue |
Where-Object {$_.Length -gt 100MB} |
Sort-Object Length -Descending |
Select-Object FullName, Length
=====================  SCRIPT 4: BACKUP A FOLDER  ====================
$source = "C:\Users\YourName\Documents"
$destination = "D:\Backup\" + (Get-Date -Format "yyyyMMdd_HHmmss")
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $destination
Copy-Item -Path $source -Destination $destination -Recurse
Write-Output "Backup completed to $destination"
=====================  SCRIPT 5: SYSTEM INFORMATION  =================
Get-ComputerInfo |
Select-Object CsName, WindowsVersion, OsArchitecture, BiosVersion, CsProcessors, CsMemoryCapacity |
Format-List
====================  SCRIPT 6: LIVE CPU MONITOR  ====================
while ($true) {
Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending | Select-Object -First 5 Name, CPU
Start-Sleep -Seconds 2
Clear-Host
}
====================  SCRIPT 7: NETWORK INFORMATION  =================
Get-NetIPAddress | Select-Object InterfaceAlias, IPAddress, AddressFamily
Get-NetIPConfiguration | Select-Object InterfaceAlias, IPv4Address, IPv6Address, DNSServer
=====================  ACTIVE DIRECTORY CHEAT SHEET  =================

# List All Users
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, Enabled
# Find a Specific User
Get-ADUser -Identity "JohnDoe" -Properties *
# Create a New User
New-ADUser `
-Name "John Doe" `
-GivenName "John" `
-Surname "Doe" `
-SamAccountName "jdoe" `
-UserPrincipalName "jdoe@domain.local" `
-Path "OU=Students,DC=domain,DC=local" `
-AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "Password123" -AsPlainText -Force) `
-Enabled $true
# Enable / Disable User
Disable-ADAccount -Identity "jdoe"
Enable-ADAccount -Identity "jdoe"
# Reset Password
Set-ADAccountPassword -Identity "jdoe" -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "NewPass123" -AsPlainText -Force)
Set-ADUser -Identity "jdoe" -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true

content:
An IPv4 address is 32 bits, written as 4 octets (0-255), separated by dots.
Example:  192  .  168  .   1   .  10
8bits   8bits  8bits  8bits
Every IP address has two parts:
- NETWORK portion  → identifies the network (like a street name)
- HOST portion     → identifies the device (like a house number)
The SUBNET MASK tells you where the split is:
255.255.255.0  =  11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000
1s = network portion     |     0s = host portion

/24 just means "24 bits are set to 1 in the subnet mask."
/24  →  255.255.255.0       (24 ones, 8 zeros)
/26  →  255.255.255.192     (26 ones, 6 zeros)
/27  →  255.255.255.224     (27 ones, 5 zeros)
Bigger slash = smaller subnet = fewer hosts.

content:
NETWORK ADDRESS   → first address, all host bits = 0   (e.g. 192.168.1.0)
BROADCAST ADDRESS → last address,  all host bits = 1   (e.g. 192.168.1.255)
USABLE HOSTS      → everything in between              (e.g. .1 to .254)
Formula:  Usable hosts = 2^(host bits) - 2

4. Next subnet starts immediately after the previous one ends
5. Repeat until all groups are assigned

content:
Given: 192.168.1.0/24
Need:
- Dept A: 50 hosts
- Dept B: 25 hosts
- Dept C: 10 hosts
- Router link: 2 hosts
Step 1 - Sort:  50, 25, 10, 2
Step 2 - Assign:
Dept A (50 hosts) → needs /26 (62 usable)
Network:   192.168.1.0/26
Hosts:     192.168.1.1   – 192.168.1.62
Broadcast: 192.168.1.63
Used:      .0 to .63
Dept B (25 hosts) → needs /27 (30 usable)
Network:   192.168.1.64/27
Hosts:     192.168.1.65  – 192.168.1.94
Broadcast: 192.168.1.95
Used:      .64 to .95
Dept C (10 hosts) → needs /28 (14 usable)
Network:   192.168.1.96/28
Hosts:     192.168.1.97  – 192.168.1.110
Broadcast: 192.168.1.111
Used:      .96 to .111
Router link (2 hosts) → needs /30 (2 usable)
Network:   192.168.1.112/30
Hosts:     192.168.1.113 – 192.168.1.114
Broadcast: 192.168.1.115
Used:      .112 to .115
Remaining free: 192.168.1.116 – 192.168.1.255  (140 addresses saved!)

content:
Decimal | Binary
--------|----------
128   | 10000000
192   | 11000000
224   | 11100000
240   | 11110000
248   | 11111000
252   | 11111100
254   | 11111110
255   | 11111111
These are the only values that appear in subnet masks (last octet).
Bit position values (right to left):
128 | 64 | 32 | 16 | 8 | 4 | 2 | 1
Example: convert 192 to binary
192 - 128 = 64  → bit 7 = 1
64 -  64 =  0  → bit 6 = 1
rest = 0        → bits 5-0 = 0
Result: 11000000 ✓
QUICK CHEAT CARD (cut out and keep)
Block size  =  256 - mask's last octet
Usable hosts = block size - 2
# of subnets = 2^(subnet bits stolen)
/25 → 128 block → 126 hosts   |   /28 → 16 block → 14 hosts
/26 →  64 block →  62 hosts   |   /29 →  8 block →  6 hosts
/27 →  32 block →  30 hosts   |   /30 →  4 block →  2 hosts
VLSM: Sort big→small. Assign. Next subnet starts after last broadcast.

content:
1. Click yellow (!) flag in Server Manager
2. "Promote this server to a domain controller"
3. Add a new forest -> Root domain: BMC.test

7. Review -> Install -> server reboots automatically

2. Test: ping bmc.test
3. Install ADDS -> Promote -> Add new domain to existing forest
4. Parent: BMC.test | New domain name: HA1 (or TA1, HA2, TA2)
5. Follow wizard with default settings

section: SECTION 9: WINDOWS SERVER - COMMAND LINE AD TOOLS
content:
CSVDE (CSV import/export):
csvde -i -v -k -F users.csv
# No passwords | Cannot modify or delete | Needs elevated prompt
LDIFDE (LDIF import/export/modify):
ldifde -i -f user.ldf -s Server2
# Can modify/delete | No passwords | No group membership changes
DSADD (add objects):
dsadd user "CN=John,OU=IT,DC=BMC,DC=test" -upn john@BMC.test -pwd Pa$$w0rd
dsadd ou "OU=NewOU,DC=BMC,DC=test"
dsadd group "CN=MyGroup,OU=IT,DC=BMC,DC=test"
DSMOD (modify objects):
dsmod user "CN=John,OU=IT,DC=BMC,DC=test" -desc "New description"
dsmod user "CN=John,OU=IT,DC=BMC,DC=test" -disabled yes
DSQUERY (search objects):
dsquery user -name "John*"
dsquery user -desc "HA1"
DSMOVE (move objects):
dsmove "CN=John,OU=OldOU,DC=BMC,DC=test" `
-newparent "OU=HA1,OU=Huisartsen,DC=BMC,DC=test"
RUNAS:
runas /user:administrator dsquerydsmove.bat
NETDOM (domain join):
NETDOM join Server1 /d:bmc.test /reboot

section: SECTION 11: WINDOWS SERVER - SYSPREP (for Linked Clones)
content:
1. Set correct date/time on the VM
2. Turn off Windows Update (test environment only!)
3. Run: C:\Windows\System32\sysprep\sysprep.exe
4. Action: OOBE | Check: Generalize | Option: Shutdown
5. Click OK -> wait for shutdown
6. NEVER start the base/parent VM again!
7. VMware: right-click VM -> Manage -> Clone (linked clone)

# Find files by name
find / -name "sshd_config"
find /etc -name "*.conf"
find /var -name "*.log" -mtime -7    # modified in last 7 days
find / -name "*.sh" -type f          # files only
find / -type d -name "ssl"           # directories only
find / -size +100M                   # files larger than 100 MB
find / -user root -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # SUID files
find /var/log -name "*.log" -exec ls -lh {} \;       # exec on results

# Search text in files
grep "error" /var/log/messages       # search for 'error'
grep -i "error" /var/log/messages    # case-insensitive
grep -r "password" /etc/             # recursive search
grep -n "Port" /etc/ssh/sshd_config  # show line numbers
grep -v "#" /etc/ssh/sshd_config     # exclude comment lines
grep -E "(error|warn)" /var/log/messages   # regex: error OR warn
grep -c "Failed" /var/log/secure     # count matching lines

# Locate (uses database, much faster)
locate sshd_config
sudo updatedb                        # update locate database

# which / whereis — find executable path
which python3
whereis sshd

# Redirect output to file
ls -la > /tmp/output.txt          # overwrite
ls -la >> /tmp/output.txt         # append

# Redirect errors
command 2> /tmp/errors.txt        # stderr to file
command > /tmp/out.txt 2>&1       # stdout + stderr to same file
command 2>/dev/null               # discard errors

# Pipes
ipconfig | grep inet              # filter output
cat /etc/passwd | cut -d: -f1     # extract first field
cat /etc/passwd | sort            # sort alphabetically
cat /etc/passwd | sort | uniq     # remove duplicates
ls -la | wc -l                    # count lines
ps aux | grep httpd               # find process

# tee — write to file AND screen
ip addr | tee /tmp/ipinfo.txt

# xargs — pass output as arguments
find /tmp -name "*.tmp" | xargs rm -f
cat servers.txt | xargs ping -c 1

# sort and uniq
sort file.txt
sort -r file.txt                  # reverse sort
sort -n file.txt                  # numeric sort
uniq file.txt                     # remove adjacent duplicates
sort file.txt | uniq -c           # count occurrences

# cut — extract fields
cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd           # first field, colon delimiter
cut -d, -f2 file.csv              # second field, comma delimiter

# awk — pattern + action
awk -F: '{print $1}' /etc/passwd  # print first field
awk '{print $1, $NF}' file.txt    # print first and last field
df -h | awk '{print $1, $5}'      # disk usage: device + percentage

content:
# View permissions
ls -la /etc/passwd
# Output: -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 2416 Jan 1 12:00 /etc/passwd
# Format: [type][owner][group][others]
# r=4, w=2, x=1

# chmod — change permissions
chmod 755 script.sh              # rwxr-xr-x (owner all, group+others r+x)
chmod 640 secret.txt             # rw-r----- (owner rw, group r, others none)
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa        # rw------- (owner rw only — required for SSH keys)
chmod +x script.sh               # add execute for all
chmod u+x script.sh              # add execute for owner only
chmod g-w file.txt               # remove write from group
chmod o-rwx file.txt             # remove all from others
chmod -R 750 /var/www            # recursive: apply to all files inside

# chown — change owner
chown root file.txt              # change owner to root
chown root:apache /var/www       # change owner and group
chown -R user:group /data        # recursive
chown :developers /data/project  # change group only

# chgrp — change group only
chgrp developers /data/project

# Special bits
chmod u+s /tmp/tail              # SUID — runs as file owner
chmod g+s /data/project          # SGID — new files inherit directory group
chmod +t /tmp                    # sticky bit — only owner can delete own files
chmod 4755 /tmp/tail             # SUID via octal (4=SUID, 2=SGID, 1=sticky)
chmod 2770 /data/project         # SGID via octal
chmod 1777 /tmp                  # sticky bit via octal

# Find SUID files (potential security risk)
sudo find / -user root -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
sudo find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # SGID files
sudo find / -perm /6000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # both SUID and SGID

# umask — default permission mask for new files
umask                            # view current umask (e.g. 0022)
# 0022 = new files get 644 (rw-r--r--), directories get 755
umask 027                        # set stricter mask: files=640, dirs=750

# Make umask permanent
nano ~/.bashrc
# Add line: umask 027

# ACLs — fine-grained access control
sudo dnf install acl

# View ACLs on a file
getfacl /data/project

# Set ACL for a specific user
setfacl -m u:jansen:rwx /data/project

# Set ACL for a specific group
setfacl -m g:developers:rx /data/project

# Set default ACL (new files inside inherit this ACL)
setfacl -d -m u:jansen:rwx /data/project

# Remove an ACL entry
setfacl -x u:jansen /data/project

# Remove all ACLs
setfacl -b /data/project

# View users
cat /etc/passwd                           # all user accounts
cat /etc/shadow                           # hashed passwords (root only)
id                                        # current user info: uid, gid, groups
id jansen                                 # specific user info
whoami                                    # just the username
who                                       # who is logged in right now
w                                         # who is logged in + what they're doing
last                                      # login history

# Create user
sudo useradd jansen                       # create user (no home dir on some distros)
sudo useradd -m jansen                    # create user with home directory
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash jansen       # specify shell
sudo useradd -m -G wheel,developers jansen  # add to groups immediately

# Set password
sudo passwd jansen

# Modify user
sudo usermod -s /bin/bash jansen          # change shell
sudo usermod -d /home/newdir jansen       # change home directory
sudo usermod -l newname jansen            # rename user
sudo usermod -a -G wheel jansen           # add to supplementary group
sudo usermod -G developers jansen         # set groups (replaces current groups)
sudo usermod -L jansen                    # lock account
sudo usermod -U jansen                    # unlock account

# Delete user
sudo userdel jansen                       # delete user (keep home dir)
sudo userdel -r jansen                    # delete user and home directory

# Change password expiry
sudo chage -l jansen                      # view password aging info
sudo chage -M 90 jansen                   # password expires after 90 days
sudo chage -E 2025-12-31 jansen           # account expires on date
sudo chage -I -1 -M 99999 jansen          # disable password expiry

# View all connections
nmcli connection show
nmcli con show

# View devices
nmcli device status
nmcli dev status

# Set static IP, gateway, DNS on existing connection
nmcli con mod ens160 ipv4.method manual
nmcli con mod ens160 ipv4.addresses 192.168.5.1/24
nmcli con mod ens160 ipv4.gateway 192.168.5.252
nmcli con mod ens160 ipv4.dns "8.8.8.8 192.168.5.1"

# Enable auto-connect
nmcli con mod ens160 connection.autoconnect yes

# Apply changes
nmcli con down ens160
nmcli con up ens160

# Set DHCP
nmcli con mod ens160 ipv4.method auto
nmcli con up ens160

# Add a new connection manually
nmcli con add type ethernet ifname ens160 con-name MyConn
nmcli con mod MyConn ipv4.method manual ipv4.addresses 10.0.0.5/24 ipv4.gateway 10.0.0.1
nmcli con up MyConn

# Delete a connection
nmcli con delete MyConn

# Interactive TUI configurator (easier on Core)
nmtui

content:
# Ping
ping 8.8.8.8                         # 4 packets (Linux pings forever by default)
ping -c 4 8.8.8.8                    # exactly 4 packets
ping -c 4 -i 0.2 8.8.8.8            # faster: 0.2s interval
ping -s 1400 8.8.8.8                 # large packet (test MTU)
ping6 ::1                            # IPv6 ping

# Trace route
traceroute 8.8.8.8
traceroute -n 8.8.8.8               # no DNS resolution (faster)
mtr 8.8.8.8                         # live traceroute (q to quit)

# DNS lookup
nslookup dc1.school.test
nslookup 172.16.1.1                  # reverse lookup
dig dc1.school.test                  # detailed DNS query
dig dc1.school.test A                # A record
dig school.test MX                   # MX record
dig @8.8.8.8 dc1.school.test         # query specific DNS server
host dc1.school.test                 # simple forward lookup
host -t MX bmc.test                  # MX record

# Test a specific port
nc -zv 192.168.5.1 22               # check if SSH port is open
nc -zv 192.168.5.1 80               # check HTTP
nc -zv -w 3 192.168.5.1 443         # 3 second timeout
telnet 192.168.5.1 80               # old-school port test

# curl — test HTTP services
curl http://192.168.5.1
curl -k https://192.168.5.1         # ignore SSL certificate errors

# View all services

# Start / stop / restart

sudo systemctl stop httpd
sudo systemctl restart httpd
sudo systemctl reload httpd          # reload config without restart
sudo systemctl reload-or-restart httpd

# Enable / disable at boot
sudo systemctl enable httpd
sudo systemctl disable httpd
sudo systemctl enable --now httpd    # enable AND start immediately

# Mask / unmask (prevent a service from being started at all)
sudo systemctl mask httpd
sudo systemctl unmask httpd

# Check if enabled / active

systemctl is-failed httpd

# View service logs

journalctl -u httpd -f               # follow in real time

# View all logs
journalctl
journalctl -n 50                     # last 50 lines
journalctl -f                        # follow in real time
journalctl --since today
journalctl --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl --since "2024-01-01" --until "2024-01-02"

journalctl -p warning                # warnings and above

journalctl -u httpd -u postfix       # multiple services

journalctl _PID=1234

# Boot logs

journalctl -b -1                     # previous boot
journalctl --list-boots              # list all boot sessions

dmesg
dmesg | grep -i error
dmesg | tail -n 30

# Traditional log files

sudo tail -f /var/log/secure         # auth/SSH events
sudo tail -f /var/log/maillog        # mail events
sudo cat /var/log/audit/audit.log    # SELinux audit log

# Check status
sudo systemctl status firewalld
sudo firewall-cmd --state             # running or not running

# View current rules
sudo firewall-cmd --list-all          # full list for default zone
sudo firewall-cmd --list-services     # allowed services
sudo firewall-cmd --list-ports        # allowed ports
sudo firewall-cmd --list-all-zones    # all zones

# Allow a predefined service
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=http --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=https --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=ssh --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=smtp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=samba --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dns --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=imap --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=pop3 --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=imaps --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=pop3s --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=openvpn --permanent

# Allow a specific port
sudo firewall-cmd --add-port=8080/tcp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-port=1194/udp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-port=51820/udp --permanent

# Remove a service or port
sudo firewall-cmd --remove-service=http --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --remove-port=8080/tcp --permanent

# Save runtime to permanent (or use --permanent from the start)
sudo firewall-cmd --runtime-to-permanent

# Apply changes
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Allow source IP or subnet
sudo firewall-cmd --add-source=192.168.5.0/24 --permanent

# Rich rule: allow specific source to specific port
sudo firewall-cmd --add-rich-rule='rule family="ipv4" source address="192.168.5.0/24" port port="443" protocol="tcp" accept' --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

subsection: dnf — Install, Update and Remove Packages
content:
# Search for a package
sudo dnf search httpd
sudo dnf search "web server"

# View package info
sudo dnf info httpd

# Install a package
sudo dnf install httpd
sudo dnf install -y httpd            # skip confirmation
sudo dnf install httpd php php-mysqlnd   # multiple packages

# Install from URL
sudo dnf install -y https://dl.fedoraproject.org/pub/epel/epel-release-latest-8.noarch.rpm

# Remove a package
sudo dnf remove httpd
sudo dnf remove -y httpd

# Update packages
sudo dnf update                      # all packages
sudo dnf update httpd                # specific package
sudo dnf upgrade                     # same as update (newer syntax)

# Check for available updates
sudo dnf check-update

# List installed packages
sudo dnf list installed
sudo dnf list installed | grep php
dnf list installed httpd*

# View installed package files
dnf repoquery -l httpd

# Clean cache
sudo dnf clean all
sudo dnf makecache

# Enable a repository
sudo dnf config-manager --enable powertools
sudo dnf config-manager --enable epel

# View enabled repos
sudo dnf repolist
sudo dnf repolist all

# Check SSH service
sudo systemctl status sshd
sudo systemctl enable --now sshd

# Connect to remote host
ssh user@hostname
ssh user@192.168.5.2
ssh -p 2222 user@hostname            # non-default port
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa user@host     # use specific key

# Connect with X11 forwarding (for GUI apps over SSH)
ssh -X user@hostname

# Run a command on remote host without interactive shell
ssh user@host "df -h"
ssh user@host "sudo systemctl restart httpd"

# Copy file to remote host
scp file.txt user@host:/tmp/
scp -r /localfolder user@host:/tmp/

# Copy file from remote host
scp user@host:/tmp/file.txt /local/

# SCP with non-default port
scp -P 2222 file.txt user@host:/tmp/

# Rsync — efficient sync (only transfers changes)
rsync -avz /local/ user@host:/remote/
rsync -avz --delete /local/ user@host:/remote/   # mirror (delete files not on source)
rsync -avz user@host:/remote/ /local/            # pull from remote

# Key files
# ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa         private key
# ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa.pub     public key
# ~/.ssh/authorized_keys  public keys allowed to log in
# ~/.ssh/known_hosts      fingerprints of hosts connected to
# ~/.ssh/config           per-host SSH settings

content:
# Generate keypair (ECDSA recommended)
ssh-keygen -t ecdsa
# Accept defaults — press Enter for all prompts
# For automation/scripting: no passphrase

# Copy public key to remote server
ssh-copy-id user@hostname
ssh-copy-id user@localhost           # even to self
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa.pub user@host  # specify key

# Test key-based login (should not ask for password)
ssh user@hostname

# Verify key file permissions (SSH refuses to use keys with wrong perms)
ls -al ~/.ssh
# id_ecdsa must be 600 (owner read/write only)
# id_ecdsa.pub can be 644
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa
chmod 644 ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa.pub
chmod 700 ~/.ssh

# After confirming key auth works, disable password auth
sudo nano /etc/ssh/sshd_config
# PasswordAuthentication no
sudo systemctl restart sshd

# View SELinux context on files
ls -Z /var/www
ls -Z /etc/ssh
ls -Z /plf/www

# View context of running processes
ps -eZ | grep httpd
ps -eZ | grep sshd

# View context of current user
id -Z

# Temporarily apply a context (lost after restorecon or relabel)
sudo chcon -R -t httpd_sys_content_t /plf/www
sudo chcon -t samba_share_t /home/secure
sudo chcon -t ssh_home_t ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

# Permanently add context rule (survives relabel)
sudo semanage fcontext -a -t httpd_sys_content_t "/plf/www(/.*)?"
sudo semanage fcontext -a -t samba_share_t "/home/secure(/.*)?"

# Apply permanent rules to existing files
sudo restorecon -R -v /plf/www
sudo restorecon -R -v /home/secure

# Reset all contexts to default
sudo restorecon -R -v /

# View allowed HTTP ports
sudo semanage port -l | grep http

# Add a custom port for a service
sudo semanage port -a -t http_port_t -p tcp 8080

# Common SELinux types:
# httpd_sys_content_t  — files Apache can serve
# samba_share_t        — files Samba can share
# ssh_home_t           — SSH key files in ~/.ssh
# postfix_spool_t      — Postfix mail spool
# named_zone_t         — BIND DNS zone files

# Check SELinux denials in audit log
sudo grep AVC /var/log/audit/audit.log
sudo grep AVC /var/log/audit/audit.log | tail -n 20

# Search denials
sudo ausearch -m AVC,USER_AVC,SELINUX_ERR -ts today

# Translate why something was denied
sudo grep httpd /var/log/audit/audit.log | audit2why
sudo grep samba /var/log/audit/audit.log | audit2why

# Generate a custom SELinux policy module to allow denied action
sudo grep httpd /var/log/audit/audit.log | audit2allow -M myhttpd
sudo semodule -i myhttpd.pp

# Install troubleshooting tools
sudo dnf install setroubleshoot-server setools-console

# Read alerts from setroubleshoot
sudo sealert -a /var/log/audit/audit.log

# List available booleans (on/off switches for common services)
getsebool -a
getsebool -a | grep httpd
getsebool -a | grep samba

# Set a boolean
sudo setsebool -P httpd_can_network_connect on
sudo setsebool -P samba_enable_home_dirs on
sudo setsebool -P httpd_use_nfs on

# Install
sudo dnf install httpd

# Enable and start
sudo systemctl enable --now httpd
sudo systemctl status httpd

# Open firewall
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=http --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=https --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Test default page
curl http://localhost
curl http://rocky1.bmc.example

# Config files
# /etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf          main config
# /etc/httpd/conf.d/                  additional vhost configs
# /var/www/html/                      default webroot
# /var/log/httpd/error_log            error log
# /var/log/httpd/access_log           access log

# Test config before restarting
sudo apachectl configtest

# Restart after config change

# Symptom: 403 Forbidden after moving webroot outside /var/www
# Cause: SELinux label is wrong on new directory

# Create new webroot
sudo mkdir -p /plf/www/html
sudo chmod -R 755 /plf

# Edit main Apache config (replace /var/www with /plf/www)
sudo nano /etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf
# Ctrl+W to search, Alt+R to search+replace in nano

sudo systemctl restart httpd

# Check context difference
ls -Z /var/www        # shows httpd_sys_content_t
ls -Z /plf/www        # shows unlabeled_t or default_t (wrong)

# Diagnose the SELinux denial
sudo grep httpd /var/log/audit/audit.log | audit2why

# Fix temporarily
sudo chcon -R -t httpd_sys_content_t /plf/www

# Fix permanently
sudo semanage fcontext -a -t httpd_sys_content_t "/plf/www(/.*)?"
sudo restorecon -R -v /plf/www

# Verify
ls -Z /plf/www
curl http://rocky1.bmc.example/test.html

[secure]
path = /home/secure

# Test config

content:
# Create Linux user and group
sudo useradd hu
sudo groupadd smbgrp
sudo usermod -a -G smbgrp hu

# Set Samba password (separate from Linux password)
sudo smbpasswd -a hu

# Enable/disable Samba user
sudo smbpasswd -e hu       # enable
sudo smbpasswd -d hu       # disable

# List Samba users
sudo pdbedit -L

# Create and configure share directory
sudo mkdir /home/secure
sudo chown -R hu:smbgrp /home/secure
sudo chmod -R 0770 /home/secure

# Fix SELinux context for Samba
sudo chcon -t samba_share_t /home/secure
sudo semanage fcontext -a -t samba_share_t "/home/secure(/.*)?"
sudo restorecon -R -v /home/secure

# Open firewall for Samba
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=samba --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Test from Linux client
smbclient //rocky1/secure -U hu

# List available shares
smbclient -L rocky1 -U hu

# Forward zone file: /var/named/bmc.test.db
# $TTL 3H
# @ IN SOA @ hostmaster.bmc.test. (
#         2024010101 ; serial (increment after every change)
#         1H         ; refresh
#         1M         ; retry
#         1D         ; expire
#         3M )       ; minimum
# NS  rocky1
# MX  10 rocky1
# rocky1  A  192.168.5.1
# rocky2  A  192.168.5.2
# srv2    A  10.13.14.1
# www     CNAME  rocky1

# Reverse zone file: /var/named/192.168.5.db
# $TTL 3H
# @ IN SOA @ hostmaster.bmc.test. (
#         2024010101 ; serial
#         1H ; refresh
#         1M ; retry
#         1D ; expire
#         3M ) ; minimum
# NS  rocky1.bmc.test.
# 1   PTR  rocky1.bmc.test.
# 2   PTR  rocky2.bmc.test.

# Set correct ownership and permissions
sudo chown root:named /var/named/bmc.test.db
sudo chmod 640 /var/named/bmc.test.db

# Check zone file syntax
sudo named-checkzone bmc.test /var/named/bmc.test.db
sudo named-checkzone 5.168.192.in-addr.arpa /var/named/192.168.5.db

# Reload zones after changes (without full restart)
sudo rndc reload
sudo systemctl reload named

# Install

# Firewall
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=smtp --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Check SMTP port is open
sudo ss -antp | grep :25

# Install
sudo dnf install -y dovecot

# Config files to edit:
# /etc/dovecot/dovecot.conf           -> listen = *, ::

# service auth {

#     user = postfix
#     group = postfix
#   }
# }

# Enable and start

# Open firewall

sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=pop3  --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=imaps --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=pop3s --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Check listening ports

content:
# Install LVM tools
sudo dnf install lvm2

# Step 1: Initialize physical volume (a disk or partition)
sudo pvcreate /dev/sdb
sudo pvcreate /dev/sdb1             # on a partition

# Step 2: Create volume group
sudo vgcreate vg_data /dev/sdb
sudo vgcreate vg_data /dev/sdb /dev/sdc   # multiple disks

# Step 3: Create logical volumes
sudo lvcreate -L 5G   -n lv_web  vg_data
sudo lvcreate -L 10G  -n lv_db   vg_data
sudo lvcreate -l 100%FREE -n lv_all vg_data   # use all remaining space

# Step 4: Create filesystems
sudo mkfs.xfs  /dev/vg_data/lv_web
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/vg_data/lv_db

# Step 5: Mount
sudo mkdir -p /data/web /data/db
sudo mount /dev/vg_data/lv_web /data/web
sudo mount /dev/vg_data/lv_db  /data/db

# Step 6: Make persistent via fstab
sudo nano /etc/fstab
# /dev/vg_data/lv_web   /data/web   xfs   defaults   0  0
# /dev/vg_data/lv_db    /data/db    ext4  defaults   0  0
sudo mount -a                       # verify fstab mounts work

# View everything
pvdisplay
vgdisplay
lvdisplay
pvs
vgs
lvs

# Extend a logical volume
sudo lvextend -L +2G /dev/vg_data/lv_web      # add 2 GB
sudo lvextend -L 10G /dev/vg_data/lv_web      # set to exactly 10 GB
sudo lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/vg_data/lv_web  # use all free space in VG

# Resize filesystem after extending LV
# ext3/ext4 (can be online):
sudo resize2fs /dev/vg_data/lv_web
# XFS (must be mounted, cannot shrink):
sudo xfs_growfs /data/web

# Check free space in VG
sudo vgdisplay vg_data
vgs

# Reduce a logical volume (ext4 only, must be unmounted)
sudo umount /data/db
sudo e2fsck -f /dev/vg_data/lv_db
sudo resize2fs /dev/vg_data/lv_db 5G
sudo lvreduce -L 5G /dev/vg_data/lv_db

# Take a snapshot
sudo lvcreate -L 1G -s -n lv_db_snap /dev/vg_data/lv_db

# Remove a logical volume
sudo umount /data/db
sudo lvremove /dev/vg_data/lv_db

# Remove a volume group
sudo vgremove vg_data

# Remove a physical volume
sudo pvremove /dev/sdb

content:
# Install mdadm
sudo dnf install mdadm

# Monitor RAID status
cat /proc/mdstat
sudo watch cat /proc/mdstat          # auto-refresh

# Create RAID1 (mirror) with 2 disks
sudo mdadm --create /dev/md0 --level=1 --raid-devices=2 /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1
# Answer 'y' when prompted

# Create RAID5 with 3 disks
sudo mdadm --create /dev/md0 --level=5 --raid-devices=3 /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1 /dev/sdd1

# View RAID details
sudo mdadm --detail /dev/md0
sudo mdadm --examine /dev/sdb1

# Create filesystem on RAID array
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/md0
sudo mount /dev/md0 /data/raid

# Persist RAID configuration
sudo mkdir /etc/mdadm
echo DEVICE /dev/sdb1 /dev/sdc1 | sudo tee /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf
sudo mdadm --detail --scan | sudo tee -a /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf
cat /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf

# RAID failure simulation and recovery
sudo mdadm /dev/md0 -f /dev/sdb1    # mark disk as failed
sudo mdadm /dev/md0 -r /dev/sdb1    # remove failed disk
sudo mdadm /dev/md0 -a /dev/sdb1    # re-add disk (resync starts)

# RAID monitoring
cat /proc/mdstat
sudo grep 'md[0-9]' /var/log/messages
sudo mdadm --detail /dev/md0

# View block devices
lsblk
lsblk -o +FSTYPE                     # include filesystem type
lsblk -f                             # filesystem info

# Partition a disk with fdisk
sudo fdisk /dev/sdb
# n = new partition
# p = primary
# t = change type (fd = Linux RAID, 8e = Linux LVM, 83 = Linux)
# w = write and exit
# q = quit without saving

# Interactive partition tool (easier)
sudo cfdisk /dev/sdb

# Create filesystems
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sdb1
sudo mkfs.xfs  /dev/sdb2
sudo mkfs.ext3 /dev/sdb3
sudo mkfs.ext2 /dev/sdb4

# Mount and unmount
sudo mkdir -p /data/disk1
sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /data/disk1
sudo mount -v /dev/sdb1 /data/disk1  # verbose
sudo umount /data/disk1
sudo umount /dev/sdb1

# View mounts and disk usage
mount
df -h
df -hT                               # includes filesystem type
du -sh /var/log                      # size of a directory
du -sh /var/log/*                    # size of each subdirectory

# View partition UUIDs
blkid
sudo blkid /dev/sdb1

# fstab — persistent mounts
sudo nano /etc/fstab
# Format: <device> <mountpoint> <fstype> <options> <dump> <pass>
# /dev/sdb1                   /data/disk1   ext4  defaults  0  2
# UUID=xxxx-xxxx              /data/disk1   ext4  defaults  0  2
# /dev/vg_data/lv_web         /data/web     xfs   defaults  0  0

# Apply all fstab mounts (test without rebooting)
sudo mount -a

# Check filesystem integrity (must be unmounted)
sudo fsck /dev/sdb1
sudo fsck -f /dev/sdb1               # force check

# dump — filesystem-level backup (for ext2/3/4)
sudo dnf install dump

# Level 0 = full backup
sudo dump -0u -f /backup/full.dump /data/db

# Level 1 = incremental (changes since last level 0)
sudo dump -1u -f /backup/inc1.dump /data/db

# Restore
mkdir /tmp/restore && cd /tmp/restore
sudo restore -f /backup/full.dump -x filename.txt   # restore specific file
sudo restore -r -f /backup/full.dump                # restore everything

# tar — archive and compress
tar -czf backup.tar.gz /data/             # create compressed archive
tar -cjf backup.tar.bz2 /data/           # bzip2 compression
tar -xzf backup.tar.gz -C /restore/      # extract to specific dir
tar -tzf backup.tar.gz                   # list contents without extracting
tar -czf - /data/ | ssh user@host "cat > /backup/data.tar.gz"  # backup over SSH

# rsync — efficient sync and backup
rsync -av /data/ /backup/                         # local backup
rsync -avz /data/ user@server:/backup/            # remote backup over SSH
rsync -avz --delete /data/ user@server:/backup/   # mirror (remove files not on source)
rsync -n /data/ /backup/                          # dry run (preview changes)

# Scheduled backup via cron
crontab -e
# 0 2 * * * rsync -avz /data/ /backup/    # run at 2am every day

# View processes
ps aux                               # all processes, full listing
ps aux | grep httpd                  # find specific process
ps -ef                               # alternative full listing
ps -ef | grep [s]shd                 # bracket trick to exclude grep itself

# Top / htop — interactive process viewer
top                                  # press q to quit, k to kill
htop                                 # better version (sudo dnf install htop)

# Send signals
kill 1234                            # send SIGTERM (graceful stop)
kill -9 1234                         # send SIGKILL (force kill)
kill -HUP 1234                       # send SIGHUP (reload config)
killall httpd                        # kill all processes named httpd
killall -9 httpd
pkill httpd                          # kill by name
pkill -u jansen                      # kill all processes by user

# Run process in background
some_command &

# Move running process to background
# Ctrl+Z (pause), then:
bg                                   # resume in background
fg                                   # bring back to foreground
jobs                                 # list background jobs

# nohup — keep process running after logout
nohup long_script.sh &
nohup long_script.sh > /tmp/output.log 2>&1 &

# nice — set process priority (-20 = highest, 19 = lowest)
nice -n 10 heavy_script.sh
renice 5 -p 1234                     # change priority of running process

# Edit current user's crontab
crontab -e

# List crontab entries
crontab -l

# Remove crontab
crontab -r

# Edit another user's crontab (as root)
sudo crontab -u jansen -e

# Cron format:
# minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week command
# *      *    *             *     *           command
# 0      2    *             *     *           /scripts/backup.sh   -> 2:00 AM daily
# 0      0    *             *     0           /scripts/weekly.sh   -> midnight every Sunday
# */5    *    *             *     *           /scripts/check.sh    -> every 5 minutes
# 0      9-17 *             *     1-5         /scripts/work.sh     -> 9am-5pm weekdays

# System crontab files
/etc/crontab
/etc/cron.d/
/etc/cron.daily/
/etc/cron.weekly/
/etc/cron.monthly/

# At — run a one-time job at a specific time
at 3am tomorrow
at> /scripts/backup.sh
at> Ctrl+D

# View scheduled at jobs
atq

# Remove an at job
atrm 2                               # remove job number 2

# Disk usage summary
df -h                                # human-readable
df -hT                               # includes filesystem type
df -i                                # inode usage

# Directory size
du -sh /var/log                      # total size of directory
du -sh /var/log/*                    # size of each item inside
du -sh /* 2>/dev/null                # size of each top-level directory
du --max-depth=1 -h /var/            # one level deep

# Find largest files
find / -type f -size +100M 2>/dev/null | xargs ls -lh
du -ah / 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -20

# SMART disk health
sudo dnf install smartmontools
sudo smartctl -a /dev/sda            # full SMART report
sudo smartctl -H /dev/sda            # health status only
sudo smartctl -t short /dev/sda      # run short self-test

# View environment variables
env
printenv
echo $PATH
echo $HOME
echo $USER
echo $SHELL
echo $HOSTNAME

# Set variable for current session
export MYVAR=hello
export PATH=$PATH:/opt/myapp/bin

# Unset variable
unset MYVAR

# Set variable permanently (per user)
nano ~/.bashrc
# Add: export MYVAR=hello
source ~/.bashrc                     # apply without logout

# Set system-wide variable
sudo nano /etc/environment
# Format: MYVAR="hello"

# Shell config files
# ~/.bashrc          — user-specific, non-login shells
# ~/.bash_profile    — user-specific, login shells
# /etc/profile       — system-wide, login shells
# /etc/profile.d/    — system-wide scripts loaded by /etc/profile
# /etc/bashrc        — system-wide, non-login shells

# Aliases
alias ll='ls -la'
alias grep='grep --color=auto'
alias ..='cd ..'

# Save alias permanently
nano ~/.bashrc
# Add: alias ll='ls -la'

# Generate private key
openssl genrsa -aes128 2048 > server.key

# Remove passphrase from key (needed for services like Apache/Postfix)
openssl rsa -in server.key -out server.key

# Create Certificate Signing Request (CSR)
openssl req -utf8 -new -key server.key -out server.csr
# Fill in prompts:
# Country Name: NL
# State: Utrecht
# Locality: Utrecht
# Organization: bmc.test
# Common Name: rocky1.bmc.test  (must match hostname!)

# Self-sign the certificate (valid 10 years)
openssl x509 -in server.csr -out server.crt -req -signkey server.key -days 3652

# Set permissions
chmod 600 server.key

# Verify certificate details
openssl x509 -in server.crt -text -noout
openssl x509 -in server.crt -noout -subject -dates

# Test SSL connection
openssl s_client -connect rocky1.bmc.test:443
openssl s_client -connect rocky1.bmc.test:993   # IMAPS
openssl s_client -connect rocky1.bmc.test:587   # SMTP submission

# Apply SELinux context to certificates
restorecon -v /etc/pki/tls/certs/server.crt
restorecon -v /etc/pki/tls/certs/server.key

# Common certificate locations on Rocky/RHEL
# /etc/pki/tls/certs/         — certificate files (.crt)
# /etc/pki/tls/private/       — private keys (.key)
# /etc/pki/ca-trust/          — CA trust bundle

# Install EPEL and OpenVPN
sudo dnf -y install https://dl.fedoraproject.org/pub/epel/epel-release-latest-8.noarch.rpm
sudo dnf install openvpn easy-rsa -y

# Copy easy-rsa
sudo cp -rp /usr/share/easy-rsa/3.0/ /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0
sudo cp -p /usr/share/doc/easy-rsa/vars.example /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/vars

# Edit vars
sudo nano /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/vars
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_COUNTRY   "NL"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_PROVINCE  "Utrecht"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_CITY      "Utrecht"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_ORG       "bmc.test"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_EMAIL     "security@bmc.test"

# Generate PKI
cd /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/
./easyrsa clean-all
./easyrsa gen-dh
./easyrsa build-ca
./easyrsa build-server-full server1 nopass
./easyrsa build-client-full client1

# Generate TLS auth key
openvpn --genkey --secret /etc/openvpn/server/ta.key

# View generated files
ls /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/
ls /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/issued/

# Copy certs to client
sudo scp -p /etc/openvpn/server/ta.key \
  /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/ca.crt \
  /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/issued/client1.crt \
  /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/private/client1.key \
  user@clientIP:/tmp

# Start server
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=openvpn --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload
sudo openvpn --cd /etc/openvpn/server --config server.ovpn

# Check current IP forwarding status
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward
# 0 = disabled, 1 = enabled

# Enable temporarily (until reboot)
sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

# Enable permanently
sudo nano /etc/sysctl.conf
# Add: net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
sudo sysctl -p                       # apply without reboot

# NAT masquerade (forward VPN clients to LAN)
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -i tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -o tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 nat POSTROUTING 0 -o ens160 -j MASQUERADE

# Permanent versions
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -i tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -o tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 nat POSTROUTING 0 -o ens160 -j MASQUERADE
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Verify

#!/bin/bash
# First line must be the shebang

# Variables
NAME="Rocky"
echo "Hello, $NAME"

# Read user input
read -p "Enter hostname: " HOST
echo "You entered: $HOST"

# If statement
if [ -f /etc/hosts ]; then
    echo "File exists"
fi

if [ "$NAME" == "Rocky" ]; then
    echo "Name is Rocky"
else
    echo "Name is something else"
fi

# Check if service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet httpd; then
    echo "httpd is running"
fi

# For loop
for SERVER in dc1 srv1 rras1; do
    ping -c 1 $SERVER && echo "$SERVER reachable" || echo "$SERVER unreachable"
done

# While loop
COUNT=0
while [ $COUNT -lt 5 ]; do
    echo "Count: $COUNT"
    COUNT=$((COUNT + 1))
done

# Functions
check_service() {
    if systemctl is-active --quiet $1; then
        echo "$1 is running"
    else
        echo "$1 is STOPPED"
    fi
}
check_service sshd
check_service httpd

# Make script executable
chmod +x script.sh
./script.sh
bash script.sh

# Debug script
bash -x script.sh

# Install Wireshark
sudo dnf install wireshark -y
sudo chmod +x /usr/bin/dumpcap
sudo usermod -a -G wireshark $USER
# Log out and back in for group to take effect

# Start Wireshark GUI
wireshark &

# Useful Wireshark display filters:
# smtp                                  = SMTP traffic
# imap                                  = IMAP traffic
# ip.addr == 192.168.5.1                = traffic to/from IP
# ip.addr == 192.168.5.1 && tcp.port == 22  = SSH to specific host
# tcp.port == 443                       = HTTPS
# dns                                   = DNS queries and responses
# http                                  = HTTP
# arp                                   = ARP
# icmp                                  = ping

# tcpdump — CLI packet capture
sudo tcpdump -i ens160                   # capture on interface
sudo tcpdump -i ens160 -n                # no DNS resolution
sudo tcpdump -i ens160 port 25           # SMTP traffic only
sudo tcpdump -i ens160 host 192.168.5.2  # traffic to/from IP
sudo tcpdump -i ens160 -w /tmp/capture.pcap   # save to file
sudo tcpdump -r /tmp/capture.pcap        # read saved capture
sudo tcpdump -i any icmp                 # ICMP on all interfaces
sudo tcpdump -i ens160 -c 100           # capture first 100 packets

# System time
timedatectl                          # view date, time, timezone, NTP status
timedatectl set-timezone Europe/Amsterdam
timedatectl set-ntp true             # enable NTP sync
date                                 # current date/time
date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"              # formatted date

# Reboot and shutdown
sudo reboot
sudo shutdown -r now                 # reboot immediately
sudo shutdown -r +5                  # reboot in 5 minutes
sudo shutdown -h now                 # halt immediately
sudo poweroff

# Check open files by process
lsof -p 1234                        # open files by PID
lsof /var/log/messages              # which process has this file open
lsof -i :22                         # which process is using port 22
lsof -i :80
lsof -u jansen                      # open files by user

# Symbolic links
ln -s /data/topsecret.txt ~/secret.txt   # create soft link
ln /data/file.txt /backup/file.txt        # create hard link
ls -la ~/secret.txt                       # shows link target
readlink ~/secret.txt                     # show target path
unlink ~/secret.txt                       # remove link

# Archiving and compression
gzip file.txt                        # compress to file.txt.gz
gzip -d file.txt.gz                  # decompress
gunzip file.txt.gz                   # same as gzip -d
bzip2 file.txt                       # better compression
bzip2 -d file.txt.bz2
zip -r archive.zip /folder
unzip archive.zip

# Screen / tmux — keep sessions alive after disconnect
sudo dnf install screen tmux
screen                               # start screen
screen -S mysession                  # named session
Ctrl+A, D                           # detach from session
screen -r                            # reattach
screen -ls                           # list sessions

tmux                                 # start tmux
tmux new -s mysession
Ctrl+B, D                           # detach
tmux attach -t mysession             # reattach

content:
LAN (Local Area Network = Ethernet on L2):
- Interconnects end devices in a limited area
- Administered by a single organization
- Provides high-speed bandwidth to internal devices
- Example: mobile/laptop on WiFi

WAN (Wide Area Network = something else on L2):
- Interconnects LANs over wide geographical areas
- Typically administered by one or more service providers
- Typically provides slower speed links between LANs
- Example: mobile on 4G/5G

Other types (not for exam): PAN (Bluetooth), Wireless (WiFi), MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)

content:
All end devices and network devices require an OS.
Cisco uses IOS (Internetwork Operating System).

IOS stored in Flash (non-volatile, not lost on power loss, can store multiple IOS versions as .bin files).
IOS runs in RAM (faster, copied from flash on boot, lost when powered off).

startup-config stored in NVRAM (non-volatile, used on startup/reboot).
running-config stored in RAM (current config, lost when powered off).

content:
Straight cable: connect devices on DIFFERENT OSI layers (e.g. router L3 to switch L2, PC L7 to switch L2)
Cross cable: connect devices on the SAME OSI layer (e.g. switch-switch, router-router)
Exception: PC (L7) to router (L3) also uses a cross cable (unless auto-sensing is enabled)
Rollover/Console cable: the rollover is built into the flat blue cable, so use a straight patch cable on the patchpanel side

Auto-sensing can detect L2/L3 ports but is usually disabled.

Subnet mask: identifies the network portion of the IPv4 address
  1 = network portion
  0 = host portion

Prefix length (slash notation): count the number of 1-bits in the subnet mask
Example: 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000 = /24 = 255.255.255.0

content:
Reasons for subnetting:
- Reduces overall network traffic and improves performance
- Implements security policies between subnets
- Reduces number of devices affected by abnormal broadcast traffic
- Segments a network into smaller subnetworks

A router is required for devices on different subnets to communicate.
Each router interface must have a host address in the subnet it connects to.
Devices use the router interface on their LAN as their default gateway.

content:
Number of subnets  = 2^s   (where s = number of bits borrowed for subnet portion)
Number of hosts    = 2^h - 2 (where h = number of bits in host portion; subtract 2 for network and broadcast)

Example with /26 (N.N.N.sshhhhhh):
  2^2 = 4 subnets
  2^6 - 2 = 62 usable hosts per subnet
  Binary:  11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000
  Decimal: 255.255.255.192
  CIDR:    /26

content:
Given: 172.100.0.0/16, need 3 subnets (e.g. 2 LANs + 1 router-to-router link)
Borrow 2 bits: /16 + 2 = /18 => 255.255.192.0
Last borrowed bit decimal value = 64 (128+64=192)

Four resulting subnets:
  1) 172.100.0.0/18    hosts: 172.100.0.1   – 172.100.63.254   broadcast: 172.100.63.255
  2) 172.100.64.0/18   hosts: 172.100.64.1  – 172.100.127.254  broadcast: 172.100.127.255
  3) 172.100.128.0/18  hosts: 172.100.128.1 – 172.100.191.254  broadcast: 172.100.191.255
  4) 172.100.192.0/18  hosts: 172.100.192.1 – 172.100.255.254  broadcast: 172.100.255.255

Note: network address and broadcast address may never be assigned to a device.

content:
VLSM = Variable Length Subnet Mask (classless)
Allows subnets of different sizes within the same address space.
Contrast: FLSM (Fixed Length Subnet Mask) wastes addresses by making all subnets equal size.

VLSM assigns only as many addresses as each subnet needs, leaving remaining space for future use.

content:
1. Sort the required subnets from largest to smallest (e.g. 105, 50, 25, 22)
2. Write down the bit pattern for the current remaining address space (e.g. N.N.N.hhhhhhhh)
3. Find the appropriate subnet mask for the largest subnet:
   a. Determine host bits needed: find smallest 2^h - 2 >= required hosts
   b. Determine subnet bits: remaining bits after host bits
   c. Derive the subnet mask (e.g. /25, /26, /27)
4. Write down the subnets in order and assign the first available block to this subnet
5. Take the next unassigned subnet block and repeat from step 2 until all subnets are allocated

content:
Given: 192.168.10.0/24; subnets needed for 105, 50, 25, 22 hosts

Step 1 — Subnet for 105 hosts:
  2^7-2=126 >= 105 => 7 host bits => 1 subnet bit => /25
  Subnet A: 192.168.10.0/25   (hosts .1 – .126, broadcast .127)
  Subnet B: 192.168.10.128/25 (remaining block, take this next)

Step 2 — Subnet for 50 hosts from 192.168.10.128/25:
  2^6-2=62 >= 50 => 6 host bits => 1 subnet bit => /26
  Subnet C: 192.168.10.128/26 (hosts .129 – .190, broadcast .191)
  Subnet D: 192.168.10.192/26 (remaining block, take this next)

Step 3 — Subnets for 25 and 22 hosts from 192.168.10.192/26:
  2^5-2=30 >= 25 and 22 => 5 host bits => 1 subnet bit => /27
  Subnet E: 192.168.10.192/27 (hosts .193 – .222, broadcast .223)
  Subnet F: 192.168.10.224/27 (hosts .225 – .254, broadcast .255)

content:
Given: 128.186.1.0/24
Required subnets: Amsterdam LAN1=25, LAN2=25, Utrecht LAN1=10, LAN2=5
Sorted: 25, 25, 10, 5

Step 1 — Two /27 subnets for 25 hosts each:
  2^5-2=30 >= 25 => /27
  Amsterdam LAN1: 128.186.1.0/27   (hosts .1 – .30)
  Amsterdam LAN2: 128.186.1.32/27  (hosts .33 – .62)

Step 2 — /28 subnet for 10 hosts:
  2^4-2=14 >= 10 => /28
  Utrecht LAN1: 128.186.1.64/28    (hosts .65 – .78)

Step 3 — /29 subnet for 5 hosts:
  2^3-2=6 >= 5 => /29
  Utrecht LAN2: 128.186.1.80/29    (hosts .81 – .86)

content:
Switch operates at OSI Layer 2 (Data Link) — uses MAC addresses for forwarding decisions.
Router operates at OSI Layer 3 (Network) — uses IP addresses for routing decisions.

MAC address = physical address, assigned by manufacturer, must be unique in the network.
IP address = logical address, manually assigned, consists of IP + subnet mask.

Switch types in lab:
  L2 switch: managed switch, model 2950 or 2960 (use this for this assignment)
  L3 switch: multilayer switch, model 3560 or 3650 (can make decisions at both L2 and L3)

IP addresses are used for LAN-to-LAN communication via routers and WANs.

content:
Use IP range 10.1.0.0/16 as base.
Instead of calculating subnets, map VLAN ID to the third octet:
  Subnet = 10.1.<VLAN-ID>.0/24
  Example: VLAN 13 => subnet 10.1.13.0/24

Subnet mask /24 = 255.255.255.0 (3 x 8 bits set to 1)

Rules:
  - Host portion (last octet) must be unique per device
  - Do not use .0 (network address) or .255 (broadcast address)
  - Default gateway for PCs = IP address of the router interface in that subnet

Default VLAN = VLAN 1. All ports are assigned to VLAN 1 by default.
Devices in the same VLAN can communicate with each other.
Devices in different VLANs cannot communicate without inter-VLAN routing.
Inter-VLAN routing is configured later.

Access port: only 1 VLAN allowed.
Trunk port: multiple VLANs allowed.

switchport mode trunk = sets trunking unconditionally (safer than dynamic desirable).

content:
1. Confirm both PCs are connected to port 1 (VLAN 1)
2. Assign IPs in same subnet, e.g. 10.1.1.10 and 10.1.1.11, mask 255.255.255.0
3. Ping between PCs: ping 10.1.1.10 or ping 10.1.1.11
4. If no reply: check Windows Firewall or cable connections
5. Move one PC cable to a VLAN 10 port, update IP to 10.1.10.x
6. Attempt ping — should FAIL (different VLANs)
7. Move second PC to same VLAN 10 port, update IP to same subnet
8. Attempt ping — should SUCCEED
9. Run continuous ping: ping 10.1.10.10 -t
10. Remove one cross cable in server room — ping may miss a few packets but should recover (STP failover)

  OUI (Organizational Unique Identifier): identifies the hardware manufacturer (first 3 bytes)
  Unique part: makes the device unique within that manufacturer (last 3 bytes)

Example lookup tool: http://www.macvendorlookup.com/

The switch MAC address table maps MAC addresses to specific switch ports.
Multiple MAC addresses on one port = another switch or hub connected to that port.

content:
ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps IP addresses to MAC addresses.

ARP Request: broadcast frame asking 'who has <destination IP>? tell <sender IP>'
  - Destination MAC = 00:00:00:00:00:00 (unknown at time of request)
  - Sent as broadcast to all devices on the LAN

ARP Reply: unicast response '<IP> is at <MAC>'
  - Sender MAC is now filled in with the actual MAC address
  - The result is stored in the ARP cache of the requesting device

Ethernet frame type for ARP = 0x0806

1. Clear MAC table on switch: clear mac-address-table
2. Clear ARP cache on PCs: arp -d *
3. Verify ARP cache is empty: arp -a
4. Start Wireshark, select correct interface, do NOT start capture yet
5. Start Wireshark capture
6. Ping the default gateway from both PCs
7. Stop the capture
8. Apply filter: type 'arp' in filter box and click Apply
9. Find ARP request frame: 'who has <gateway IP> tell <PC IP>'
10. Find ARP reply frame: '<gateway IP> is at <MAC>' (indicated as ARP reply)

content:
When applications communicate using TCP/IP over Ethernet, data is encapsulated as an Ethernet II frame.

Frame fields:
  Destination MAC: target device MAC address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF = broadcast)
  Source MAC: sending device MAC address
  Type/EtherType: indicates upper layer protocol (e.g. 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x0806 = ARP)
  Data field: payload (variable length)
  FCS (Frame Check Sequence): error checking value at end of frame

Broadcast destination address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF = sent to all devices on the LAN segment.

subsection: Configure SSH on Router or Switch
content:
1. Set hostname (already done in base config)
2. Set domain name: ip domain-name <dns.naam>
3. Create local user: username <name> privilege 15 secret <password>
4. Generate RSA keys: crypto key generate rsa
   (512 bits = SSHv1, 1024 bits = SSHv2)
5. Configure VTY lines to accept SSH:
   line vty 0 15
   transport input ssh
   login local
6. Note: some devices require 'aaa new-model' for SSH to work
7. Test: connect via PuTTY using SSH to the switch or router IP

content:
Network consists of: 2 routers (A and B), 2 switches (C and D), 2 PCs.
Router A and Router B are serially connected on interface S../../0 on both routers.
Router A connects to switch via Ethernet; Router B connects to switch via Ethernet.
Router B also has Loopback interfaces (Lo1, Lo2).

Serial interface mapping:
  Router A <-> Router B: both use S../../0
  Router A <-> Router C: Router A uses S../../1, Router C uses S../../0
  Router D has no serial interface

IMPORTANT: Only use Router A and Router B for this assignment.
For switches: use Switch C and Switch D.
NEVER use the setup wizard — always choose NO.

content:
Given IP range: 172.16.32.0/20 (decimal: 255.255.240.0)
Requirements:
  - Minimum 5 subnets
  - Each subnet must support at least 50 usable host addresses

Approach:
  2^6 - 2 = 62 usable hosts => 6 host bits needed => /26 mask
  172.16.32.0/20 has 12 host bits available
  Borrowing 6 bits for subnets: 2^6 = 64 possible subnets
  Each subnet has 2^6 - 2 = 62 usable host addresses
  Subnet increment = 64 in the last relevant octet

Example subnets from 172.16.32.0/26:
  172.16.32.0/26    hosts: .1 – .62     broadcast: .63
  172.16.32.64/26   hosts: .65 – .126   broadcast: .127
  172.16.32.128/26  hosts: .129 – .190  broadcast: .191
  172.16.32.192/26  hosts: .193 – .254  broadcast: .255
  172.16.33.0/26    hosts: .1 – .62     broadcast: .63

content:
Given IP range: 2001:ABCD:1234::/48
Requirements:
  - Minimum 5 subnets
  - Use /64 prefix length for all subnets

With /48 prefix and /64 subnets: 16 bits available for subnetting => 2^16 = 65536 possible subnets.

Two methods to choose subnets:
  Method 1: mirror IPv4 subnet numbering in the 4th group
    e.g. 2001:ABCD:1234:0001::/64
         2001:ABCD:1234:0002::/64
  Method 2: choose 5 random hex values between 0000 and FFFF
    e.g. 2001:ABCD:1234:CAFE::/64

IPv6 address notation: :: means all zeros between segments
  Example: 2001:ABCD:1234:BAD::1/64 = 2001:ABCD:1234:BAD:0000:0000:0000:0001/64

content:
Before testing ping, verify routing tables on both routers:
  show ip route   — all subnets should appear (C = connected, R = learned)
  show ipv6 route — same for IPv6

Expected ping results when configured correctly:
  PC1 <-> PC2: yes (same or routed subnet)
  PC -> router interfaces: yes
  PC -> loopback interfaces: yes (via RIP)
  PC -> remote switch SVI: may fail until default-gateway is set on switch

If ping fails:
  - Check Windows Firewall (disable or add ICMP allow rule)
  - Verify IP addresses and default gateways
  - Verify RIP is advertising correct networks

SSH requirements:

  2. Domain name must be set (ip domain-name)
  3. RSA keys must be generated (crypto key generate rsa)
  4. Local username and password must exist
  5. VTY lines must use 'login local' and 'transport input ssh'

2048-bit key = SSHv2 (recommended).
SSH cannot be tested until IP addresses are configured on routers and PCs.

content:
Normal router interface config assigns one IP to the physical port = only one VLAN.
Inter-VLAN routing (router-on-a-stick) uses subinterfaces on one physical port to route between multiple VLANs.

Process:
  1. Enable the physical interface (no shutdown) — do NOT assign an IP to it
  2. Create a subinterface per VLAN (e.g. gi0/0/0.11 for VLAN 11)
  3. Set encapsulation to dot1Q with the VLAN ID
  4. Assign an IP address to the subinterface

The trunk port on the switch carries tagged VLAN traffic to the router.
The router uses the subinterface to route between VLANs.

Step-by-step troubleshooting process:
1. READ the assignment carefully
2. Fill in the network diagram accurately
3. Identify what DOES work — narrow down where the problem is
4. Form a hypothesis about the cause
5. Change ONE setting at a time — if it does not fix the problem, revert it
6. Keep notes of every change made
7. Repeat until resolved

IMPORTANT: Never change multiple settings at once. Always revert unsuccessful changes before trying the next step.

content:
1. Run ipconfig /all on PC — check IP, mask, default gateway for obvious errors
   (e.g. IP ending in .0 = network address, not valid for a host)
2. Ping own default gateway — if fails: problem is between PC and router
3. Ping second interface of own router — if fails: problem is on the router
4. Ping first interface of next router — if fails: problem is between routers
5. Use tracert/traceroute to identify exactly where the path stops
6. Ping destination PC — if tracert stops at last router: problem is between that router and PC
7. On suspect router: show ip int brief (interfaces up?), show ip route (routes present?)
8. Verify IP of router interface matches default gateway set on PC
9. If needed: temporarily shut interface to confirm physical connection mapping

Diagnosis steps taken:
1. ipconfig /all on PC0 — IP and gateway appear normal
2. Ping own gateway from PC0 — SUCCESS => PC0-to-router link is OK
3. Ping second router interface from PC0 — SUCCESS => Router0 is not the problem
4. tracert to PC1 (172.13.128.11) — path stops at 172.13.128.2 => problem is near Router1/PC1
5. ipconfig /all on PC1 — default gateway shows 172.13.128.200
6. show ip interface brief on Router1 — interface toward PC1 has IP 172.13.128.2
7. Mismatch found: PC1 default gateway (172.13.128.200) does not match router interface IP (172.13.128.2)

Fix: Change default gateway on PC1 from 172.13.128.200 to 172.13.128.2
Result: Ping from PC1 to gateway and PC0 succeeds.

content:
Both UDP and TCP operate at OSI Layer 4 (Transport Layer).

UDP (User Datagram Protocol):
  - Connectionless — no handshake before sending
  - No guaranteed delivery, no ordering, no error recovery
  - Very low overhead — fast
  - Used by: DNS, DHCP, TFTP, RTP (audio/video streaming), SNMP
  - Header fields: Source Port, Destination Port, Length, Checksum (4 fields only)

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):
  - Connection-oriented — requires 3-way handshake before data transfer
  - Guaranteed delivery, ordered packets, retransmission on error
  - Flow control via Window Size
  - Used by: HTTP, HTTPS, SMTP, POP3, FTP, SSH, Telnet

Other Layer 4 protocols (not covered in this lab):
  RTP  — real-time audio/video
  QUIC — modern fast protocol replacing TCP in many web contexts

content:
TCP Three-Way Handshake establishes a connection before data is sent:

Frame 1 — SYN (client -> server):
  Source port: random high port (client)
  Destination port: 80 (HTTP)
  Flags: SYN = 1
  Meaning: client requests connection, sends initial sequence number

Frame 2 — SYN-ACK (server -> client):
  Flags: SYN = 1, ACK = 1
  Meaning: server acknowledges client SYN, sends its own sequence number

Frame 3 — ACK (client -> server):
  Flags: ACK = 1
  Meaning: client acknowledges server SYN, connection established

Wireshark filter to find SYN frames: tcp.flags.syn == 1
After finding it: clear the filter to see the full handshake sequence.

content:
Sequence number: identifies the byte position of the first data byte in this segment
Acknowledgment number: tells the sender which byte the receiver expects NEXT

Example flow:
  Frame 1: SEQ=0, ACK=0 (SYN)
  Frame 2: SEQ=0, ACK=1 (SYN-ACK — acknowledges byte 0, expects byte 1)
  Frame 3: SEQ=1, ACK=1 (ACK)

Window Size: how many bytes the receiver can accept before requiring an ACK
  Large window = faster throughput
  Reduced window = flow control, slows sender down

FTP uses TCP port 21 for control and port 20 for data transfer.
FTP sends credentials in PLAINTEXT — visible in Wireshark capture.
This is why SSH/SFTP is preferred over FTP.

content:
SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol):
  Port: 25 (TCP)
  Used for: SENDING email (client to server, server to server)
  Authentication: set to 'no authentication' for this lab

POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3):
  Port: 110 (TCP)
  Used for: RECEIVING email (download from server to client)

IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol):
  Port: 143 (TCP)
  Used for: reading email on server (not downloaded, stays on server)

Thunderbird configuration for this lab:
  SMTP server: eagle-server.example.com
  POP3 server: eagle-server.example.com
  Password: cisco
  DNS server: IP of Eagle server

Test SMTP manually with Telnet:
  telnet eagle-server.example.com 25
  EHLO yourdomain.com   -> server responds with supported extensions

# Windows — nslookup
nslookup                          # enter interactive mode
nslookup example.com              # query A record
nslookup eagle-server.example.com # forward lookup

# Linux — dig

dig -x 192.168.254.253            # reverse lookup

# Both tools show:
#   Server name and IP of the DNS server used

# CDP commands
show cdp neighbors              # summary: device ID, local interface, capability, platform, remote port
show cdp neighbors detail       # adds: IP address, IOS version, duplex, native VLAN
show cdp                        # CDP timer and holdtime settings
show cdp interface              # per-interface CDP status
no cdp run                      # disable CDP globally
no cdp enable                   # disable CDP on specific interface

# LLDP commands (vendor-neutral alternative to CDP)
lldp run                        # enable LLDP globally (in config mode)
no lldp run                     # disable LLDP globally

# Per interface:
interface gi0/0
 lldp transmit
 lldp receive

show lldp neighbors             # summary view
show lldp neighbors detail      # detailed view including IP, capabilities

# 'B' under capability in LLDP output = Bridge (switch)

# View IOS version and hardware info
show version

content:
CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol):
  - Cisco proprietary
  - Works only between Cisco devices
  - Enabled by default
  - Layer 2 — does not traverse routers

LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol):
  - IEEE standard (802.1AB) — vendor neutral
  - Works between any vendor's devices
  - Must be manually enabled on Cisco devices
  - Also Layer 2 — does not traverse routers
  - Used in OpenFlow/SDN environments

Both protocols are useful for:
  - Network topology discovery
  - Troubleshooting cable connections
  - Identifying neighbor device models and interfaces

! Enter OSPF process
enable
conf t
router ospf 1

! View directly connected routes to advertise
do show ip route

! Advertise only routes marked with C (connected)
network <ip_range> <wildcard_mask> area 0

! Example:
network 10.1.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
network 10.1.11.0 0.0.0.255 area 0
network 192.168.1.0 0.0.0.3 area 0

! On Dorpsplein router only: redistribute static routes
redistribute static

content:
This assignment configures three routers (A=Rotterdam, B=Amsterdam, C=Utrecht) connected serially, with two optional switches and two PCs. Topics covered: OSPF single-area (area 0), standard and extended ACLs, and OSPFv3 or RIPng for IPv6.

Connect and configure routers one at a time: start with A and B, verify serial interface comes UP on both, then add C. Switches and PCs are connected later.

content:
After configuring OSPF and enabling debug on Amsterdam, answer the following:
1. What information is received on the router during OSPF neighbor formation?
2. Which router is the 'slave' (DR/BDR election)?
3. What is the MTU for data transfer?
4. After full database replication (ADJCHG ... from Loading to FULL), what type of frames are still periodically received?
5. How many networks are in the routing table on Amsterdam?
6. What does the value 110 mean in the routing table? (Administrative Distance)
7. What does the second number mean in e.g. [110/65] or [110/128]? (OSPF metric/cost)
8. Can all systems in the network ping each other via IPv4?
9. What appears on Amsterdam/Utrecht regarding the static route redistributed from Rotterdam?

! Standard ACL — blocks all traffic from a specific range
! Fill in the IP range and wildcard mask on the dotted lines
access-list 30 deny <network> <wildcard>
access-list 30 permit any

! Apply to an interface — choose IN or OUT (lowercase)
interface <interface>
 ip access-group 30 in
! OR:
 ip access-group 30 out

! Note: standard ACLs should be placed as close to the DESTINATION as possible
! Verify
show access-list 30

! Extended ACL — blocks HTTP and Telnet from a specific source network
! Fill in source network and wildcard mask
access-list 110 deny tcp <network> <wildcard> any eq www
access-list 110 deny tcp <network> <wildcard> any eq telnet
access-list 110 permit ip any any

! Apply to interface — determine correct direction (in/out) yourself
interface <interface>
 ip access-group 110 in
! OR:
 ip access-group 110 out

! Note: extended ACLs should be placed as close to the SOURCE as possible
! Verify
show access-list 110

! PAT rule — replace gi0/0/1 with your actual WAN interface name
! The '1' refers to ACL 1
ip nat inside source list 1 interface gi0/0/1 overload

! Create ACL 1 to define which source IPs are allowed to NAT

! Room 42:
access-list 1 permit 10.42.0.0 0.0.255.255

! Mark internal interface
interface gi0/0/0
 ip nat inside

! Mark external interface
interface gi0/0/1
 ip nat outside

! Verify NAT translations (after pinging 8.8.8.8 from PC)
do show ip nat translations

subsection: Standard ACL to Block Internal Traffic
content:
! Let ping -t to 8.8.8.8 run on the PC first, then apply ACL

! Create ACL 10 to deny 10.x.x.x range
access-list 10 deny 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255

! Apply outbound on WAN interface
interface gi0/0/1
 ip access-group 10 out

! Verify ping from PC to 8.8.8.8 is now blocked

! View ACLs and match counters
show ip access-list
show run | sec access

! Remove the deny rule to restore internet access
no access-list 10 deny 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255

content:
1. Open disk with fdisk: sudo fdisk /dev/sdX
2. Press m to see available commands
3. Press n to create a new partition
4. Choose p for primary partition
5. Choose partition number (1-4)
6. Set first and last sector (or size, e.g. +512M)
7. Repeat for additional partitions
8. Press t to change partition type
   - For RAID: set type to fd (Linux RAID autodetect)
9. Press w to write changes to disk

NOTE: Max 4 primary partitions on MBR/DOS disklabel.
To exceed 4: delete 4th primary and replace with extended partition,
then create logical partitions inside it.

ext2: no journaling — faster but no crash recovery log
ext3: adds a journal — slightly more disk space used but recovers cleanly after crash

Practical effect: ext3 will show slightly less free space than ext2 on the same size partition
because the journal uses reserved space.

To automount at boot, add entries to /etc/fstab.
Format: <device> <mountpoint> <fstype> <options> <dump> <pass>

Examples:
  /dev/sdX1                    /data/part1     ext2  defaults  0  0
  /dev/sdX2                    /data/part2     ext3  defaults  0  0
  /dev/vg_data/lv_webcontent   /data/webcontent xfs  defaults  0  0
  /dev/vg_raid/lvwebcontent    /data/web2      ext4  defaults  0  0

Apply all fstab mounts immediately (without rebooting):
  sudo mount -a

Verify mounts after reboot:
  mount | grep data
