content:
LVM hierarchy:
  Physical Volume (PV)  — a disk or partition initialized for LVM use
  Volume Group (VG)     — pool of one or more PVs
  Logical Volume (LV)   — flexible partition carved from a VG, given a filesystem

Benefits: resize volumes online, span multiple disks, snapshots.

Key commands:
  pvcreate   — initialize disk/partition as PV
  vgcreate   — create VG from one or more PVs
  lvcreate   — create LV inside a VG
  pvdisplay / vgdisplay / lvdisplay — view details
  lvextend   — grow a logical volume

# Step 2: Create volume group

# Step 3: View volume group properties

# Step 4: Create logical volumes
sudo lvcreate -L 64M  -n lv_webcontent vg_data
sudo lvcreate -L 192M -n lv_database   vg_data

# Step 5: Create filesystems on logical volumes
sudo mkfs.xfs /dev/vg_data/lv_webcontent
sudo mkfs.xfs /dev/vg_data/lv_database

# Step 6: Create mount points and mount
sudo mkdir -p /data/webcontent /data/db
sudo mount /dev/vg_data/lv_webcontent /data/webcontent
sudo mount /dev/vg_data/lv_database   /data/db

# View logical volumes
sudo lvdisplay

1. Add 2-3 new virtual disks in VMware (same type as existing: NVMe or SATA, 20GB each)
2. Boot the VM
3. Identify new disks: lsblk or sudo fdisk -l
4. For each new disk, create a RAID partition with fdisk:
   a. sudo fdisk /dev/sdX
   b. n  -> create new primary partition (full disk)
   c. t  -> change type to fd (Linux RAID autodetect, hex fd)
   d. w  -> write changes
5. Verify partitions: lsblk

NOTE: If using NVMe disks, new disks will be nvme0n3, nvme0n4 etc.
If using SATA, they will be sdb, sdc etc.

# Create mdadm config directory and file
sudo mkdir /etc/mdadm
sudo touch /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf

# Add DEVICE line (replace partition names with your own)
echo DEVICE /dev/nvme0n3p1 /dev/nvme0n4p1 | sudo tee /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf
# OR for SATA:
echo DEVICE /dev/sda1 /dev/sdb1 | sudo tee /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf

# Append ARRAY line with UUID (use -a to append, not overwrite)
sudo mdadm --detail --scan | sudo tee -a /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf

# Verify config file has two lines
cat /etc/mdadm/mdadm.conf

# Reboot and verify RAID starts automatically
sudo reboot
cat /proc/mdstat

# Initialize RAID array as LVM physical volume
sudo pvcreate /dev/md0

# Create volume group on RAID
sudo vgcreate VG_RAID /dev/md0

# View volume group
sudo vgdisplay

# Create logical volumes
sudo lvcreate -L 2G -n lvwebcontent VG_RAID
sudo lvcreate -L 3G -n lvdatabase   VG_RAID

# Create filesystems (avoid XFS on LVM-over-RAID in this setup)
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/VG_RAID/lvwebcontent
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/VG_RAID/lvdatabase

# Create mount points
sudo mkdir -p /data/{web2,db2}

# Add to /etc/fstab
# /dev/vg_raid/lvwebcontent  /data/web2  ext4  defaults  0  0
# /dev/vg_raid/lvdatabase    /data/db2   ext4  defaults  0  0

# Mount all
sudo mount -a

# Copy data
sudo cp -r /usr/share/doc/* /data/web2
sudo cp -r /var/cache/dnf   /data/db2

subsection: Simulate and Recover from Disk Failure
content:
1. Open a monitoring terminal: watch cat /proc/mdstat
2. Simulate disk failure (mark disk as faulty):
   sudo mdadm /dev/md0 -f /dev/nvme0n3p1
3. Observe status change in monitoring terminal and in logs:
   sudo cat /var/log/messages | grep 'md[0-9]'
4. Verify data is still accessible (RAID1 uses remaining disk):
   ls /data/db2
   cat /data/db2/test3.txt
5. Remove the failed disk from RAID set:
   sudo mdadm /dev/md0 -r /dev/nvme0n3p1
6. Re-add the disk (simulating replacement with new disk):
   sudo mdadm /dev/md0 -a /dev/nvme0n3p1
7. Monitor resync in /proc/mdstat — array rebuilds automatically
8. Check logs for rebuild confirmation:
   sudo grep 'md[0-9]' /var/log/messages

content:
AGDLP = Accounts -> Global groups -> Domain Local groups -> Permissions

Process:
1. Add user Accounts to Global groups (e.g. all doctors in 'Huisartsen_Global')
2. Add Global groups to Domain Local groups (e.g. 'Huisartsen_DL')
3. Assign permissions (NTFS + Share) to Domain Local groups

Benefit: flexible permission management — change access by changing group membership,
not by editing individual permissions on every share.

Effective permissions = most restrictive of NTFS permissions AND Share permissions.

content:
SUID (Set User ID) bit on executable:
  File runs with permissions of the FILE OWNER, not the user running it
  Example: /usr/bin/passwd is owned by root with SUID — any user can change their password
  Set with: chmod u+s <file> or chmod 4755 <file>
  Shown as 's' in place of 'x' for owner: -rwsr-xr-x

SGID (Set Group ID) bit on directory:
  New files in directory inherit the GROUP of the directory, not the creator's primary group
  Useful for shared project directories
  Set with: chmod g+s <dir>
  Shown as 's' in place of 'x' for group: drwxrwsr-x

Sticky bit on directory:
  Files can only be deleted or renamed by their OWNER (even if others have write permission)
  Classic use: /tmp
  Set with: chmod +t <dir> or chmod 1777 <dir>
  Shown as 't' in place of 'x' for others: drwxrwxrwt

sudo chown root /tmp/tail

# Create a symbolic link (soft link)
ln -s /data/topsecret.txt ~/secretpassage.txt

# Symbolic link points to the original file
# Editing the link edits the original file
# Permissions on the link itself are irrelevant — what matters is the target file's permissions

# Restrict file to root only
sudo chmod 600 /data/topsecret.txt   # only root can read/write

# Verify
ls -la /data

# Hard link vs soft link:
# Soft link: separate file pointing to path — breaks if target is moved/deleted
# Hard link: additional directory entry pointing to same inode — survives target rename

content:
SSH (Secure Shell) — first version 1995 — replaces Telnet, RSH, rlogin.
All traffic including credentials is ENCRYPTED.
Default port: 22
Config file (server): /etc/ssh/sshd_config
Config file (client): /etc/ssh/ssh_config

Key files on server:
  /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key      private key
  /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key.pub  public key

Key files per user:
  ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa          private key (keep secret!)
  ~/.ssh/id_ecdsa.pub      public key
  ~/.ssh/authorized_keys   public keys allowed to log in
  ~/.ssh/known_hosts       fingerprints of hosts connected to

SSH also used by: SCP, rsync, SFTP

# Disable password auth after setting up key auth
PasswordAuthentication no

# Restart to apply

content:
1. Log in as a normal user (not root)
2. Generate ECDSA keypair:
   ssh-keygen -t ecdsa
   Accept defaults (press Enter), choose NO passphrase for scripting use
3. Copy public key to the server (even if same machine):
   ssh-copy-id <username>@localhost
   (Or: ssh-copy-id <username>@<remote-host>)
4. Log out and log back in:
   ssh <username>@localhost
   Should NOT prompt for password
5. After confirming key auth works, disable password auth:
   Edit /etc/ssh/sshd_config -> PasswordAuthentication no
   sudo systemctl restart sshd
6. Verify key file permissions:
   ls -al ~/.ssh
   id_ecdsa should be 600 (owner read/write only)
   id_ecdsa.pub can be 644

# Check current mode
getenforce
# Returns: Enforcing, Permissive, or Disabled

# View full status and policy
sestatus

# Change mode temporarily (survives until reboot)
sudo setenforce 0    # set Permissive
sudo setenforce 1    # set Enforcing

# Change default mode permanently (requires reboot)
sudo nano /etc/selinux/config

content:
# Create user with confined SELinux label 'staff_u'
sudo useradd -Z staff_u example.user
sudo passwd example.user

# Reboot so SELinux label takes effect
sudo reboot

# After reboot, log in as example.user and verify context
id -Z
# Expected output includes: staff_u:staff_r:staff_t

# Difference between confined and unconfined users:
# unconfined_u: default for regular users — very few SELinux restrictions
# staff_u: confined — restricted to allowed roles and types

content:
When moving Apache webroot outside /var/www, SELinux will block access.
SELinux uses 'security contexts' (labels) on files. Apache expects label: httpd_sys_content_t

Symptom: browser shows 403 Forbidden even if file permissions are correct.
Diagnose: sudo grep http /var/log/audit/audit.log | audit2why

Fix steps:
1. Create new webroot directory
2. Update /etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf — replace all /var/www with new path
3. Set file permissions (755)
4. Apply SELinux context temporarily: sudo chcon -R -t httpd_sys_content_t /plf/www
5. Apply SELinux context permanently (survives restorecon):
   sudo semanage fcontext -a -t httpd_sys_content_t "/plf/www(/.*)?"
   sudo restorecon -R -v /plf/www

NOTE: chcon is temporary — use semanage + restorecon for permanent fix.

subsection: Add CNAME Record for Web Alias
content:
A CNAME record creates an alias pointing to an existing hostname.
Example: www.bmc.example -> rocky1.bmc.example

Add to zone file:
  www    IN    CNAME    rocky1.

After editing zone file:
  1. Increment the serial number in the SOA record
  2. Reload DNS: sudo systemctl reload named

Test from client:
  nslookup www.bmc.example
  nslookup bmc.example

subsection: Secure Default Web Site in IIS
content:
Security requirements for Default Web Site:

a) IP Restrictions:
   - Allow only 192.168.2.0/24
   - Deny 192.168.2.2 specifically
   - Default access rule = Deny

b) Basic Authentication:
   - Enable Basic Authentication
   - Set a realm name (e.g. 'Basic authentication')
   - NOTE: Basic auth sends credentials as Base64 (not encrypted) — insecure without HTTPS

c) Windows Authentication:
   - Disable Basic Auth (insecure)
   - Enable Windows Authentication
   - Windows Auth uses Kerberos/NTLM — only works from Windows domain-joined clients
   - Linux clients cannot authenticate with Windows Auth natively

d) Logging:
   - Enable all available logging fields in IIS logging settings

subsection: Create and Share a Folder on Windows (GUI)
content:
1. Create folder: C:\data\share1 on the DC
2. Set NTFS permissions:
   - Users: Read & Execute
   - Administrators: Full Control
3. Share the folder with Share permissions: Read
4. Access from client via UNC path: \\DC1\share1

NOTE: Effective permissions = most restrictive of NTFS + Share permissions.
Administrators have Full Control on NTFS but only Read on share => can only read.

For Core server:
  Create: C:\data\share1
  Copy a logfile from C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\W3SVC1 to that folder

Share via PowerShell:
  New-SmbShare -Name share1 -Path C:\data\share1
  Get-SmbShare   # view all shares

subsection: Create Samba User, Group and Directory
content:
# Create Linux user and group
sudo useradd hu
sudo groupadd smbgrp
sudo usermod -a -G smbgrp hu

# Set Samba password for user (separate from Linux password)
sudo smbpasswd -a hu

# Create and configure share directory
sudo mkdir /home/secure
sudo chown -R hu:smbgrp /home/secure/
sudo chmod -R 0770 /home/secure/

# Set SELinux context for Samba
sudo chcon -t samba_share_t /home/secure/

sudo firewall-cmd --runtime-to-permanent

# Restart Samba services
sudo systemctl restart smb nmb

# Verify shares are running
sudo systemctl status smb
sudo systemctl status nmb

content:
To map the Linux Samba share from Windows (BMC-PC1):
1. Open Windows Explorer
2. Right-click 'This PC' -> 'Map network drive'
3. Enter share path: \\rocky1\secure
4. Deselect 'Reconnect at sign-in'
5. Select 'Connect using different credentials'
6. Enter credentials: username = hu, password = (samba password set with smbpasswd)

If domain membership causes issues, try from a non-domain Windows client
or from another Linux system using:
  smbclient //rocky1/secure -U hu

content:
1. Clone a Windows Server GUI base disk.
2. Assign 2–4 GB RAM depending on available hardware.
3. Add four NICs before starting the VM:
   - NIC 1: LAN Segment Rotterdam
   - NIC 2: LAN Segment DMZ1
   - NIC 3: LAN Segment Leased
   - NIC 4: NAT (preferred) or Bridged for internet access.
4. Enable Time Sync in VMware: VM Settings → Options → VMware Tools → Synchronize guest with host.
5. Start the VM, log in as local administrator.
6. Run RRAS1.ps1 with administrator rights.
7. After reboot, verify settings.

1. Open Server Manager → Tools → Routing and Remote Access.
2. Right-click RRAS1 → Configure and Enable Routing and Remote Access.
3. Choose Custom Configuration → select NAT and LAN Routing → start the service.
4. Under IPv4 → General → right-click → New Routing Protocol → select RIP.
5. Under RIP → right-click → add the three internal interfaces (LAN Rotterdam, DMZ1, Leased). Do NOT add the internet interface.
6. Wait for Response Sent/Received counters to increment on the Leased interface.

content:
1. Open Routing and Remote Access → IPv4 → NAT.
2. Right-click NAT → New Interfaces.
3. Add LAN Rotterdam as a Private interface (default selection, click OK).
4. Add Internet interface as Public; check 'Enable NAT on this interface'.
5. Verify on DC1 that ping to 8.8.8.8 succeeds.
6. On DC1: open DNS Manager → DC1 → Forwarders tab → add 8.8.8.8 (and/or 9.9.9.9).
7. Verify green checkmarks appear in the Forwarders tab.
8. On RRAS1: check that Mappings and Inbound Packets Translated counters increment.

content:
AD replication is essential for domain functioning.

Site: a location (usually based on a subnet) with fast internal connections.
  - DCs within same site replicate frequently and fast (every 15 sec for FFL 2008+, otherwise 5 min)
  - DCs between sites replicate less frequently (configurable schedule)

Site Link: represents a (slower) WAN connection between sites.
  - Has a Cost value — lower cost = preferred path (like OSPF cost)
  - Has a Replication Interval — default is 180 minutes (3 hours)
  - One site link exists by default: DEFAULTIPSITELINK
  - Create one site link per WAN connection
  - Site links are bridged by default — any DC can communicate via any site link

Bridgehead server: the DC that organizes replication between sites.
  - Automatically selected; manual override possible but not recommended.

KCC (Knowledge Consistency Checker):
  - Runs on every DC every 15 minutes
  - Generates the most efficient replication topology
  - Intrasite KCC for within a site
  - ISTG (Intersite Topology Generator) for between sites — only 1 DC per site has this role
  - Supports up to 5000 sites

content:
Open: Server Manager -> Tools -> Active Directory Sites and Services

Default folders under Sites:
  1. Subnets — maps IP subnets to sites
  2. Inter-Site Transports — IP and SMTP site link protocols
  3. Default-First-Site-Name — the default site containing all DCs initially

Inter-Site Transport protocols:
  IP (RPC): faster and more efficient, can replicate everything
  SMTP: used when WAN links are unreliable or always-on not guaranteed;
         requires a CA for encryption; cannot replicate all AD data

Default IP site link cost: 100
Default replication interval: 180 minutes

To rename a site:
  Right-click site -> Rename

To create a new site:
  Right-click Sites -> New Site -> choose site link

To create subnets and link to sites:
  Right-click Subnets -> New Subnet -> enter CIDR -> select site

To move a DC to another site:
  Right-click the server object -> Move

content:
1. On SRV1: Server Manager -> click the notification flag (!) -> promote to DC
2. Choose: Add a domain controller to an existing domain
3. Log in with domain administrator credentials
4. On Domain Controller Options tab:
   - DNS Server role: leave checked (recommended for fault tolerance)
   - Global Catalog: leave checked
   - RODC: do NOT select (RODC = Read-Only DC, cannot accept write changes)
   - Site Name: select 'Intern' (not Default-First-Site-Name)
5. Set DSRM password
6. Additional Options: choose replication from dc1.school.test specifically
7. Leave AD DB paths as default
8. Review PowerShell script and proceed
9. After reboot: verify SRV1 appears in ADSS on DC1 under Intern site

To force replication:
  repadmin /syncall
  or right-click connection object in ADSS -> Replicate Now

# DCdiag — checks overall DC health
dcdiag /h                          # show all options
dcdiag /test:connectivity          # test DC connectivity
dcdiag /test:kccevent              # check KCC events
dcdiag /test:topology              # verify replication topology
dcdiag /a                          # run all tests on local DC
dcdiag /e                          # run all tests on all DCs in enterprise

# Repadmin — specific to replication analysis
repadmin /kcc                      # trigger KCC to recalculate topology
repadmin /queue                    # show objects waiting to be replicated
repadmin /replsummary              # success/error counts and avg replication time
repadmin /showrepl                 # detailed replication status
repadmin /syncall                  # force replication with all partners

# NOTE: if replication is broken, Exchange installs may fail and new users cannot be created

content:
Domain: Security and Administration Boundary
  - Has its own SAM database, AD (NTDS.dit), Group Policies, Password Policies
  - Multi-master replication — changes can be made from any DC

Trust types:
  Transitive trust: automatic two-way trust between domains in same forest
    If A trusts B and C trusts B, then A trusts C
  Explicit trust: manually created, one-way or two-way
  Shortcut trust: explicit trust between two domains within a tree (for faster auth)
  Cross-forest transitive trust: between two separate AD forests

RODC (Read-Only Domain Controller):
  - Holds read-only copy of AD database
  - Used at remote/branch offices — cannot process write requests
  - Safer for locations with lower physical security

Global Catalog (GC):
  - Holds full copy of objects in home domain + partial copy of all other domain objects
  - Required for forest-wide searches and universal group membership
  - Recommended: give every DC the GC role
  - Universal Group Membership Caching: alternative to GC at small remote sites

FSMO Roles (Flexible Single Master Operation):
  Forest-wide: Schema Master, Domain Naming Master
  Domain-wide: PDC Emulator, RID Master, Infrastructure Master

content:
Create two DHCP scopes on SRV1:

Scope 1 — Datacenter1 (DMZ1):
  Name: LAN-DMZ
  IP range: 172.16.2.10 – 172.16.2.100
  Subnet mask: /24 (255.255.255.0) — NOTE: default is /16, change it!
  Default gateway: 172.16.2.251 (RRAS1)
  DNS servers: DC1 and SRV1
  Lease duration: default

Scope 2 — LAN Rotterdam (Intern):
  IP range: 172.16.1.10 – 172.16.1.100
  Subnet mask: /24
  Default gateway: 172.16.1.251 (RRAS1)
  DNS servers: DC1 and SRV1

Activate both scopes after creation.

content:
1. Right-click scope -> Configure Failover
2. Choose RRAS1 as partner server
   If server not visible: check that IPv6 is not set to ::1 on DNS — disable IPv6 if needed
3. For LAN Rotterdam scope:
   Mode: Hot standby
   Role of Partner Server: Active (not Standby)
   Uncheck 'Enable message authentication'
4. For Datacenter1 scope:
   Same steps, but assign Active role to SRV1
   Uncheck 'Reuse existing failover relationships'
5. Test failover:
   - Start PC1, set NIC to DHCP
   - Verify PC1 gets IP from RRAS1
   - On RRAS1: right-click DHCP server -> All Tasks -> Pause/Stop
   - On PC1: ipconfig /release then ipconfig /renew
   - PC1 should now get IP from SRV1
   - Restart DHCP on RRAS1 and verify counters (Replies received) increase

section: IIS AND FTP ON WINDOWS SERVER
content:
1. Open IIS Manager on SRV1
2. Right-click Sites -> Add FTP Site
3. Site name: ftp.school.test
4. Content directory: C:\inetpub\ftproot
5. IP binding: 172.16.2.3
6. SSL: No SSL (SSL added later with CA)
7. Authentication: Anonymous and Basic
8. Authorization: Read and Write for all users
9. Create a subdirectory: C:\inetpub\ftproot\belangrijk
10. If write access fails: add Domain Users to NTFS permissions on ftproot with Modify rights
11. On RRAS1 — disable built-in FTP proxy (interferes with FTP through NAT):
    netsh routing ip nat delete ftp
12. Test: ftp://ftp.school.test in browser or Windows File Explorer

content:
1. Open IIS Manager -> select website -> Authentication
2. Enable Windows Authentication
3. Disable Anonymous Authentication
4. Restart the IIS service (not the server): IIS home -> Actions -> Restart

Only works natively from Windows domain-joined clients.
Linux clients cannot authenticate with Windows Auth without additional config.

subsection: Create a Storage Pool and Virtual Disk
content:
1. Shut down SRV1
2. Add two virtual disks (5GB each, SATA type) via VM settings
3. Start SRV1 and log in with domain admin
4. Server Manager -> File and Storage Services -> Disks
5. Right-click each new disk -> Initialize
6. Under Storage Pools -> TASKS -> New Storage Pool:
   Name: Webservers
   Select both new disks, Allocation: Automatic
7. Click on pool 'Webservers' -> New Virtual Disk:
   Name: storagepool1
   Storage layout: Simple (striped = RAID0)
   Provisioning: Thin
   Size: 2 GB
8. New Volume Wizard starts automatically:
   Select disk: storagepool1
   Size: 2 GB
   Drive letter: E (not C)
   File system: ReFS
   Volume label: Applicatie_data
9. Verify E:\ appears in Windows Explorer

ReFS notes:
  - Still in development
  - Cannot boot OS from ReFS
  - No disk quotas, compression, or EFS support yet
  - NTFS and Share permissions still apply to folders
  - Microsoft equivalent of Linux LVM

content:
DNS is critical for AD — without DNS, AD does not function.
Netlogon service uses DNS to locate DCs.

Recommendation: give every DC the DNS Server role (fault tolerance + performance).

DNS zone types:
  Standard zone: stored as text file, only primary can accept writes
  AD-integrated zone: stored as AD objects, replicated via AD, all DCs can accept writes

Namespace design:
  External namespace: resolvable on internet
  Internal namespace: only used internally
    - All domains and TLDs can be used internally
    - .internal is reserved for private name addressing
    - Do NOT use same name internally and externally (security risk)
    - Recommended: use different TLD (e.g. company.com external, company.local internal)

AD DS security:
  Kerberos: secure authentication — passwords never sent over network
    - Client sends encrypted ticket request to KDC
    - KDC issues ticket — like solving a puzzle only the other party knows
  IPsec: can be used for additional encryption between DCs

content:
RRAS (Routing and Remote Access Service) provides:
  - Static routing or RIP (dynamic routing)
  - DHCP Relay Agent (equivalent to Cisco 'ip helper-address')
  - NAT/PAT (Network Address Translation)
  - VPN server functionality

DHCP Relay Agent:
  Forwards DHCP broadcasts from clients to a DHCP server on another subnet.
  Configure under: Routing and Remote Access -> IPv4 -> DHCP Relay Agent
  Add the IP of the DHCP server in Relay Agent Properties.

NAT:
  Translates private internal IPs to a public IP for internet access.
  Configured under IPv4 -> NAT.

   - Is the username shown on the login screen after logout?
   - Can the Run command be used?
   - Can the desktop be customized?
   - Is Control Panel directly accessible?
3. Use 'gpresult' and 'gpupdate' if settings are not as expected.
4. Check time synchronization between client and server.
5. Confirm PC2 is in the kiosk-computers OU.
6. Explain the difference in behavior for 'piethein' on PC1 vs PC2.
7. Also verify behavior with admin account 'ad'.

1. On DC1: create and share the folder C:\shares\medewerkers.
   - Grant 'Domain Users' Full Control.
   - Remove all other groups.
2. Create a new GPO named 'Folder Redirection'.
   Path: User Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Folder Redirection > Documents > Properties
3. Set redirection option: Basic - Redirect everyone's folder to the same location.
   Target: \\dc1\medewerkers  (UNC format)
4. Link the GPO to the medewerkers OU.
5. Users must log off and back on for redirection to take effect.
6. Verify on PC1: check Documents folder properties (should show green icon).
7. Verify on DC1: new user folders should appear in the medewerkers share.

   If it says 'local computer', the central store was not set up correctly.

content:
This assignment covers two topics:
1. Windows Advanced Firewall with IPsec (Server & Domain Isolation)
2. Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) with Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS)

Required VMs: DC1 (172.16.1.1), RRAS1, SRV1, PC1 (172.16.1.10)
Optional: CA1.school.test (standalone offline root CA — not required for practical exam)

Before starting: verify AD replication with 'repadmin /showrepl'. Force sync with 'repadmin /syncall' if needed. Take snapshots before making changes.

subsection: Add Inbound Firewall Rules to GPO
content:
1. In the same GPO, expand Firewall → Inbound Rules → New Rule.
2. ICMP/Ping rule (Domain profile only):
   - Type: Predefined → File and Printer Sharing
   - Leave all checkboxes selected → Action: Allow the connection
3. FTP rule (Domain profile only):
   - Type: Custom → All Programs
   - Protocol: TCP → Remote Port: 20, 21
   - Scope → Local or Remote IP: 172.16.0.0/16
   - Action: Allow the connection
   - Name: FTP

content:
1. On RRAS1: install Active Directory Certificate Services role.
2. Include all Role Services (including IIS web service). Check all roles.
3. Restart RRAS1 after installation.
4. In Server Manager: click the exclamation mark (!) → configure ADCS.
5. Configure the following role services:
   - Certification Authority
   - Certification Authority Web Enrollment
   - Online Responder
6. Settings during wizard:
   - CA type: Enterprise CA and Root CA
   - Private key: Create a new private key
   - All other defaults are acceptable
7. After first config: select Yes to configure additional role services.
8. Select Certificate Enrollment Web Service:
   - Use service account: CA_Admin
   - Server certificate: select default (school-RRAS1-CA)
9. Configure remaining services — use admin@school.test and school.test for RA info. Defaults are fine.

1. On SRV1: open MMC (Win+R → mmc).
2. Add snap-ins: Certificates for 'My user account' and 'Computer account'.

4. Under Certificates (Local Computer) → Personal → Certificates: check for existing certs.

9. View certificate properties (right-click → Properties) — should show EFS, Secure Email, Client Authentication.

subsection: Configure Auto-Enrollment via Default Domain Policy
content:
1. On DC1: open GPMC → Default Domain Policy → Edit.
2. Navigate to: Computer Configuration → Policies → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Public Key Policies.
3. Open 'Certificate Services Client - Auto-Enrollment'.
4. Set to: Enabled
5. Check: Renew expired certificates, update pending certificates and revoked certificates.
6. Click Apply and OK.
7. On all Windows VMs: run 'gpupdate /force' and 'certutil -pulse'.
8. On RRAS1: verify new certificates appear in 'Issued Certificates' in the CA tool.

content:
1. On RRAS1: open Certification Authority → Certificate Templates → right-click → Manage.
2. Find 'Workstation Authentication' → right-click → Duplicate Template.
3. Make the following changes:
   - General tab: Display name = Client-Server Authentication. Check 'Publish certificate in Active Directory'.
   - Extensions tab: Application Policies → Edit → Add 'Server Authentication'. Click OK and Apply.
   - Security tab: Domain Computers → check Autoenroll (and Read if not already set). Optionally check Enroll.
4. Click Apply/OK.
5. Back in CA Authority: Certificate Templates folder → right-click → New → Certificate Template to Issue.
6. Select 'Client-Server Authentication' → OK.
   (May take time to appear due to DC replication; force with repadmin or gpupdate)
7. On SRV1: open certificate MMC → Certificates (Local Computer) → Personal → Certificates.
   Verify srv1.school.test cert appears with intended purposes: Server Authentication and Client Authentication.

subsection: Create VPN Certificate Template (Required for VPN Assignment)
content:
1. On RRAS1: open Certificate Templates Console (via Manage in CA Authority).
2. Duplicate the 'Web Server' template:
   - General tab: Name = VPN. Check 'Publish certificate in Active Directory'.
   - Compatibility tab: Certification Authority = 2016, Certificate recipient = Windows 10. Accept warnings.
   - Security tab: Read and Enroll for Administrators and Authenticated Users.
   - Subject Name tab: verify 'Supply in the request' and check 'Use subject information'.
3. Click Apply and OK.
4. Add VPN to Certificate Templates to Issue in the CA Authority.

1. On RRAS1: open MMC with Certificates snap-in for Local Computer.
2. Personal → All Tasks → Request New Certificate.
3. Select the VPN certificate template → click the exclamation mark to fill in required details.
4. Subject name type: Common Name. Enter the VPN hostname (e.g. sstp.school.test).
   IMPORTANT: This name must match exactly what you use when configuring the VPN later.
5. Click Add to move the name to the right panel.
6. Click Enroll. Verify the process completes successfully.
7. If a revocation error occurs: restart the CA service via All Tasks → Stop Service, then Start Service.

content:
This assignment covers VPN configuration on both Windows and Linux.

Windows: PPTP, SSTP, NPS (Network Policy Server)
Linux: OpenVPN (single-client), optional WireGuard

Required VMs: DC1, RRAS1, SRV1, PC1 (add second NIC for external testing)
Linux: srv2.bmc.test (OpenVPN server), rocky2.bmc.test (OpenVPN client), VyOS router

Take snapshots before starting. Start all DCs weekly and verify replication.

content:
1. Start RRAS1, DC1, and SRV1.
2. Open Routing and Remote Access on RRAS1.
3. Stop the RRAS service (right-click RRAS1 (local) → All Tasks → Stop).
4. Open Properties:
   - General tab: enable 'IPv4 Remote Access Server'; change to 'LAN and demand-dial routing'. Click Apply.
   - IPv4 tab: create static address pool 172.16.1.201 – 172.16.1.210 (no overlap with DHCP pool, no subnet mask needed).
   - Logging tab: enable 'Log additional Routing and Remote Access information'.
   - Security tab: verify Authentication Provider = Windows Authentication. Authentication Methods: EAP and MS-CHAPv2 selected.
5. Restart the RRAS service.
6. Verify Ports and Remote Access Clients appear in the MMC.
7. In Ports → Properties → PPTP → Configure: enable 'Remote access connections' and 'Demand-dial routing'. Repeat for SSTP.
   Under 'Used by' column, verify PPTP shows RAS/Routing.

content:
1. On SRV1: open Network and Sharing Center (ncpa.cpl).
2. Set up a new connection: Set up a new connection or network → Connect to a workplace → Use my Internet connection (VPN).
3. Internet address: 172.16.2.251. Name: PPTP-VPN. Uncheck 'Remember credentials'. Check 'Allow other people'. Click Create.
4. Open the new adapter → Properties:
   - Security tab: change 'Automatic' to PPTP.
   - Authentication: EAP → EAP-MSCHAPv2.
5. On DC1 in ADUC: open administrator user Properties → Dial-in tab → change to 'Allow access'. Do the same for your own user account.
6. On SRV1: connect to PPTP-VPN. Verify active port on RRAS1.
7. Test: type \\dc1.school.test in Explorer to access shares.

content:
Common error codes and fixes:
  - 'Windows was unable to add this computer to valid remote access servers': add RRAS1 to 'RAS and IAS Servers' group in ADUC, or run: netsh ras add registeredserver (on RRAS1)
  - 'Connection terminated by remote computer': use CHAP instead of MS-CHAP, disable required encryption
  - 807: check IP settings, ping RRAS1 from PC
  - 629: open firewall port 1723
  - 913: add GRE TCP port 47
  - 720: disable firewall temporarily
  - 812: RRAS1 not member of RAS IAS Servers group — add and restart service

subsection: Install and Configure Network Policy Server
content:
1. Install the NPAS (NPS) role on RRAS1.
2. Open Network Policy Server via Tools.
3. Go to Policies → Network Policies → right-click → New.
4. Name: VPN-gebruikers. Type: Remote Access Server (VPN-Dial up).
5. Add Condition: Windows Groups → add 'Domain Users'.
6. Specify Access Permission:
   - Select 'Access is granted'
   - Also check 'Access is determined by NPS Network Policy'
7. Add EAP-MSCHAPv2 to Authentication Methods (EAP Types) if not already present.
8. Constraints: set idle time to 5 minutes. Day and Time Restrictions: allow only weekdays (or block midnight–6am all days).
9. Configure Settings: remove the 'No Encryption' checkmark to require encryption.
10. On DC1 in ADUC: change dial-in tab for administrator and your user back to 'Control access through NPS Network Policy'.
11. Test that PPTP connection still works with the new policy.

content:
1. Install DNS Server role on RRAS1 via Server Manager.
2. On DC1: open DNS tool → zone school.test → Properties → Name Servers tab → add RRAS1 with IP 172.16.1.251 (test may fail initially).
3. Zone Transfers tab on DC1: enable Allow zone transfer.
4. On RRAS1 (after DNS installed): Forward Lookup Zones → New Zone → Secondary Zone → domain: school.test → Master server: DC1 (172.16.1.1).
5. In zone Properties → Zone Transfers: Only to following servers → add DC1 (172.16.1.1) and optionally SRV1 (172.16.2.3).
6. On DC1: add an A record for RRAS1's external IP and a CNAME record 'sstp' pointing to RRAS1.school.test.
7. On PC1: ping sstp.school.test. Run ipconfig /flushdns if needed. Run nslookup sstp.school.test — should return two IPs.

content:
1. On PC1: Network and Sharing Center → Set up a new connection → Connect to a workplace.
2. Internet address: sstp.school.test. Destination name: SSTP-VPN. Check 'Allow other people'.
3. Change Adapter Settings → SSTP-VPN Properties → Security tab → Type of VPN: SSTP. Enable EAP (Microsoft Secured Password EAP-MSCHAPv2).
4. Apply CRL revocation workaround via regedit:
   Key: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SstpSvc\Parameters
   New DWORD (32-bit): NoCertRevocationCheck = 1
5. Connect to SSTP-VPN using administrator account or your personal account (dial-in must allow access).
6. On RRAS1: verify active SSTP port connection under Ports.

content:
1. Shut down PC1. Add a second NIC in the same network as RRAS1's internet/WAN interface.
2. Disable the Rotterdam LAN NIC on PC1.
3. Start PC1. Set static IP matching the external network. Point DNS and default gateway to RRAS1.
4. Verify with ping and nslookup sstp.school.test.
5. Connect via SSTP-VPN. Verify VPN IP is assigned (subnet mask /32, no default gateway).
6. Access \\dc1 and \\srv1 in File Explorer to verify internal share access.
7. After testing: move PC1 back to Rotterdam LAN segment, re-enable the NIC, set to DHCP, disable the second NIC. Verify ping to 172.16.1.1.

# Copy easy-rsa files
sudo cp -rp /usr/share/easy-rsa/3.0/ /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0
sudo cp -p /usr/share/doc/easy-rsa/vars.example /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/vars

# Edit vars file
sudo nano /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/vars
# Uncomment and set:
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_COUNTRY "NL"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_PROVINCE "Utrecht"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_CITY "Utrecht"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_ORG "School Test organisatie"
# set_var EASYRSA_REQ_EMAIL "security@bmc.test"

# Generate certificates
cd /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/
./easyrsa clean-all
./easyrsa gen-dh
./easyrsa build-ca
./easyrsa build-server-full server1 nopass
./easyrsa build-client-full client1

# Generate TLS authentication key
openvpn --genkey --secret /etc/openvpn/server/ta.key

# View generated files
ls /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/
ls /etc/openvpn/server/easyrsa3.0/pki/issued/

subsection: Enable IP Forwarding and Firewall Rules on srv2
content:
# Enable IP forwarding temporarily
sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

# Make permanent
sudo nano /etc/sysctl.conf
# Add: net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

# Apply
sysctl -p

# Configure firewall (replace ens160 with actual interface toward VyOS/LAN)
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -i tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -o tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 nat POSTROUTING 0 -o ens160 -j MASQUERADE
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -i tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -o tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 nat POSTROUTING 0 -o ens160 -j MASQUERADE

# Test LAN access via VPN

privkey=$(sudo cat /etc/wireguard/$HOSTNAME.private.key)
# Get client public key from rocky2:
# sudo cat /etc/wireguard/rocky2.bmc.test.public.key
clientkey='<public key of client>'

sudo tee /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf <<WGCONFIG
[Interface]
Address = 192.0.2.1/24, 2001:db8:1::1/32
ListenPort = 51820
PrivateKey = $privkey

[Peer]
PublicKey = $clientkey
AllowedIPs = 192.0.2.2, 2001:db8:1::2
WGCONFIG

sudo chmod 600 /etc/wireguard/wg0.conf
sudo systemctl enable --now wg-quick@wg0

# Firewall
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --add-port=51820/udp --zone=public
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=public --add-masquerade
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

content:
1. Gateway-to-Gateway VPN: a VPN between two network gateways (routers/firewalls) representing two complete networks, encrypting all inter-network traffic.
2. Four tunneling protocols: PPTP, L2TP, SSTP, IKEv2.
3. L2TP uses IPsec for encryption (L2TP itself provides none).
4. IPsec disadvantage: compatibility issues with NAT.
5. SSTP uses TCP port 443.
6. IKEv2 advantage: fast reconnect after network changes (e.g. Wi-Fi to mobile).
7. VPN Reconnect: IKEv2 feature that automatically restores VPN after temporary interruption.
8. NPS: Microsoft RADIUS implementation — manages network access via policies, authentication, authorization.
9. HCAP: Health Certificate Authority Protocol — integrates NPS with NAP to check client health before granting access.
10. Network Access Policy: defines conditions under which users/devices get network access (time, location, device state, etc.).
11. Policy conditions: criteria like user group, IP, or time. Policy order: first matching policy is applied.
12. Example condition: 'User is member of RemoteUsers group'.
13. Auth protocols: PAP, CHAP, MS-CHAP, MS-CHAPv2, EAP, PEAP.
14. Most secure: EAP-TLS (mutual certificate verification).
15. L2TP with IPsec advantages: strong encryption, multi-protocol support, better security than PPTP.
16. Ports: PPTP = TCP 1723 + GRE (protocol 47); L2TP = UDP 1701.

content:
1. Every VPN solution requires a tunneling driver/VPN protocol stack for encapsulating/decapsulating traffic.
2. TLS/SSL: used for encryption and authentication → Confidentiality.
3. HMAC: verifies message integrity → Integrity.
4. X.509 certificates / pre-shared keys: used for authentication → Confidentiality and Integrity.
5. Missing CIA aspect: Availability — covered by failover servers, load balancing, DDoS protection.
6. Minimum: 1 IP address and 1 port (default UDP 1194).
7. TAP mode (Layer 2 bridging) creates a single broadcast domain.
8. Server: accepts connections and manages virtual network. Client: connects to server to access network.
9. X.509 advantages over PSK: scalable (per-client certs), stronger security, individual revocation possible.
10. Split tunnel: only traffic destined for the remote network goes through VPN; other traffic uses local connection.
11. IP forwarding required: without it, the VPN server cannot forward client traffic to other networks (LAN, internet).

This assignment sets up a Linux mail stack using Postfix (MTA/MSA) and Dovecot (Access Agent) on rocky1.bmc.test, with rocky2.bmc.test as the mail client.

Components:
  - Postfix: Message Transfer Agent
  - Dovecot: IMAP/POP3 Access Agent
  - Evolution or Thunderbird: Mail User Agent
  - mailx: command-line mail client for testing

Prerequisites: VyOS router running, rocky1 and rocky2 can ping each other and reach the internet. DNS must resolve rocky1.bmc.test and rocky2.bmc.test.

# Open with line numbers visible
sudo nano -c /etc/postfix/main.cf

# Key sections to modify (approximate line numbers for Postfix 3.5.8-7.el8):
# Use Ctrl+W to search in nano

# ~line 94: INTERNET HOST AND DOMAIN NAMES
myhostname = rocky1.bmc.test

# ~line 102:
mydomain = bmc.test

# ~line 118: SENDING MAIL - remove # to enable
myorigin = $myhostname

# ~line 120: RECEIVING MAIL
inet_interfaces = all
inet_protocols = all

# ~line 164: use second mydestination line
mydestination = $myhostname, localhost.$mydomain, localhost, $mydomain

# ~line 272: TRUST AND RELAY CONTROL
mynetworks = 192.168.5.0/24

# Save the file!

content:
sudo tee /var/named/bmc.test.db <<EOM
\$TTL 3H
@ IN SOA @ hostmaster.bmc.test. (
 $(date +"%Y%m%d")01 ; serial
 1H ; refresh
 1M ; retry
 1D ; expire
 3M ) ; minimum
NS rocky1
MX 10 rocky1
rocky1 A 192.168.5.1
rocky2 A 192.168.5.2
EOM

sudo tee /var/named/192.168.5.db <<EOM
\$TTL 3H
@ IN SOA @ hostmaster.bmc.test. (
 $(date +"%Y%m%d")01 ; serial
 1H ; refresh
 1M ; retry
 1D ; expire
 3M ) ; minimum
NS rocky1.bmc.test.
1 PTR rocky1.bmc.test.
2 PTR rocky2.bmc.test.
EOM

sudo chown root:named /var/named/bmc.test.db /var/named/192.168.5.db
sudo chmod 640 /var/named/bmc.test.db /var/named/192.168.5.db

# Start and enable BIND
sudo systemctl restart named
sudo systemctl enable named
systemctl status named

# Test MX record
host -t mx bmc.test

# Install mailx and mail clients on both rocky1 and rocky2
sudo dnf install -y mailx evolution thunderbird

# On rocky2: install and enable Postfix for local mail delivery
sudo dnf install -y postfix
sudo systemctl enable --now postfix

# Create test user on rocky1
sudo useradd stacker
sudo passwd stacker

# Switch to local user
su - stacker

# Send test mail using a file as body
mailx -s "Dit is een eerste mail test" stacker@bmc.test < /etc/nsswitch.conf

# Read mail (as recipient user)
su - stacker
mailx
# Commands inside mailx: p (print/read), ? (help), q (quit)

# Send a reply mail
mailx -s "Hallo" <other_user>@bmc.test
# Type message body, then press Ctrl+D to send

# View mail file directly
sudo cat /var/spool/mail/stacker

# Check Postfix mail log
sudo cat /var/log/maillog

subsection: Install Dovecot and Update Postfix for Auth
content:
sudo dnf install -y dovecot

# Add Maildir and SASL auth settings to Postfix main.cf
sudo nano /etc/postfix/main.cf

# Find and enable (remove #):
# home_mailbox = Maildir/

# Find smtpd_banner section and add/enable:
# smtpd_banner = $myhostname ESMTP $mail_name

# Add below smtpd_banner section (no indentation allowed!):
#SMTP authenticatie zonder beveiliging
smtpd_sasl_type = dovecot
smtpd_sasl_path = private/auth
smtpd_sasl_auth_enable = yes
smtpd_sasl_security_options = noanonymous
smtpd_sasl_local_domain = $myhostname
smtpd_recipient_restrictions = permit_mynetworks, permit_auth_destination, permit_sasl_authenticated, reject

# Save and restart Postfix
sudo systemctl restart postfix

Start Evolution on rocky1 and follow the setup wizard:

1. Identity tab: enter Full Name and email address (user@bmc.test)
2. Receiving Email tab:
   - Server type: IMAP (or POP3)
   - Server: rocky1.bmc.test
   - Port: 143
   - Username: local account name
   - Security: No encryption
   - Authentication: Password (click 'Check for supported types' if needed)
3. Sending Email tab:
   - Protocol: SMTP
   - Server: rocky1.bmc.test
   - Port: 25
   - Check 'Server requires authentication'
   - Security: No encryption
   - Authentication: PLAIN
4. Repeat for a second user account via Edit → Accounts → Add.

content:
# Work as root in the certs directory
sudo su -
cd /etc/pki/tls/certs

# Generate private key (with passphrase then remove it)
openssl genrsa -aes128 2048 > rocky12.key
openssl rsa -in rocky12.key -out rocky12.key

# Create Certificate Signing Request
openssl req -utf8 -new -key rocky12.key -out rocky12.csr
# Fill in when prompted:
# Country Name: NL
# State: Utrecht
# Locality: Utrecht
# Organization: bmc.test
# OU: ict
# Common Name: rocky1.bmc.test
# Email: postmaster@bmc.test
# Challenge password: (press Enter to skip)
# Optional company name: bmc.test

# Self-sign certificate (valid 10 years)
openssl x509 -in rocky12.csr -out rocky12.crt -req -signkey rocky12.key -days 3652

# Set secure permissions
chmod 600 rocky12.key

subsection: Restart Services and Apply SELinux Context
content:
# Restart both services
sudo systemctl restart postfix dovecot

# Check status
systemctl status postfix dovecot

# Check and apply SELinux context to certificates
sestatus
restorecon -v /etc/pki/tls/certs/rocky12.crt
restorecon -v /etc/pki/tls/certs/rocky12.key

# Update firewall for secure mail services
sudo firewall-cmd --list-all
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=smtp-submission --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=smtps --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=pop3s --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=imaps --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

# Verify
firewall-cmd --list-all

content:
For each account in Evolution:
1. Right-click account → Properties.
2. Receiving Email tab: set Security to STARTTLS. Click 'Check for supported types' and permanently accept the certificate. Authentication: Password.
3. Sending Email tab: set Security to STARTTLS. Authentication: PLAIN.
4. Repeat for the second user account.

If errors occur, check: /var/log/maillog
Also useful: grep dovecot /var/log/messages or grep postfix /var/log/messages

content:
sudo dnf install wireshark -y
sudo chmod +x /usr/bin/dumpcap
sudo usermod -a -G wireshark <your_username>

# Start Wireshark
wireshark&

# Select ens160 or 'any' interface
# Apply filter: smtp

content:
# SGID on directory — new files inherit directory group
sudo chmod g+s /data/project
# Sticky bit — only owner can delete their own files
sudo chmod +t /data/tmp
sudo chmod 1777 /data/tmp
# SUID on executable — runs as file owner
sudo chmod u+s /tmp/tail
# Find all root-owned SUID files
sudo find / -user root -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null
# Find SGID files
sudo find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null

sudo nano /etc/sysctl.conf
# net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
sysctl -p
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -i tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -o tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --direct --add-rule ipv4 nat POSTROUTING 0 -o ens160 -j MASQUERADE
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -i tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 filter FORWARD 0 -o tun+ -j ACCEPT
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --direct --add-rule ipv4 nat POSTROUTING 0 -o ens160 -j MASQUERADE

Get-Command -Module DHCPServer         # all DHCP server commands

Get-ADUser jansen | Get-Member         # see all properties + methods
Get-Service | Get-Member

Get-Alias ls                           # what does ls alias to?
Get-Alias | Where-Object {$_.Definition -eq 'Get-ChildItem'}

content:
# Where-Object — filter results
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Where-Object {$_.Enabled -eq $false}
Get-Process | Where-Object {$_.CPU -gt 10}
Get-EventLog -LogName System -Newest 100 | Where-Object {$_.EntryType -eq 'Error'}

# Select-Object — choose specific properties
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, Enabled
Get-NetIPAddress | Select-Object InterfaceAlias, IPAddress, PrefixLength
Get-Process | Select-Object -First 10
Get-Process | Select-Object -Last 5

# Sort-Object
Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Sort-Object Name
Get-HotFix | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending

# Format output
Get-Service | Format-Table Name, Status, StartType
Get-ADUser jansen -Properties * | Format-List
Get-NetAdapter | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Export and output
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Export-Csv C:\users.csv -NoTypeInformation
Get-Service | Out-File C:\services.txt
Get-ADUser -Filter * | ConvertTo-Json | Out-File C:\users.json
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test | Export-Csv C:\dns.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Measure and count
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Measure-Object          # count results
(Get-ADUser -Filter *).Count                   # quick count
Get-Process | Measure-Object CPU -Sum -Average # aggregate CPU

subsection: Create, View and Modify AD Users
content:
Import-Module ActiveDirectory

# Create a basic user
New-ADUser -Name "Jan Jansen" -SamAccountName jjansen -Enabled $true `
    -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd1" -AsPlainText -Force)

# Create a fully detailed user in a specific OU
New-ADUser `
    -Name "Piet Hein" `
    -GivenName "Piet" `
    -Surname "Hein" `
    -SamAccountName phein `
    -UserPrincipalName phein@school.test `
    -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd1" -AsPlainText -Force) `
    -Enabled $true `
    -Path "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test" `
    -Department "IT" `
    -Title "Sysadmin" `
    -Office "Rotterdam" `
    -Description "IT staff member"

# View user
Get-ADUser jjansen
Get-ADUser jjansen -Properties *                          # all properties
Get-ADUser jjansen -Properties Department, Title, Email   # specific properties

# Search users
Get-ADUser -Filter {Name -like "Jan*"}
Get-ADUser -Filter {Enabled -eq $false}                   # all disabled accounts
Get-ADUser -Filter {Department -eq "IT"}
Get-ADUser -Filter * -SearchBase "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"  # within OU
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, Enabled

# Modify user
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -Title "Senior Sysadmin"
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -Department "Infrastructure"
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -Description "Updated description"
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -OfficePhone "0612345678"
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -EmailAddress "jjansen@school.test"
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -PasswordNeverExpires $true
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -SmartcardLogonRequired $false

# Reset password
Set-ADAccountPassword -Identity jjansen `
    -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "NewP@ss1" -AsPlainText -Force) -Reset

# Force password change at next logon
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -ChangePasswordAtLogon $true

# Enable / disable
Enable-ADAccount -Identity jjansen
Disable-ADAccount -Identity jjansen

# Unlock locked account
Unlock-ADAccount -Identity jjansen

# Find locked accounts
Search-ADAccount -LockedOut
Search-ADAccount -LockedOut | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName

# Find disabled accounts
Search-ADAccount -AccountDisabled | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName

# Find accounts with expired passwords
Search-ADAccount -PasswordExpired | Select-Object Name, PasswordExpired

# Delete user
Remove-ADUser -Identity jjansen -Confirm:$false

# Move user to different OU
Get-ADUser jjansen | Move-ADObject -TargetPath "OU=admin,DC=school,DC=test"

content:
# CSV file format (save as users.csv):
# Name,First,Last,SamAccountName,Password,OU
# Jan Jansen,Jan,Jansen,jjansen,P@ssw0rd1,"OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
# Piet Hein,Piet,Hein,phein,P@ssw0rd1,"OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"

# Import and create users from CSV
$users = Import-Csv C:\users.csv
foreach ($user in $users) {
    New-ADUser `
        -Name $user.Name `
        -GivenName $user.First `
        -Surname $user.Last `
        -SamAccountName $user.SamAccountName `
        -UserPrincipalName "$($user.SamAccountName)@school.test" `
        -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString $user.Password -AsPlainText -Force) `
        -Enabled $true `
        -Path $user.OU
    Write-Host "Created user: $($user.Name)"
}

# Bulk disable users from a list
$toDisable = @("jjansen", "phein", "kwillem")
foreach ($user in $toDisable) {
    Disable-ADAccount -Identity $user
    Write-Host "Disabled: $user"
}

# Bulk add users to a group
$members = @("jjansen", "phein")
Add-ADGroupMember -Identity "Docenten_Global" -Members $members

# Export all users to CSV
Get-ADUser -Filter * -Properties * | `
    Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, EmailAddress, Department, Enabled | `
    Export-Csv C:\all-users.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Find all users who haven't logged in for 90 days
$cutoff = (Get-Date).AddDays(-90)
Get-ADUser -Filter {LastLogonDate -lt $cutoff -and Enabled -eq $true} `
    -Properties LastLogonDate | `
    Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, LastLogonDate

Import-Module ActiveDirectory

# Create OUs
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name "medewerkers" -Path "DC=school,DC=test"

New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name "slb"         -Path "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name "admin"       -Path "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name "gasten"      -Path "DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name "kiosk-computers" -Path "DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name "IPSec_Sys"   -Path "DC=school,DC=test"

# View all OUs
Get-ADOrganizationalUnit -Filter * | Select-Object Name, DistinguishedName

# Modify OU
Set-ADOrganizationalUnit -Identity "OU=gasten,DC=school,DC=test" -Description "Guest accounts"

# Disable accidental deletion protection (required before deleting)
Set-ADOrganizationalUnit -Identity "OU=gasten,DC=school,DC=test" `
    -ProtectedFromAccidentalDeletion $false

# Delete OU and everything inside
Remove-ADOrganizationalUnit -Identity "OU=gasten,DC=school,DC=test" -Recursive -Confirm:$false

# View computer objects
Get-ADComputer -Filter *
Get-ADComputer -Filter * | Select-Object Name, DistinguishedName, Enabled

Get-ADComputer PC1 -Properties *

# Move computer to OU
Get-ADComputer PC2 | Move-ADObject -TargetPath "OU=kiosk-computers,DC=school,DC=test"
Get-ADComputer RRAS1 | Move-ADObject -TargetPath "OU=IPSec_Sys,DC=school,DC=test"

# Disable a computer account
Disable-ADAccount -Identity (Get-ADComputer OldPC)

# Delete computer account
Get-ADComputer OldPC | Remove-ADObject -Recursive -Confirm:$false

# Find stale computer accounts (not logged in for 90 days)
$cutoff = (Get-Date).AddDays(-90)
Get-ADComputer -Filter {LastLogonDate -lt $cutoff} `
    -Properties LastLogonDate | Select-Object Name, LastLogonDate

Import-Module ActiveDirectory

# View domain info
Get-ADDomain
Get-ADDomain | Select-Object Name, DomainMode, PDCEmulator, RIDMaster, InfrastructureMaster

# View forest info
Get-ADForest
Get-ADForest | Select-Object Name, ForestMode, SchemaMaster, DomainNamingMaster

# View all domain controllers
Get-ADDomainController -Filter *
Get-ADDomainController -Filter * | Select-Object Name, Site, IsGlobalCatalog, IsReadOnly

# View FSMO role holders
netdom query fsmo
Get-ADDomain | Select-Object PDCEmulator, RIDMaster, InfrastructureMaster
Get-ADForest | Select-Object SchemaMaster, DomainNamingMaster

# Transfer FSMO role
Move-ADDirectoryServerOperationMasterRole -Identity dc1.school.test `
    -OperationMasterRole PDCEmulator -Force
Move-ADDirectoryServerOperationMasterRole -Identity dc1.school.test `
    -OperationMasterRole SchemaMaster, DomainNamingMaster, RIDMaster, InfrastructureMaster -Force

# Replication
repadmin /syncall
repadmin /syncall /Ade
repadmin /showrepl
repadmin /replsummary
repadmin /kcc

# AD Sites
Get-ADReplicationSite -Filter *
Get-ADReplicationSiteLink -Filter *
Get-ADReplicationConnection -Filter *

# DCDiag
dcdiag /test:connectivity
dcdiag /test:replications
dcdiag /a
dcdiag /e

# Check AD recycle bin status
Get-ADOptionalFeature -Filter {Name -like "Recycle*"}

# Enable AD recycle bin
Enable-ADOptionalFeature -Identity 'Recycle Bin Feature' `
    -Scope ForestOrConfigurationSet -Target school.test -Confirm:$false

# Restore deleted object from recycle bin
Get-ADObject -Filter {IsDeleted -eq $true -and Name -like "*jjansen*"} `
    -IncludeDeletedObjects | Restore-ADObject

content:
Import-Module DnsServer

# View all zones
Get-DnsServerZone
Get-DnsServerZone | Select-Object ZoneName, ZoneType, IsDsIntegrated, IsReverseLookupZone

# Create primary forward lookup zone (file-based)
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "school.test" -ZoneFile "school.test.dns"

# Create AD-integrated forward zone (stores in AD, replicates automatically)
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "school.test" -ReplicationScope Domain
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "bmc.test" -ReplicationScope Forest

# Create primary reverse lookup zone
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -NetworkID "172.16.1.0/24" -ZoneFile "1.16.172.in-addr.arpa.dns"
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -NetworkID "192.168.5.0/24" -ReplicationScope Domain

# Create secondary zone (read-only copy from primary)
Add-DnsServerSecondaryZone -Name "school.test" -ZoneFile "school.test.dns" `
    -MasterServers 172.16.1.1

# Create stub zone (only stores NS and SOA records)
Add-DnsServerStubZone -Name "school.test" -MasterServers 172.16.1.1 -ReplicationScope Domain

# View zone properties
Get-DnsServerZone -Name "school.test"

# Set zone to AD-integrated
Set-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "school.test" -ReplicationScope Domain

# Configure zone transfer
Set-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "school.test" `
    -SecureSecondaries TransferToZoneNameServer
Set-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "school.test" `
    -SecureSecondaries TransferToAnyServer

# Set notify (push changes to secondaries)
Set-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name "school.test" -Notify Notify `
    -NotifyServers 172.16.2.3

# Remove zone
Remove-DnsServerZone -Name "school.test" -Force

# Reload zone from file
Invoke-DnsServerZoneTransfer -Name "school.test"

# Sign zone with DNSSEC
Invoke-DnsServerZoneSign -ZoneName "school.test" -SignWithDefault

Import-Module DnsServer

# Add A record (hostname → IPv4 address)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name dc1  -IPv4Address 172.16.1.1
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name srv1 -IPv4Address 172.16.2.3
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name www  -IPv4Address 172.16.2.3
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name ftp  -IPv4Address 172.16.2.3
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name sstp -IPv4Address 172.16.1.251
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name rras1 -IPv4Address 172.16.1.251

# Add AAAA record (hostname → IPv6 address)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecord -AAAA -ZoneName school.test -Name dc1 `
    -IPv6Address "fd00::1"

# Add CNAME record (alias → canonical name)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordCName -ZoneName school.test -Name www `
    -HostNameAlias rras1.school.test
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordCName -ZoneName school.test -Name sstp `
    -HostNameAlias rras1.school.test
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordCName -ZoneName school.test -Name mail `
    -HostNameAlias rocky1.bmc.test

# Add MX record (mail exchanger)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordMX -ZoneName school.test -Name "." `
    -MailExchange mail.school.test -Preference 10
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordMX -ZoneName bmc.test -Name "." `
    -MailExchange rocky1.bmc.test -Preference 10

# Add PTR record (IP → hostname, in reverse zone)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordPtr -ZoneName "1.16.172.in-addr.arpa" `
    -Name "1" -PtrDomainName dc1.school.test
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordPtr -ZoneName "1.16.172.in-addr.arpa" `
    -Name "251" -PtrDomainName rras1.school.test
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordPtr -ZoneName "2.16.172.in-addr.arpa" `
    -Name "3" -PtrDomainName srv1.school.test

# Add TXT record (e.g. SPF)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Txt -Name "." `
    -DescriptiveText "v=spf1 mx ~all"

# Add SRV record
Add-DnsServerResourceRecord -Srv -ZoneName school.test `
    -Name "_ldap._tcp" -DomainName dc1.school.test `
    -Priority 0 -Weight 100 -Port 389

# Add NS record
Add-DnsServerResourceRecord -NS -ZoneName school.test `
    -Name "." -NameServer ns2.school.test

subsection: View, Modify and Remove DNS Records
content:
Import-Module DnsServer

# View all records in a zone
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test | Format-Table

# View records by type
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType A
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType CNAME
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType MX
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType PTR
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType TXT

# View specific record by name
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name www
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name dc1 -RRType A

# Modify a record (change TTL or data)
# Pattern: get old record, clone, modify, apply
$old = Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name www -RRType A
$new = $old.Clone()
$new.RecordData.IPv4Address = [System.Net.IPAddress]::Parse("172.16.2.10")
Set-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -OldInputObject $old -NewInputObject $new

# Change TTL on a record
$old = Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name dc1 -RRType A
$new = $old.Clone()
$new.TimeToLive = [TimeSpan]::FromMinutes(5)
Set-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -OldInputObject $old -NewInputObject $new

# Remove a specific A record
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name www -RRType A `
    -RecordData 172.16.2.3 -Force

# Remove a CNAME record
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name sstp -RRType CNAME -Force

# Remove ALL records for a name
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name oldserver -Force

# Remove a PTR record
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName "1.16.172.in-addr.arpa" `
    -Name 251 -RRType PTR -Force

# Remove a MX record
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name "." -RRType MX `
    -RecordData mail.school.test -Force

Import-Module DnsServer

# View current forwarders
Get-DnsServerForwarder

# Add forwarders
Add-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 8.8.8.8
Add-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 9.9.9.9
Add-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 8.8.4.4

# Remove a forwarder
Remove-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 8.8.8.8 -Force

# Set forwarders (replace all existing)
Set-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 8.8.8.8, 9.9.9.9

# Conditional forwarder (forward specific zone to specific server)
Add-DnsServerConditionalForwarderZone -Name "bmc.test" `
    -MasterServers 192.168.5.1
Add-DnsServerConditionalForwarderZone -Name "corp.internal" `
    -MasterServers 10.10.10.1, 10.10.10.2

# View conditional forwarders
Get-DnsServerZone | Where-Object {$_.ZoneType -eq 'Forwarder'}

# Remove conditional forwarder
Remove-DnsServerZone -Name "bmc.test" -Force

# Flush DNS server cache
Clear-DnsServerCache -Force

# View server cache
Get-DnsServerCache

# View DNS server settings
Get-DnsServer
Get-DnsServerSetting

# Enable/disable recursion
Set-DnsServerRecursion -Enable $true
Set-DnsServerRecursion -Enable $false

# View DNS server statistics
Get-DnsServerStatistics

# Flush DNS resolver cache (client-side)
Clear-DnsClientCache
Get-DnsClientCache                     # view current cache
Get-DnsClientCache | Where-Object {$_.Entry -like "*school*"}

Import-Module DhcpServer

# Authorize DHCP server in AD (required — otherwise server won't hand out leases)
Add-DhcpServerInDC -DnsName rras1.school.test -IPAddress 172.16.1.251
Get-DhcpServerInDC

# Create IPv4 scopes
Add-DhcpServerv4Scope -Name "LAN Rotterdam" `
    -StartRange 172.16.1.10 -EndRange 172.16.1.100 `
    -SubnetMask 255.255.255.0 -State Active

Add-DhcpServerv4Scope -Name "LAN DMZ" `
    -StartRange 172.16.2.10 -EndRange 172.16.2.100 `
    -SubnetMask 255.255.255.0 -State Active

Add-DhcpServerv4Scope -Name "LAN Amersfoort" `
    -StartRange 192.168.5.100 -EndRange 192.168.5.200 `
    -SubnetMask 255.255.255.0 `
    -LeaseDuration 1.00:00:00 `
    -State Active

# View all scopes
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope | Select-Object ScopeId, Name, StartRange, EndRange, State

# View scope details
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope -ScopeId 172.16.1.0

# Activate / deactivate scope
Set-DhcpServerv4Scope -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -State Active
Set-DhcpServerv4Scope -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -State InActive

Set-DhcpServerv4Scope -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -StartRange 172.16.1.20 -EndRange 172.16.1.150

Set-DhcpServerv4Scope -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -LeaseDuration 2.00:00:00   # 2 days

# Delete scope
Remove-DhcpServerv4Scope -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -Force

Import-Module DhcpServer

# Set scope options (gateway, DNS, domain)
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -Router 172.16.1.251
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -DnsServer 172.16.1.1, 172.16.2.3
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -DnsDomain school.test

# Set scope options for DMZ scope
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.2.0 -Router 172.16.2.251
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.2.0 -DnsServer 172.16.1.1, 172.16.2.3

# Set server-wide options (apply to all scopes unless overridden)
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -Router 172.16.1.251
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -DnsServer 172.16.1.1

# View options on a scope
Get-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0

# Remove a specific option
Remove-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -OptionId 6   # 6 = DNS servers
Remove-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -OptionId 3   # 3 = Router

# Add exclusion range (IPs that won't be handed out)
Add-DhcpServerv4ExclusionRange -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -StartRange 172.16.1.1 -EndRange 172.16.1.9
Add-DhcpServerv4ExclusionRange -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -StartRange 172.16.1.200 -EndRange 172.16.1.254

# View exclusion ranges
Get-DhcpServerv4ExclusionRange -ScopeId 172.16.1.0

# Remove exclusion range
Remove-DhcpServerv4ExclusionRange -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -StartRange 172.16.1.200 -EndRange 172.16.1.254

# Create a reservation (always give same IP to same MAC address)
Add-DhcpServerv4Reservation -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -IPAddress 172.16.1.50 `
    -ClientId "AA-BB-CC-DD-EE-FF" `
    -Name "PrinterLobby" `
    -Description "Lobby printer fixed IP"

Add-DhcpServerv4Reservation -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -IPAddress 172.16.1.51 `
    -ClientId "11-22-33-44-55-66" `
    -Name "Kiosk-PC1"

# View reservations
Get-DhcpServerv4Reservation -ScopeId 172.16.1.0

# Modify reservation
Set-DhcpServerv4Reservation -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -IPAddress 172.16.1.50 -Name "LobbyPrinter2" -Description "Updated"

# Remove reservation
Remove-DhcpServerv4Reservation -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -IPAddress 172.16.1.50 -Force

content:
Import-Module DhcpServer

# View active leases
Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId 172.16.1.0
Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 | `
    Select-Object IPAddress, HostName, ClientId, AddressState, LeaseExpiryTime

# View leases for all scopes
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope | ForEach-Object {
    Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId $_.ScopeId
}

# Find lease by hostname
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope | ForEach-Object {
    Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId $_.ScopeId
} | Where-Object {$_.HostName -like "PC1*"}

# Remove a lease (force client to renew)
Remove-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -IPAddress 172.16.1.25

# View DHCP statistics
Get-DhcpServerv4Statistics
Get-DhcpServerv4Statistics | Select-Object TotalScopes, TotalAddresses, InUse, Available

# Configure DHCP failover (Hot Standby mode)
Add-DhcpServerv4Failover `
    -Name "LAN-Failover" `
    -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 `
    -PartnerServer rras1.school.test `
    -Mode HotStandby `
    -ServerRole Active `
    -AutoStateTransition $true `
    -SharedSecret "FailoverSecret"

# View failover configuration
Get-DhcpServerv4Failover

# Remove failover
Remove-DhcpServerv4Failover -Name "LAN-Failover" -Force

# Replicate scopes to failover partner
Invoke-DhcpServerv4FailoverReplication -Name "LAN-Failover"

content:
Import-Module GroupPolicy

# View all GPOs
Get-GPO -All
Get-GPO -All | Select-Object DisplayName, GpoStatus, CreationTime, ModificationTime

# View specific GPO
Get-GPO -Name "Baseline Security"
Get-GPO -Name "Baseline Security" | Format-List *

# Create a new GPO
New-GPO -Name "Baseline Security"
New-GPO -Name "Restrict Control Panel"
New-GPO -Name "Kiosk Computer Security"
New-GPO -Name "Restrict Run Command"
New-GPO -Name "Folder Redirection"
New-GPO -Name "7-Zip Deploy"

# Link GPO to an OU or domain
New-GPLink -Name "Baseline Security" -Target "DC=school,DC=test"
New-GPLink -Name "Restrict Run Command" -Target "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-GPLink -Name "Restrict Control Panel" -Target "OU=kiosk-computers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-GPLink -Name "Kiosk Computer Security" -Target "OU=kiosk-computers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-GPLink -Name "Folder Redirection" -Target "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"

# Enforce a GPO link (bypasses block inheritance)
Set-GPLink -Name "Baseline Security" -Target "DC=school,DC=test" -Enforced Yes

# Disable a GPO link without removing it
Set-GPLink -Name "Restrict Run Command" `
    -Target "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test" -LinkEnabled No

# Remove a GPO link (unlinks but keeps the GPO)
Remove-GPLink -Name "Restrict Run Command" -Target "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"

# Block inheritance on an OU
Set-GPInheritance -Target "OU=admin,DC=school,DC=test" -IsBlocked Yes
Set-GPInheritance -Target "OU=admin,DC=school,DC=test" -IsBlocked No

# View inheritance on an OU
Get-GPInheritance -Target "OU=admin,DC=school,DC=test"
Get-GPInheritance -Target "DC=school,DC=test"

# Delete a GPO entirely
Remove-GPO -Name "Restrict Control Panel" -Confirm:$false

# Rename a GPO
Rename-GPO -Name "OldName" -TargetName "NewName"

Backup-GPO -Name "Baseline Security" -Path C:\GPOBackups

# View backups
Get-GPOBackup -All -Path C:\GPOBackups
Get-GPOBackup -All -Path C:\GPOBackups | Select-Object DisplayName, BackupId, Timestamp

# Restore a GPO
Restore-GPO -Name "Baseline Security" -Path C:\GPOBackups

# Import settings into a different GPO
Import-GPO -BackupGpoName "Baseline Security" -Path C:\GPOBackups `
    -TargetName "Baseline Security v2" -CreateIfNeeded

# Generate HTML report for a GPO
Get-GPOReport -Name "Baseline Security" -ReportType HTML `
    -Path C:\Reports\BaselineSecurity.html

# Generate HTML report for ALL GPOs
Get-GPOReport -All -ReportType HTML -Path C:\Reports\AllGPOs.html

# Apply GPO update on local machine
gpupdate /force
gpupdate /force /boot
gpupdate /force /logoff

# View applied GPOs on local machine
gpresult /R
gpresult /H C:\Reports\gpresult.html
gpresult /Scope Computer
gpresult /Scope User

# View applied GPOs for specific user on remote computer
gpresult /S pc1 /U school\piethein /R

# View all shares (excluding hidden admin shares)
Get-SmbShare
Get-SmbShare | Where-Object {$_.Special -eq $false}
Get-SmbShare | Select-Object Name, Path, Description

# Create share with default permissions
New-SmbShare -Name Data -Path C:\data

# Create share with specific permissions
New-SmbShare -Name Recepten -Path C:\data\recepten `
    -FullAccess "school\Huisartsen_DL" `
    -ReadAccess "school\Apothekers_DL"

New-SmbShare -Name Fysioverwijzingen -Path C:\data\fysio `
    -FullAccess "school\Huisartsen_DL" `
    -ReadAccess "school\Fysiotherapeuten_DL" `
    -NoAccess Everyone

# Create share with description
New-SmbShare -Name Medewerkers -Path C:\shares\medewerkers `
    -Description "Staff home folders" `
    -FullAccess "school\Domain Users"

# View share permissions
Get-SmbShareAccess -Name Recepten

# Add permission to existing share
Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name Data `
    -AccountName "school\Domain Users" -AccessRight Read -Force
Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name Data `
    -AccountName "school\Admins_DL" -AccessRight Full -Force

# Change a permission (revoke then re-grant)
Revoke-SmbShareAccess -Name Data -AccountName "school\Domain Users" -Force
Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name Data `
    -AccountName "school\Domain Users" -AccessRight Change -Force

# Remove a share (does NOT delete the folder)
Remove-SmbShare -Name Data -Force

# View connected sessions
Get-SmbSession
Get-SmbSession | Select-Object ClientComputerName, ClientUserName, NumOpens

# View open files on share
Get-SmbOpenFile
Get-SmbOpenFile | Select-Object ClientComputerName, Path

# Disconnect all sessions
Get-SmbSession | Close-SmbSession -Force

# Map a network drive (useful in scripts)
New-PSDrive -Name Z -PSProvider FileSystem -Root \\dc1\medewerkers -Persist
net use Z: \\dc1\medewerkers /persistent:yes

# View NTFS ACL
Get-Acl C:\data\recepten
Get-Acl C:\data\recepten | Format-List
(Get-Acl C:\data\recepten).Access | Format-Table IdentityReference, FileSystemRights, AccessControlType

# Grant NTFS permission
$acl  = Get-Acl C:\data\recepten
$rule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule(
    "school\Huisartsen_DL",
    "FullControl",
    "ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit",
    "None",
    "Allow"
)
$acl.AddAccessRule($rule)
Set-Acl C:\data\recepten $acl

# Deny permission
$acl  = Get-Acl C:\data\recepten
$deny = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule(
    "Everyone",
    "Write",
    "ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit",
    "None",
    "Deny"
)
$acl.AddAccessRule($deny)
Set-Acl C:\data\recepten $acl

# Remove inherited permissions (break inheritance)
$acl = Get-Acl C:\data\recepten
$acl.SetAccessRuleProtection($true, $false)
Set-Acl C:\data\recepten $acl

# Sign-off command: show shares, permissions and Windows version
$shares = Get-SmbShare | Where-Object {$_.Special -eq $false}
$shares | Get-SmbShareAccess
(Get-Acl $shares.path).access | Format-Table
gp "HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion" | Select-Object InstallationType

# View adapters
Get-NetAdapter
Get-NetAdapter | Format-Table Name, InterfaceIndex, MacAddress, Status, LinkSpeed

# Rename adapters
Rename-NetAdapter -Name "Ethernet0" -NewName "LAN_Rotterdam"
Rename-NetAdapter -Name "Ethernet1" -NewName "DMZ1"
Rename-NetAdapter -Name "Ethernet2" -NewName "Leased"
Rename-NetAdapter -Name "Ethernet3" -NewName "WAN"

# Enable / disable adapter
Disable-NetAdapter -Name "DMZ1" -Confirm:$false
Enable-NetAdapter -Name "DMZ1"
Restart-NetAdapter -Name "LAN_Rotterdam"

# Disable IPv6
Disable-NetAdapterBinding -Name "LAN_Rotterdam" -ComponentID ms_tcpip6

# View IP addresses
Get-NetIPAddress
Get-NetIPAddress -AddressFamily IPv4
Get-NetIPAddress -InterfaceAlias "LAN_Rotterdam"

# Set static IP
Remove-NetIPAddress -InterfaceAlias "LAN_Rotterdam" -Confirm:$false
Remove-NetRoute -InterfaceAlias "LAN_Rotterdam" -DestinationPrefix "0.0.0.0/0" -Confirm:$false
New-NetIPAddress -InterfaceAlias "LAN_Rotterdam" `
    -IPAddress 172.16.1.1 -PrefixLength 24 -DefaultGateway 172.16.1.251
Set-DnsClientServerAddress -InterfaceAlias "LAN_Rotterdam" `
    -ServerAddresses 172.16.1.1, 8.8.8.8

# View routing table
Get-NetRoute
Get-NetRoute -AddressFamily IPv4

# Add / remove static route
New-NetRoute -DestinationPrefix "10.0.0.0/8" `
    -NextHop 192.168.1.1 -InterfaceAlias "LAN_Rotterdam"
Remove-NetRoute -DestinationPrefix "10.0.0.0/8" -Confirm:$false

# Test connectivity
Test-NetConnection 8.8.8.8
Test-NetConnection dc1.school.test -Port 389    # test LDAP
Test-NetConnection dc1.school.test -Port 3389   # test RDP
Test-NetConnection dc1.school.test -Port 443    # test HTTPS

# DNS lookup
Resolve-DnsName dc1.school.test
Resolve-DnsName school.test -Type MX
Resolve-DnsName 172.16.1.1             # reverse lookup

content:
# View firewall profiles
Get-NetFirewallProfile
Get-NetFirewallProfile | Select-Object Name, Enabled, DefaultInboundAction

# Enable / disable firewall
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain,Public,Private -Enabled True
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain,Public,Private -Enabled False

# View all active inbound rules
Get-NetFirewallRule | Where-Object {$_.Direction -eq 'Inbound' -and $_.Enabled -eq $true} `
    | Select-Object DisplayName, Action

# Create inbound rule — allow a port
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTP" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 80 -Action Allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTPS" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 443 -Action Allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SMTP" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 25 -Action Allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "FTP" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 20,21 -Action Allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "OpenVPN" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol UDP -LocalPort 1194 -Action Allow

# Create inbound rule — allow ICMP (ping)
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow ICMPv4" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol ICMPv4 -IcmpType 8 -Action Allow

# Create rule with IP restriction
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "FTP-Restricted" -Direction Inbound `
    -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 20,21 -RemoteAddress 172.16.0.0/16 -Action Allow

# Enable built-in rule group
Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "Remote Desktop"
Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "File and Printer Sharing"
Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI)"

# Disable a rule
Disable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTP"

# Remove a rule
Remove-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTP"

# View available and installed features
Get-WindowsFeature
Get-WindowsFeature | Where-Object {$_.Installed -eq $true}
Get-WindowsFeature | Where-Object {$_.Installed -eq $true} | Select-Object Name, DisplayName

# Install common roles
Install-WindowsFeature -Name AD-Domain-Services -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name DHCP -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name DNS -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name Web-Server -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name Web-Server, Web-Windows-Auth, Web-Ftp-Server
Install-WindowsFeature -Name File-Services -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name FS-FileServer -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name RSAT-AD-Tools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name NPAS -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name RemoteAccess -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name ADCS-Cert-Authority -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature -Name ADCS-Web-Enrollment -IncludeManagementTools

# Install and auto-reboot if needed
Install-WindowsFeature -Name AD-Domain-Services -IncludeManagementTools -Restart

# Check if restart is needed after install
$result = Install-WindowsFeature -Name DNS
$result.Success
$result.RestartNeeded

# Remove a role
Remove-WindowsFeature -Name DHCP
Remove-WindowsFeature -Name Web-Server -Restart

# Promote server to domain controller (new forest)
Install-ADDSForest `
    -DomainName school.test `
    -DomainNetbiosName SCHOOL `
    -SafeModeAdministratorPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd1" -AsPlainText -Force) `
    -InstallDns -Force

# Add replica DC to existing domain
Install-ADDSDomainController `
    -DomainName school.test `
    -Credential (Get-Credential SCHOOL\administrator) `
    -SafeModeAdministratorPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd1" -AsPlainText -Force) `
    -InstallDns -SiteName "Intern" -ReplicationSourceDC dc1.school.test -Force

# View services
Get-Service
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Stopped'}
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.StartType -eq 'Disabled'}

# View specific service
Get-Service -Name dhcpserver
Get-Service -Name dhcpserver | Format-List *

# Start, stop, restart
Start-Service -Name dhcpserver
Stop-Service -Name dhcpserver
Restart-Service -Name dhcpserver
Restart-Service -Name dhcpserver -Force

# Set startup type
Set-Service -Name dhcpserver -StartupType Automatic
Set-Service -Name dhcpserver -StartupType Manual
Set-Service -Name dhcpserver -StartupType Disabled

# Start service and set to automatic in one line
Set-Service -Name dhcpserver -Status Running -StartupType Automatic

# Manage services on remote computer
Get-Service -ComputerName srv1
Restart-Service -Name DNS -ComputerName dc1

# Common service names
# dhcpserver   = DHCP Server
# dns          = DNS Server
# netlogon     = Netlogon
# w32tm        = Windows Time
# WinRM        = Windows Remote Management
# wuauserv     = Windows Update
# spooler      = Print Spooler
# RemoteRegistry = Remote Registry
# WMSVC        = IIS Remote Management
# SstpSvc      = SSTP VPN
