content:
# Enable PowerShell remoting
Enable-PSRemoting -Force
winrm quickconfig

# Add trusted hosts (when NOT on same domain)
Set-Item WSMan:\localhost\Client\TrustedHosts -Value "192.168.1.10" -Force
Set-Item WSMan:\localhost\Client\TrustedHosts -Value "*" -Force
Get-Item WSMan:\localhost\Client\TrustedHosts

# Interactive remote session
Enter-PSSession -ComputerName srv1.school.test -Credential school\administrator
Exit-PSSession

# Run command on remote server
Invoke-Command -ComputerName srv1.school.test `
    -ScriptBlock { Get-Service DHCP } `
    -Credential school\administrator

# Run on multiple servers at once
$servers = @("dc1", "srv1", "rras1")
Invoke-Command -ComputerName $servers `
    -ScriptBlock { Get-Service } `
    -Credential school\administrator

# Run a local script on a remote machine
Invoke-Command -ComputerName srv1.school.test `
    -FilePath C:\scripts\configure.ps1 `
    -Credential school\administrator

# Collect info from all servers
Invoke-Command -ComputerName dc1, srv1, rras1 -ScriptBlock {
    [PSCustomObject]@{
        Server  = $env:COMPUTERNAME
        OS      = (Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem).Caption
        Uptime  = (Get-Date) - (Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem).LastBootUpTime
        CPU     = (Get-Counter '\Processor(_Total)\% Processor Time').CounterSamples.CookedValue
    }
} | Format-Table

# Copy file to remote machine
$session = New-PSSession -ComputerName srv1.school.test `
    -Credential school\administrator
Copy-Item C:\setup.ps1 -Destination C:\temp\ -ToSession $session
Remove-PSSession $session

# Enable RDP
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server" `
    -Name fDenyTSConnections -Value 0
Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "Remote Desktop"

content:
# Browse certificate stores
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My         # machine personal certs
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\Root       # trusted root CAs
Get-ChildItem Cert:\CurrentUser\My          # user personal certs

# Find certificates by subject
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Subject -like "*sstp*"}
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Subject -like "*school*"}

# Find expiring certificates (within 30 days)
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | `
    Where-Object {$_.NotAfter -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(30)} | `
    Select-Object Subject, NotAfter, Thumbprint

# View certificate details
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Format-List *

# Export PFX (with private key)
$cert = Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Subject -like "*sstp*"}
$pwd  = ConvertTo-SecureString "ExportP@ss" -AsPlainText -Force
Export-PfxCertificate -Cert $cert -FilePath C:\certs\sstp.pfx -Password $pwd

# Export CER (certificate only, no private key)
Export-Certificate -Cert $cert -FilePath C:\certs\sstp.cer

# Import PFX
$pwd = ConvertTo-SecureString "ExportP@ss" -AsPlainText -Force
Import-PfxCertificate -FilePath C:\certs\sstp.pfx `
    -CertStoreLocation Cert:\LocalMachine\My -Password $pwd

# Import CER to Trusted Root
Import-Certificate -FilePath C:\certs\rootca.cer `
    -CertStoreLocation Cert:\LocalMachine\Root

# Delete a certificate
$cert = Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Subject -like "*old*"}
$cert | Remove-Item

# CA commands
certutil -ping                   # test CA connectivity
certutil -pulse                  # trigger auto-enrollment
certutil -cainfo                 # CA information
certutil -adca                   # AD CA information
certutil -verifykeys             # verify CA keys
Get-CATemplate                   # list certificate templates
Get-CAAuthorityInformationAccess # AIA info

content:
# View recent events
Get-EventLog -LogName System      -Newest 20
Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Newest 20
Get-EventLog -LogName Security    -Newest 20

# Filter by type
Get-EventLog -LogName System -EntryType Error   -Newest 20
Get-EventLog -LogName System -EntryType Warning -Newest 20

# Filter by Event ID using Get-WinEvent (more powerful)
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4624}   # successful logon
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4625}   # failed logon
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4740}   # account lockout
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4728}   # user added to group
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4720}   # user account created
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4726}   # user account deleted
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System';   Id=6006}   # event log stopped
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System';   Level=1}   # critical
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System';   Level=2}   # errors only

# Filter by time range
$start = (Get-Date).AddHours(-2)
$end   = Get-Date
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System'; StartTime=$start; EndTime=$end}

# Search message text
Get-EventLog -LogName System -Newest 100 | `
    Where-Object {$_.Message -like "*DHCP*"}

# Export to CSV
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4625} | `
    Select-Object TimeCreated, Message | `
    Export-Csv C:\failed-logons.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Get events from remote computer
Get-EventLog -LogName System -Newest 20 -ComputerName srv1
Get-WinEvent -ComputerName dc1 -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4740}

# Clear event log
Clear-EventLog -LogName Application
[System.Diagnostics.EventLog]::Delete('OldLog')

content:
# Navigate registry
Set-Location HKLM:
Get-ChildItem HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft
Get-Item HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion

# Read values
Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion"
Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion" -Name ProductName
(Get-ItemProperty "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion").CurrentBuild

# Set a value
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server" `
    -Name fDenyTSConnections -Value 0

# Create a new key
New-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp"

# Create a value in the new key
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" `
    -Name Version -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" `
    -Name Name -Value "MyApp" -PropertyType String
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" `
    -Name InstallPath -Value "C:\MyApp" -PropertyType ExpandString

# Delete a value
Remove-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" -Name Version

# Delete entire key
Remove-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" -Recurse

# Useful paths
# HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion          = OS info
# HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server      = RDP on/off
# HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\WebManagement\Server               = IIS remote mgmt
# HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SstpSvc\Parameters  = SSTP VPN
# HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer    = Explorer settings

content:
# View disks
Get-Disk
Get-Disk | Format-Table Number, FriendlyName, OperationalStatus, Size, PartitionStyle

# Initialize a new disk
Initialize-Disk -Number 1 -PartitionStyle GPT
Initialize-Disk -Number 1 -PartitionStyle MBR

# Initialize all raw (uninitialized) disks
Get-Disk | Where-Object {$_.PartitionStyle -eq 'RAW'} | `
    Initialize-Disk -PartitionStyle GPT

# Create a partition
New-Partition -DiskNumber 1 -UseMaximumSize -AssignDriveLetter
New-Partition -DiskNumber 1 -Size 5GB -DriveLetter E

# Format a volume
Format-Volume -DriveLetter E -FileSystem NTFS -NewFileSystemLabel "AppData" -Confirm:$false
Format-Volume -DriveLetter E -FileSystem ReFS -NewFileSystemLabel "StoragePool" -Confirm:$false

# View volumes
Get-Volume
Get-Volume | Select-Object DriveLetter, FileSystemLabel, FileSystem, Size, SizeRemaining

# View disk space per drive
Get-PSDrive -PSProvider FileSystem | `
    Select-Object Name, @{N='Used(GB)';E={[math]::Round($_.Used/1GB,1)}}, `
    @{N='Free(GB)';E={[math]::Round($_.Free/1GB,1)}}

# Storage Pools (Storage Spaces — Windows equivalent of LVM)
$disks = Get-PhysicalDisk -CanPool $true
New-StoragePool -FriendlyName Webservers `
    -StorageSubSystemFriendlyName "*Windows*" -PhysicalDisks $disks

New-VirtualDisk -StoragePoolFriendlyName Webservers `
    -FriendlyName vdisk1 -Size 2GB `
    -ResiliencySettingName Simple -ProvisioningType Thin

Get-VirtualDisk -FriendlyName vdisk1 | Get-Disk | `
    Initialize-Disk -PartitionStyle GPT -PassThru | `
    New-Partition -UseMaximumSize -DriveLetter E | `
    Format-Volume -FileSystem ReFS -NewFileSystemLabel Data -Confirm:$false

content:
# View processes
Get-Process
Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending | Select-Object -First 10
Get-Process | Sort-Object WorkingSet -Descending | Select-Object -First 10
Get-Process | Where-Object {$_.CPU -gt 50}
Get-Process -Name svchost

# Top processes with memory in MB
Get-Process | Sort-Object WorkingSet -Descending | `
    Select-Object -First 10 Name, Id, `
    @{N='CPU(s)';E={[math]::Round($_.CPU,1)}}, `
    @{N='RAM(MB)';E={[math]::Round($_.WorkingSet/1MB,1)}}

# Kill a process
Stop-Process -Name notepad -Force
Stop-Process -Id 4321 -Force
Get-Process -Name chrome | Stop-Process -Force

# Start a process
Start-Process notepad
Start-Process powershell -Verb RunAs   # as admin

# Performance counters (live sampling)
Get-Counter -Counter "\Processor(_Total)\% Processor Time"
Get-Counter -Counter "\Memory\Available MBytes"
Get-Counter -Counter "\PhysicalDisk(_Total)\Disk Reads/sec"
Get-Counter -Counter "\Network Interface(*)\Bytes Total/sec"

# Sample 5 times every 2 seconds
Get-Counter -Counter "\Processor(_Total)\% Processor Time" `
    -SampleInterval 2 -MaxSamples 5

# Uptime
(Get-Date) - (Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem).LastBootUpTime
(Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem).LastBootUpTime

# System info via WMI
Get-WmiObject Win32_OperatingSystem | `
    Select-Object @{N='FreeRAM_MB';E={[math]::Round($_.FreePhysicalMemory/1024,1)}}, `
    @{N='TotalRAM_MB';E={[math]::Round($_.TotalVisibleMemorySize/1024,1)}}

content:
# View all scheduled tasks
Get-ScheduledTask
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object {$_.State -eq 'Running'}
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object {$_.TaskPath -eq '\'}

# View task details
Get-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup" | Format-List *
Get-ScheduledTaskInfo -TaskName "NightlyBackup"   # last run, next run

# Create a daily task at 3am
$action  = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "powershell.exe" `
    -Argument "-NonInteractive -File C:\scripts\backup.ps1"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Daily -At 3am
$settings = New-ScheduledTaskSettingsSet -RunOnlyIfNetworkAvailable
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup" `
    -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -Settings $settings `
    -RunLevel Highest -User "SYSTEM"

# Create a task that runs at startup
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -AtStartup
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "StartupScript" `
    -Action (New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "powershell.exe" -Argument "C:\scripts\startup.ps1") `
    -Trigger $trigger -RunLevel Highest -User "SYSTEM"

# Create a task that runs on logon
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -AtLogOn
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "LogonScript" `
    -Action (New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "powershell.exe" -Argument "C:\scripts\logon.ps1") `
    -Trigger $trigger

# Run a task immediately
Start-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup"

# Enable / disable task
Enable-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup"
Disable-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup"

# Remove a task
Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup" -Confirm:$false

content:
# Check all DCs for replication errors
$dcs = (Get-ADDomainController -Filter *).Name
foreach ($dc in $dcs) {
    Write-Host "=== $dc ==="
    repadmin /showrepl $dc
}

# List all AD users with their last logon date
Get-ADUser -Filter * -Properties LastLogonDate | `
    Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, LastLogonDate, Enabled | `
    Sort-Object LastLogonDate -Descending | `
    Export-Csv C:\user-logons.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Find users in a specific OU and add them to a group
Get-ADUser -Filter * -SearchBase "OU=docenten,OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test" | `
    ForEach-Object { Add-ADGroupMember -Identity "Docenten_Global" -Members $_ }

# Check if a list of ports is open on a server
$server = "dc1.school.test"
$ports  = @(53, 80, 135, 389, 443, 3389)
foreach ($port in $ports) {
    $result = Test-NetConnection $server -Port $port -WarningAction SilentlyContinue
    "Port $port : $($result.TcpTestSucceeded)"
}

# Get all DHCP leases across all scopes
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope | ForEach-Object {
    Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId $_.ScopeId
} | Select-Object IPAddress, HostName, ClientId, AddressState | `
    Export-Csv C:\all-leases.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Get all DNS A records from a zone
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType A | `
    Select-Object HostName, `
    @{N='IPAddress';E={$_.RecordData.IPv4Address}} | `
    Sort-Object HostName

# Show last 20 failed logon attempts
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4625} -MaxEvents 20 | `
    Select-Object TimeCreated, `
    @{N='User';E={$_.Properties[5].Value}}, `
    @{N='Source IP';E={$_.Properties[19].Value}}

# Restart a service on all servers if stopped
$servers = @("dc1", "srv1")
foreach ($s in $servers) {
    $svc = Get-Service -Name DNS -ComputerName $s
    if ($svc.Status -ne 'Running') {
        Start-Service -InputObject $svc
        Write-Host "Started DNS on $s"
    }
}

content:
/24 = 256 addresses, 254 usable hosts
/25 = 128 addresses,  126 usable hosts
/26 =  64 addresses,   62 usable hosts
/27 =  32 addresses,   30 usable hosts
/28 =  16 addresses,   14 usable hosts
/29 =   8 addresses,    6 usable hosts
/30 =   4 addresses,    2 usable hosts
/31 =   2 addresses,    2 usable hosts (point-to-point, RFC 3021)
/32 =   1 address,      1 usable host  (host route)

Formula: Usable hosts = 2^(32 - prefix) - 2
Exception: /31 and /32 do not subtract 2

content:
1. Write the IP address in decimal: 192.168.10.75
2. Write the subnet mask:          255.255.255.192  (/26)
3. Perform bitwise AND on each octet:
   192 AND 255 = 192
   168 AND 255 = 168
    10 AND 255 = 10
    75 AND 192 = 64
4. Network address: 192.168.10.64/26

content:
1. Find the network address (see above):  192.168.10.64
2. Invert the subnet mask (wildcard):     0.0.0.63
3. Bitwise OR network address with wildcard:
   192 OR 0  = 192
   168 OR 0  = 168
    10 OR 0  = 10
    64 OR 63 = 127
4. Broadcast address: 192.168.10.127
5. Usable range: 192.168.10.65 – 192.168.10.126

content:
Number of subnets = 2^s
where s = number of subnet bits borrowed from host portion

Example: Splitting /24 into /27
  s = 27 - 24 = 3 subnet bits borrowed
  2^3 = 8 subnets

Example: Splitting /16 into /20
  s = 20 - 16 = 4 subnet bits borrowed
  2^4 = 16 subnets

Example: Splitting /8 into /24
  s = 24 - 8 = 16 subnet bits borrowed
  2^16 = 65,536 subnets

content:
IPv6 address = 128 bits, written as 8 groups of 4 hex digits
Example: 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334

Typical allocation hierarchy:
  /32  — ISP allocation
  /48  — Site/organization allocation (from ISP)
  /56  — Home user allocation (some ISPs)
  /64  — Single subnet (standard LAN prefix)
  /128 — Single host (loopback, anycast)

A /48 site prefix provides:
  2^(64-48) = 2^16 = 65,536 possible /64 subnets

A /64 subnet provides:
  2^64 ≈ 18.4 quintillion interface addresses

content:
A subnet (short for subnetwork) is a logically divided segment of a larger IP network. Subnetting allows a single network address block to be split into multiple smaller networks.

Why we subnet:
  - Reduce broadcast traffic (each subnet = its own broadcast domain)
  - Improve security (isolate departments, VLANs, or zones)
  - Efficient use of IP address space (allocate only what is needed)
  - Easier network management and troubleshooting
  - Required for routing between network segments

content:
An IPv4 address is 32 bits long, written as four 8-bit octets in dotted decimal notation.

Example: 192.168.10.75

In binary:
  192 = 11000000
  168 = 10101000
   10 = 00001010
   75 = 01001011

Full binary: 11000000.10101000.00001010.01001011

Every IP address has two parts:
  1. Network portion  — identifies which network the host belongs to
  2. Host portion     — identifies the specific device on that network

The subnet mask determines the boundary between these two parts.

content:
A subnet mask is a 32-bit number that marks which bits of an IP address belong to the network and which belong to the host.

  Bit = 1 → Network bit
  Bit = 0 → Host bit

Example — 255.255.255.0 in binary:
  11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000
  |--- 24 network bits ---|-- 8 host bits --|

This means:
  - The first 24 bits identify the network
  - The last 8 bits identify hosts on that network
  - Written in CIDR notation as /24

CIDR notation is just the count of consecutive 1-bits in the mask:
  255.255.255.0   = /24  (twenty-four 1-bits)
  255.255.255.128 = /25  (twenty-five 1-bits)
  255.255.255.192 = /26  (twenty-six 1-bits)

content:
Network Address
  The first address in a subnet. All host bits are 0.
  Cannot be assigned to a device. Identifies the subnet itself.
  Example: 192.168.1.0/24 — network address is 192.168.1.0

Broadcast Address
  The last address in a subnet. All host bits are 1.
  Cannot be assigned to a device. Sends data to all hosts in the subnet.
  Example: 192.168.1.0/24 — broadcast address is 192.168.1.255

Usable Host Range
  All addresses between the network address and the broadcast address.
  Example: 192.168.1.0/24 — usable hosts are 192.168.1.1 through 192.168.1.254

Default Gateway
  The router interface on the subnet. Usually assigned the first or last usable host address.

Prefix Length
  The number after the slash in CIDR notation (e.g. /24).
  Indicates how many bits are used for the network portion.

content:
Each octet in an IP address or subnet mask is 8 bits. The bit values are:

  Bit position: 8    7    6    5    4    3    2    1
  Bit value:   128   64   32   16    8    4    2    1

To convert decimal to binary:
  Take the number and subtract the largest power of 2 that fits, mark that bit as 1, repeat.

  Example: 192
  192 - 128 = 64  → bit 8 = 1
   64 -  64 =  0  → bit 7 = 1
   Remaining bits = 0
  Result: 11000000

  Example: 168
  168 - 128 = 40  → bit 8 = 1
   40 -  32 =  8  → bit 6 = 1  (64 doesn't fit)
    8 -   8 =  0  → bit 4 = 1
  Result: 10101000

To convert binary to decimal:
  Add up the values of all positions where the bit is 1.
  Example: 11100000 = 128 + 64 + 32 = 224

content:
Given: IP address 192.168.5.130 with subnet mask 255.255.255.192 (/26)

1. Convert the subnet mask to binary
   255.255.255.192 = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000
   Count the 1s: 26 → this is a /26

2. Determine the block size
   Block size = 256 - interesting octet of mask
   Interesting octet = 192 (the octet that is not 0 or 255)
   Block size = 256 - 192 = 64

3. List the subnet boundaries (multiples of the block size)
   0, 64, 128, 192
   The subnets are: .0/26, .64/26, .128/26, .192/26

4. Find which subnet the IP falls in
   IP last octet = 130
   130 is between 128 and 192 → belongs to the .128/26 subnet

5. Calculate network address
   192.168.5.128

6. Calculate broadcast address
   Next subnet boundary - 1 = 192 - 1 = 191
   Broadcast: 192.168.5.191

7. Calculate usable host range
   First host: 192.168.5.129  (network + 1)
   Last host:  192.168.5.190  (broadcast - 1)

8. Count usable hosts
   2^(32-26) - 2 = 2^6 - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 usable hosts

content:
The most accurate way to find the network address is to AND the IP with the subnet mask.
AND rule: 1 AND 1 = 1, anything AND 0 = 0

Example: IP 10.20.35.200 with mask 255.255.240.0 (/20)

1. Write IP in binary:
   10  = 00001010
   20  = 00010100
   35  = 00100011
   200 = 11001000

2. Write mask in binary:
   255 = 11111111
   255 = 11111111
   240 = 11110000
     0 = 00000000

3. AND each octet:
   00001010 AND 11111111 = 00001010 = 10
   00010100 AND 11111111 = 00010100 = 20
   00100011 AND 11110000 = 00100000 = 32
   11001000 AND 00000000 = 00000000 = 0

4. Network address: 10.20.32.0/20

5. Broadcast: set all host bits to 1
   Host bits are the last 12 bits (32 - 20 = 12)
   10.20.32.0 → last 12 bits all 1 = 10.20.47.255

6. Usable range: 10.20.32.1 – 10.20.47.254

content:
Formula: Usable hosts = 2^h - 2
where h = number of host bits = 32 - prefix length

We subtract 2 to exclude:
  - The network address (all host bits = 0)
  - The broadcast address (all host bits = 1)

Examples:
  /24 → h = 32 - 24 = 8 → 2^8 - 2 = 256 - 2 = 254 hosts
  /25 → h = 32 - 25 = 7 → 2^7 - 2 = 128 - 2 = 126 hosts
  /26 → h = 32 - 26 = 6 → 2^6 - 2 = 64 - 2  = 62 hosts
  /27 → h = 32 - 27 = 5 → 2^5 - 2 = 32 - 2  = 30 hosts
  /28 → h = 32 - 28 = 4 → 2^4 - 2 = 16 - 2  = 14 hosts
  /29 → h = 32 - 29 = 3 → 2^3 - 2 = 8 - 2   = 6 hosts
  /30 → h = 32 - 30 = 2 → 2^2 - 2 = 4 - 2   = 2 hosts

Special cases:
  /31 → 2 addresses, no network/broadcast — used for point-to-point (RFC 3021)
  /32 → single host route — used for loopback or specific host entries

content:
When splitting a network into subnets:

Formula: Number of subnets = 2^s
where s = number of bits borrowed from the host portion

Bits borrowed = new prefix - original prefix

Examples:
  /24 split into /26 → s = 26 - 24 = 2 bits → 2^2 = 4 subnets
  /24 split into /27 → s = 27 - 24 = 3 bits → 2^3 = 8 subnets
  /24 split into /28 → s = 28 - 24 = 4 bits → 2^4 = 16 subnets
  /16 split into /24 → s = 24 - 16 = 8 bits → 2^8 = 256 subnets
  /16 split into /20 → s = 20 - 16 = 4 bits → 2^4 = 16 subnets
  /8  split into /24 → s = 24 - 8  = 16 bits → 2^16 = 65,536 subnets

Conservation check:
  Total addresses = subnets × addresses per subnet
  Example: /24 (256 addresses) → 4 × /26 (64 each) = 256 ✓

subsection: Finding the Right Prefix for a Required Number of Hosts
content:
Goal: You need to fit N hosts into a subnet. Find the smallest prefix.

1. Add 2 to the required hosts (for network and broadcast addresses)
   Example: Need 50 hosts → 50 + 2 = 52 addresses needed

2. Find the smallest power of 2 that is >= 52
   2^5 = 32  (too small)
   2^6 = 64  (fits — 64 >= 52) ✓

3. The number of host bits = 6
   Prefix = 32 - 6 = /26

4. Verify: /26 gives 2^6 - 2 = 62 usable hosts >= 50 ✓

More examples:
  Need 100 hosts → 102 needed → 2^7 = 128 → /25 (126 usable) ✓
  Need 200 hosts → 202 needed → 2^8 = 256 → /24 (254 usable) ✓
  Need 10 hosts  → 12 needed  → 2^4 = 16  → /28 (14 usable)  ✓
  Need 2 hosts   → 4 needed   → 2^2 = 4   → /30 (2 usable)   ✓

subsection: Finding the Right Prefix for a Required Number of Subnets
content:
Goal: You need at least N subnets from a given network. Find the new prefix.

1. Find the smallest power of 2 that is >= N
   Example: Need 6 subnets from a /24
   2^2 = 4  (too few)
   2^3 = 8  (enough) ✓

2. Bits to borrow = 3
   New prefix = 24 + 3 = /27

3. Verify: /27 gives 8 subnets, each with 30 usable hosts ✓

More examples:
  Need 10 subnets from /24 → 2^4 = 16 → borrow 4 bits → /28
  Need 3 subnets from /16  → 2^2 = 4  → borrow 2 bits → /18
  Need 30 subnets from /16 → 2^5 = 32 → borrow 5 bits → /21
  Need 100 subnets from /8 → 2^7 = 128→ borrow 7 bits → /15

content:
Block size (also called the magic number or increment) is the size of each subnet — how far apart the network addresses are.

Formula: Block size = 256 - value of the interesting octet in the subnet mask

The 'interesting octet' is the octet that is neither 255 nor 0 — it is the octet where the network/host boundary falls.

Examples:
  /25 → mask = 255.255.255.128 → interesting octet = 128 → block = 256 - 128 = 128
  /26 → mask = 255.255.255.192 → interesting octet = 192 → block = 256 - 192 = 64
  /27 → mask = 255.255.255.224 → interesting octet = 224 → block = 256 - 224 = 32
  /28 → mask = 255.255.255.240 → interesting octet = 240 → block = 256 - 240 = 16
  /29 → mask = 255.255.255.248 → interesting octet = 248 → block = 256 - 248 = 8
  /30 → mask = 255.255.255.252 → interesting octet = 252 → block = 256 - 252 = 4
  /20 → mask = 255.255.240.0   → interesting octet = 240 → block = 256 - 240 = 16 (in 3rd octet)
  /22 → mask = 255.255.252.0   → interesting octet = 252 → block = 256 - 252 = 4  (in 3rd octet)

subsection: Using Block Size to Find Subnets Quickly
content:
Given: 172.16.45.200/22  — what subnet is this in?

1. Identify the mask for /22
   /22 = 255.255.252.0

2. Identify the interesting octet
   252 is in the 3rd octet → work in the 3rd octet

3. Calculate block size
   256 - 252 = 4

4. List multiples of 4 in the 3rd octet until you pass the IP's 3rd octet
   IP 3rd octet = 45
   Multiples of 4: 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40, 44, 48...
   45 falls between 44 and 48

5. Network address = 172.16.44.0/22

6. Broadcast = next boundary minus 1 = 172.16.48.0 - 1 = 172.16.47.255

7. Usable range: 172.16.44.1 – 172.16.47.254

8. Total usable hosts: 2^(32-22) - 2 = 2^10 - 2 = 1,022

content:
CIDR stands for Classless Inter-Domain Routing.
The number after the slash represents the prefix length — the count of consecutive 1-bits in the subnet mask.

Example: 192.168.1.0/24
  /24 means the first 24 bits are the network portion.
  Subnet mask in binary: 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000
  Count of 1s = 24 ✓

Example: 10.0.0.0/8
  /8 means the first 8 bits are the network portion.
  Subnet mask: 11111111.00000000.00000000.00000000 = 255.0.0.0

Example: 172.16.0.0/12
  /12 means the first 12 bits are the network portion.
  Subnet mask: 11111111.11110000.00000000.00000000 = 255.240.0.0

CIDR replaced the old Class A/B/C system, which had fixed boundaries at /8, /16, and /24. CIDR allows any prefix length, enabling efficient allocation.

content:
Classful addressing (obsolete but useful background knowledge):

  Class A: 1.0.0.0   – 126.0.0.0   | Default mask /8  | 16M hosts per network
  Class B: 128.0.0.0 – 191.255.0.0 | Default mask /16 | 65K hosts per network
  Class C: 192.0.0.0 – 223.255.255.0| Default mask /24 | 254 hosts per network
  Class D: 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.0| Multicast (no mask)
  Class E: 240.0.0.0 – 255.255.255.0| Reserved/Experimental

Problems with classful addressing:
  - A Class B gave 65,534 hosts even if you only needed 1,000 — wasteful
  - A Class C only gave 254 hosts — too small for many organizations
  - No flexibility in allocation

CIDR solves this by allowing any prefix length:
  Need 1,000 hosts? Use a /22 (1,022 usable)
  Need 50 hosts? Use a /26 (62 usable)
  This prevents waste and extends the life of IPv4 addresses.

content:
Problem: Given 192.168.1.200/26, find the network address, broadcast, and host range.

Step 1: Identify the mask
  /26 = 255.255.255.192, block size = 64

Step 2: Find subnet boundaries in the last octet
  Multiples of 64: 0, 64, 128, 192

Step 3: 200 falls between 192 and 256
  Network address: 192.168.1.192

Step 4: Broadcast = next boundary - 1
  Next boundary = 256 (wraps to end of octet)
  Broadcast: 192.168.1.255

Step 5: Host range
  First host: 192.168.1.193
  Last host:  192.168.1.254
  Usable hosts: 62

content:
Problem: Given 10.0.75.5/20, find the network address, broadcast, and host range.

Step 1: Identify the mask
  /20 = 255.255.240.0
  Interesting octet is the 3rd (240), block size = 256 - 240 = 16

Step 2: Find subnet boundaries in the 3rd octet
  Multiples of 16: 0, 16, 32, 48, 64, 80...
  3rd octet of IP = 75
  75 falls between 64 and 80

Step 3: Network address
  10.0.64.0/20

Step 4: Broadcast
  Next boundary = 10.0.80.0
  Broadcast = 10.0.79.255

Step 5: Host range
  First host: 10.0.64.1
  Last host:  10.0.79.254
  Usable hosts: 2^12 - 2 = 4,094

content:
Problem: You have 192.168.100.0/24. Create subnets for:
  - Engineering: 60 hosts
  - HR: 28 hosts
  - Management: 10 hosts

1. Find the smallest subnet for each department
   Engineering (60 hosts): 2^6 = 64 → /26 (62 usable) ✓
   HR (28 hosts):          2^5 = 32 → /27 (30 usable) ✓
   Management (10 hosts):  2^4 = 16 → /28 (14 usable) ✓

2. Allocate largest subnet first (VLSM best practice)
   Engineering: 192.168.100.0/26
     Range: .1 – .62,  Broadcast: .63

3. Next subnet starts at .64
   HR: 192.168.100.64/27
     Range: .65 – .94,  Broadcast: .95

4. Next subnet starts at .96
   Management: 192.168.100.96/28
     Range: .97 – .110,  Broadcast: .111

5. Remaining space: 192.168.100.112 – 192.168.100.255 (available for future use)

Summary:
   Engineering: 192.168.100.0/26  (62 hosts)
   HR:          192.168.100.64/27 (30 hosts)
   Management:  192.168.100.96/28 (14 hosts)

content:
Problem: Are 192.168.50.65 and 192.168.50.100 in the same /26 subnet?

1. Mask for /26 = 255.255.255.192, block size = 64
   Subnet boundaries in last octet: 0, 64, 128, 192

2. Find subnet for 192.168.50.65
   65 falls between 64 and 128 → subnet 192.168.50.64/26

3. Find subnet for 192.168.50.100
   100 falls between 64 and 128 → subnet 192.168.50.64/26

4. Both IPs are in 192.168.50.64/26 → YES, same subnet ✓

Bonus: Are 192.168.50.65 and 192.168.50.130 in the same /26?
   65  → subnet .64/26
   130 → subnet .128/26
   Different subnets — they need a router to communicate.

content:
Problem: Summarize these four subnets into one route:
  10.1.4.0/24
  10.1.5.0/24
  10.1.6.0/24
  10.1.7.0/24

1. Write the network addresses in binary (focus on differing octets)
  4 = 00000100
  5 = 00000101
  6 = 00000110
  7 = 00000111

2. Find the common prefix (bits that are the same across all)
  00000100
  00000101
  00000110
  00000111
  Common: 000001 (first 6 bits match)

3. The existing prefix is /24. The 3rd octet has 6 common bits.
  Common bits in 3rd octet = 6
  Summary prefix = 16 (first two octets) + 6 = /22

4. Summary route: 10.1.4.0/22
  This covers 10.1.4.0 through 10.1.7.255 ✓

Verification: 2^(24-22) = 4 original /24 subnets summarized ✓

content:
1. Block size shortcut
   The block size equals the subnet's total address count.
   /26 has 64 addresses → subnets start at 0, 64, 128, 192.

2. Broadcast is always block_start + block_size - 1
   /26 starting at .64: broadcast = 64 + 64 - 1 = .127

3. Last usable host = broadcast - 1
   First usable host = network + 1

4. To check if an IP is valid for a subnet:
   IP AND mask = network address of that subnet?
   If yes → IP is in that subnet.

5. Powers of 2 in increasing prefix order:
   /32 /31 /30 /29 /28 /27 /26 /25 /24
    1    2   4   8   16   32   64  128  256  ← block sizes / total addresses

6. The prefix and block size always multiply to 256 in the interesting octet:
   192 × ? → 256 - 192 = 64 (/26)
   224 × ? → 256 - 224 = 32 (/27)
   240 × ? → 256 - 240 = 16 (/28)

content:
1. Assigning the network address to a host
   192.168.1.0/24 — the .0 is the network address, NOT usable.

2. Assigning the broadcast address to a host
   192.168.1.255/24 — the .255 is the broadcast, NOT usable.

3. Overlapping subnets
   192.168.1.0/25 and 192.168.1.64/25 are NOT valid together.
   The second /25 should start at .128, not .64.
   Subnets must not overlap — each block must start at the correct boundary.

4. Off-boundary subnets
   192.168.1.20/27 is NOT valid.
   /27 has a block size of 32. Valid starts: 0, 32, 64, 96...
   A subnet cannot start mid-block.

5. Confusing subnet mask with wildcard mask
   Subnet mask /26 = 255.255.255.192
   Wildcard mask    = 0.0.0.63  (used in ACLs — this is the inverse)

6. Wrong octet for the block size
   For /20 (255.255.240.0), the block size applies to the 3RD octet, not the 4th.
   Subnets are .0.0, .16.0, .32.0, .48.0 — incrementing in the third octet.

content:
For any IP/prefix, answer the 5 key questions in under 30 seconds:

1. What is the block size?
   256 - interesting octet of mask

2. What subnet is the IP in?
   Find the largest multiple of the block size that is <= the IP's interesting octet

3. What is the network address?
   Replace interesting octet with that multiple; remaining host octets = 0

4. What is the broadcast address?
   Network address + block size - 1 in the interesting octet; remaining octets = 255

5. What is the host range?
   First host = network address + 1
   Last host  = broadcast address - 1

Example in action — 172.31.200.55/21:
  1. /21 = 255.255.248.0, block = 256 - 248 = 8 (3rd octet)
  2. 3rd octet = 200, largest multiple of 8 <= 200 is 200
  3. Network:   172.31.200.0
  4. Broadcast: 172.31.207.255  (200 + 8 - 1 = 207)
  5. Hosts:     172.31.200.1 – 172.31.207.254

# Start PowerShell from CMD
powershell
powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\scripts\setup.ps1

# Get help
Get-Help Get-NetIPAddress
Get-Help Get-NetIPAddress -Examples
Get-Help Get-NetIPAddress -Full
Update-Help                               # download latest help files

# Discover commands
Get-Command *dhcp*
Get-Command *dns*
Get-Command *gpo*
Get-Command -Noun ADUser
Get-Command -Module ActiveDirectory

# Object pipeline
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}
Get-Service | Sort-Object DisplayName | Select-Object Name, Status
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Stopped'} | Start-Service

# Output
Get-NetIPAddress | Out-File C:\ipinfo.txt
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Export-Csv C:\users.csv -NoTypeInformation
Get-Service | ConvertTo-Json | Out-File C:\services.json

# Execution policy
Get-ExecutionPolicy
Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned
Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process   # only for current session

# Variables
$hostname = $env:COMPUTERNAME
$ip = (Get-NetIPAddress -AddressFamily IPv4 -InterfaceAlias Ethernet0).IPAddress
Write-Host "This server is: $hostname"

# String formatting
"IP address is: {0}" -f $ip

# Arrays and loops
$servers = @("dc1", "srv1", "rras1")
foreach ($s in $servers) { ping $s -n 1 }

# Step 1: Find interface index

# Example: Name=Ethernet0, InterfaceIndex=6

# Step 2: Remove existing DHCP IP (if set)

# Step 3: Remove existing default gateway
Remove-NetRoute -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet0" -DestinationPrefix "0.0.0.0/0" -Confirm:$false

# Step 4: Set static IP, prefix length (subnet), and gateway
New-NetIPAddress -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet0" -IPAddress 172.16.1.1 -PrefixLength 24 -DefaultGateway 172.16.1.251

# Step 5: Set DNS servers
Set-DnsClientServerAddress -InterfaceAlias "Ethernet0" -ServerAddresses 172.16.1.1, 8.8.8.8

# Verify everything is set
Get-NetIPConfiguration

# Example: set IP via netsh (older method, still works)
netsh interface ip set address "Ethernet0" static 172.16.1.1 255.255.255.0 172.16.1.251
netsh interface ip set dns "Ethernet0" static 172.16.1.1 primary
netsh interface ip add dns "Ethernet0" 8.8.8.8 index=2

content:
# Basic ping
ping 8.8.8.8                              # 4 packets to Google DNS
ping -t 8.8.8.8                           # continuous ping
ping -n 10 8.8.8.8                        # exactly 10 packets
ping -l 1400 8.8.8.8                      # large packet (test MTU / fragmentation)
ping -a 172.16.1.1                        # resolve hostname of IP

# Trace route
tracert 8.8.8.8                           # hop-by-hop path to destination
tracert -d 8.8.8.8                        # skip DNS resolution (faster)
pathping 8.8.8.8                          # combines tracert + latency stats

# DNS lookup
nslookup dc1.school.test                  # forward lookup
nslookup 172.16.1.1                       # reverse lookup
nslookup -type=MX school.test            # query MX record
nslookup -type=NS school.test            # query NS records

# PowerShell equivalents
Test-NetConnection 8.8.8.8               # basic ICMP test
Test-NetConnection dc1.school.test -Port 389    # test LDAP port
Test-NetConnection dc1.school.test -Port 443    # test HTTPS port
Test-NetConnection dc1.school.test -Port 3389   # test RDP
Resolve-DnsName dc1.school.test          # DNS forward lookup
Resolve-DnsName school.test -Type MX    # MX record
Resolve-DnsName 172.16.1.1               # reverse lookup

# View connections and listening ports
netstat -an                  # all connections and listening ports
netstat -ano                 # same + PID for each entry
netstat -b                   # includes process name (requires admin)
netstat -r                   # routing table
netstat -s                   # per-protocol statistics

# PowerShell
Get-NetTCPConnection                                 # all TCP connections
Get-NetTCPConnection -State Listen                   # listening ports only
Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 443                  # check specific port
Get-NetTCPConnection | Select-Object LocalAddress, LocalPort, RemoteAddress, State | Sort-Object LocalPort

# View and modify routing table
route print                                         # full routing table
route add 10.0.0.0 mask 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.1       # add static route
route delete 10.0.0.0                               # remove static route
route change 10.0.0.0 mask 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.2    # change existing route

# Persistent route (survives reboot)
route add 10.0.0.0 mask 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.1 -p

# PowerShell route management
Get-NetRoute
New-NetRoute -DestinationPrefix "10.0.0.0/8" -NextHop 192.168.1.1 -InterfaceAlias Ethernet0
Remove-NetRoute -DestinationPrefix "10.0.0.0/8" -Confirm:$false

content:
Import-Module ActiveDirectory

# Create AD user with minimum required fields
New-ADUser -Name "Jan Jansen" -SamAccountName jjansen -Enabled $true -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd1" -AsPlainText -Force)

# Create AD user in specific OU with more details
New-ADUser `
  -Name "Piet Hein" `
  -GivenName Piet `
  -Surname Hein `
  -SamAccountName phein `
  -UserPrincipalName phein@school.test `
  -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ssw0rd1" -AsPlainText -Force) `
  -Enabled $true `
  -Path "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test" `
  -Description "Employee"

# View user
Get-ADUser jjansen
Get-ADUser jjansen -Properties *
Get-ADUser -Filter {Name -like "Jan*"}
Get-ADUser -Filter {Enabled -eq $false}   # find all disabled accounts
Get-ADUser -Filter * | Select-Object Name, SamAccountName, Enabled

# Reset password
Set-ADAccountPassword -Identity jjansen -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "NewP@ss1" -AsPlainText -Force) -Reset

# Enable / disable
Enable-ADAccount -Identity jjansen
Disable-ADAccount -Identity jjansen

# Unlock locked account
Unlock-ADAccount -Identity jjansen

# Set password never expires
Set-ADUser -Identity jjansen -PasswordNeverExpires $true

# Move user to different OU
Get-ADUser jjansen | Move-ADObject -TargetPath "OU=admin,DC=school,DC=test"

# Delete user
Remove-ADUser -Identity jjansen -Confirm:$false

# Find all users whose password is expiring in 7 days
$expire = (Get-Date).AddDays(7)
Search-ADAccount -AccountExpiring -DateTime $expire | Select-Object Name, AccountExpirationDate

# CSV format example (medewerkers.csv):
# Name,SamAccountName,Password,OU
# Jan Jansen,jjansen,P@ssw0rd1,"OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
# Piet Hein,phein,P@ssw0rd1,"OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"

# Import from CSV
$users = Import-Csv C:\users.csv
foreach ($user in $users) {
    New-ADUser -Name $user.Name `
        -SamAccountName $user.SamAccountName `
        -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString $user.Password -AsPlainText -Force) `
        -Enabled $true `
        -Path $user.OU
}

# Alternative: csvde command (AD LDIF import tool)
csvde -i -v -k -f C:\medewerkers.csv
# -i = import mode  -v = verbose  -k = skip errors  -f = file path

# After import, force replication
repadmin /syncall

New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name slb      -Path "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name admin    -Path "OU=medewerkers,DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name gasten   -Path "DC=school,DC=test"
New-ADOrganizationalUnit -Name kiosk-computers -Path "DC=school,DC=test"

# List all OUs
Get-ADOrganizationalUnit -Filter * | Select-Object Name, DistinguishedName

# Move computer to OU
Get-ADComputer PC2 | Move-ADObject -TargetPath "OU=kiosk-computers,DC=school,DC=test"

# Move user to OU
Get-ADUser kioskuser | Move-ADObject -TargetPath "OU=gasten,DC=school,DC=test"

# Remove OU (must disable accidental deletion protection first)
Set-ADOrganizationalUnit -Identity "OU=gasten,DC=school,DC=test" -ProtectedFromAccidentalDeletion $false
Remove-ADOrganizationalUnit -Identity "OU=gasten,DC=school,DC=test" -Recursive -Confirm:$false

# View only installed
Get-WindowsFeature | Where-Object {$_.Installed -eq $true}

Install-WindowsFeature -Name DNS -IncludeManagementTools

Install-WindowsFeature -Name Web-Server, Web-Windows-Auth, Web-Ftp-Server
Install-WindowsFeature -Name File-Services -IncludeManagementTools

# Install with automatic reboot if required
Install-WindowsFeature -Name AD-Domain-Services -IncludeManagementTools -Restart

# Uninstall a role
Remove-WindowsFeature -Name DHCP

# View result of last install

$result.RestartNeeded

content:
# List all services
net start                                            # running services only
sc query                                             # all services with state
Get-Service                                          # all services
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Stopped'}

# View specific service
sc query dhcpserver
Get-Service -Name dhcpserver
Get-Service dhcpserver | Format-List *               # verbose

# Start, stop, restart
net start dhcpserver
net stop dhcpserver
Start-Service dhcpserver
Stop-Service dhcpserver
Restart-Service dhcpserver
Restart-Service dhcpserver -Force                    # force even if dependent services

# Set startup type
Set-Service dhcpserver -StartupType Automatic
Set-Service dhcpserver -StartupType Manual
Set-Service dhcpserver -StartupType Disabled
sc config dhcpserver start= auto                     # cmd equivalent

# Common service names
# dhcpserver     = DHCP Server
# dns            = DNS Server
# netlogon       = Netlogon (AD auth)
# w32tm          = Windows Time
# RemoteRegistry = Remote Registry
# WinRM          = Windows Remote Management
# wuauserv       = Windows Update
# spooler        = Print Spooler
# smb            = Server (SMB file sharing)
# rpcss          = Remote Procedure Call
# SstpSvc        = SSTP VPN service
# WMSVC          = IIS Remote Management

content:
# View firewall status on all profiles
netsh advfirewall show allprofiles
Get-NetFirewallProfile | Select-Object Name, Enabled, DefaultInboundAction

# Enable / disable firewall
netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state on
netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state off
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain,Public,Private -Enabled True
Set-NetFirewallProfile -Profile Domain,Public,Private -Enabled False

# Enable a predefined rule group
netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="File and Printer Sharing" new enable=Yes
netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="Remote Desktop" new enable=Yes
Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "Remote Desktop"
Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "File and Printer Sharing"

# Allow a specific TCP port
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="HTTP" protocol=TCP dir=in localport=80 action=allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTP" -Direction Inbound -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 80 -Action Allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTPS" -Direction Inbound -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 443 -Action Allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "SMTP" -Direction Inbound -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 25 -Action Allow

# Allow ICMP (ping)
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="Allow ICMPv4" protocol=icmpv4:8,any dir=in action=allow
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow ICMPv4" -Direction Inbound -Protocol ICMPv4 -IcmpType 8 -Action Allow

# Allow application through firewall
netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name="MyApp" dir=in action=allow program="C:\myapp.exe"
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "MyApp" -Direction Inbound -Program "C:\myapp.exe" -Action Allow

# View all firewall rules
Get-NetFirewallRule | Select-Object DisplayName, Direction, Action, Enabled
Get-NetFirewallRule | Where-Object {$_.Enabled -eq $true -and $_.Direction -eq "Inbound"} | Select-Object DisplayName

# Delete a rule
netsh advfirewall firewall delete rule name="HTTP"
Remove-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "HTTP"

# Export and import rules
netsh advfirewall export C:\fw-rules.wfw
netsh advfirewall import C:\fw-rules.wfw

# View all shares including hidden (admin shares)
Get-SmbShare
net share

# Create a basic share
New-SmbShare -Name Data -Path C:\data

# Create share with specific permissions
New-SmbShare -Name Recepten -Path C:\data\recepten `
    -FullAccess "school\Huisartsen_DL" `
    -ReadAccess "school\Apothekers_DL" `
    -NoAccess Everyone

# View share permissions
Get-SmbShareAccess -Name Data

# Add share permission
Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name Data -AccountName "school\Domain Users" -AccessRight Read -Force
Grant-SmbShareAccess -Name Data -AccountName "school\Admins_DL" -AccessRight Full -Force

# Change share permission
Set-SmbPathAcl -ShareName Data

# Remove share permission
Revoke-SmbShareAccess -Name Data -AccountName "school\Domain Users" -Force

# Remove a share (does not delete the folder)
Remove-SmbShare -Name Data -Force

# View connected users and open files
Get-SmbSession
Get-SmbOpenFile

# Close all sessions (disconnect users)
Get-SmbSession | Close-SmbSession -Force

# Map a drive (useful in scripts / logon)
net use Z: \\dc1\medewerkers /persistent:yes
New-PSDrive -Name Z -PSProvider FileSystem -Root \\dc1\medewerkers -Persist

content:
# View NTFS ACL on a folder
Get-Acl C:\data\recepten | Format-List
(Get-Acl C:\data\recepten).Access | Format-Table IdentityReference, FileSystemRights, AccessControlType

# Grant NTFS permission via PowerShell
$acl  = Get-Acl C:\data\recepten
$rule = New-Object System.Security.AccessControl.FileSystemAccessRule(
    "school\Huisartsen_DL", "FullControl", "ContainerInherit,ObjectInherit", "None", "Allow"
)
$acl.AddAccessRule($rule)
Set-Acl C:\data\recepten $acl

# Break inheritance (stop inheriting from parent)
$acl = Get-Acl C:\data\recepten
$acl.SetAccessRuleProtection($true, $false)   # $true=block, $false=don't copy inherited rules
Set-Acl C:\data\recepten $acl

# Via icacls (CMD)
icacls C:\data\recepten                               # view permissions
icacls C:\data\recepten /grant "school\Huisartsen_DL:(OI)(CI)F"   # Full Control
icacls C:\data\recepten /grant "school\Apothekers_DL:(OI)(CI)R"   # Read
icacls C:\data\recepten /deny  Everyone:(OI)(CI)W                  # Deny write
icacls C:\data\recepten /inheritance:r                             # remove inherited
icacls C:\data\recepten /reset /T                                  # reset all permissions

# Sign-off command: show shares, ACLs, and Windows version
$shares = Get-SmbShare | where special -eq $False
$shares | Get-SmbShareAccess
(get-acl $shares.path).access | ft
gp "HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion" | select InstallationType

content:
# View DNS zones
Get-DnsServerZone

# Create forward lookup zone
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name school.test -ZoneFile school.test.dns

# Create reverse lookup zone for 172.16.1.0/24
Add-DnsServerPrimaryZone -NetworkID "172.16.1.0/24" -ZoneFile 1.16.172.in-addr.arpa.dns

# Add A record (hostname → IP)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name dc1  -IPv4Address 172.16.1.1
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name www  -IPv4Address 172.16.2.3
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name ftp  -IPv4Address 172.16.2.3
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordA -ZoneName school.test -Name sstp -IPv4Address 172.16.1.251

# Add CNAME record (alias → hostname)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordCName -ZoneName school.test -Name www -HostNameAlias rocky1.bmc.test

# Add MX record
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordMX -ZoneName school.test -Name "." -MailExchange mail.school.test -Preference 10

# Add PTR record (IP → hostname)
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordPtr -ZoneName 1.16.172.in-addr.arpa -Name 1 -PtrDomainName dc1.school.test
Add-DnsServerResourceRecordPtr -ZoneName 1.16.172.in-addr.arpa -Name 3 -PtrDomainName srv1.school.test

# View all records in a zone
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test
Get-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -RRType A

# Delete a record
Remove-DnsServerResourceRecord -ZoneName school.test -Name www -RRType A -RecordData 172.16.2.3 -Force

# Add DNS forwarders
Add-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 8.8.8.8
Add-DnsServerForwarder -IPAddress 9.9.9.9
Get-DnsServerForwarder

# Flush DNS server cache
Clear-DnsServerCache -Force

# Reload zone from file
Invoke-DnsServerZoneTransfer -Name school.test

# Allow zone transfer to specific IPs
Set-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name school.test -SecureSecondaries TransferToZoneNameServer
Set-DnsServerPrimaryZone -Name school.test -Notify Notify -NotifyServers 172.16.2.3

content:
# Authorize DHCP server in AD (required before it will hand out leases)
Add-DhcpServerInDC -DnsName rras1.school.test -IPAddress 172.16.1.251
Get-DhcpServerInDC

# Create IPv4 scope
Add-DhcpServerv4Scope -Name "LAN Rotterdam" -StartRange 172.16.1.10 -EndRange 172.16.1.100 -SubnetMask 255.255.255.0 -State Active
Add-DhcpServerv4Scope -Name "LAN DMZ" -StartRange 172.16.2.10 -EndRange 172.16.2.100 -SubnetMask 255.255.255.0 -State Active

# Set scope options: default gateway and DNS
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -Router 172.16.1.251
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -DnsServer 172.16.1.1, 172.16.2.3
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -DnsDomain school.test

# Set server-wide options
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -Router 172.16.1.251
Set-DhcpServerv4OptionValue -DnsServer 172.16.1.1

# Add exclusion range (IPs not handed out)
Add-DhcpServerv4ExclusionRange -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -StartRange 172.16.1.1 -EndRange 172.16.1.9

# Create reservation (always give same IP to specific MAC)
Add-DhcpServerv4Reservation -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 -IPAddress 172.16.1.50 -ClientId "AA-BB-CC-DD-EE-FF" -Name "PrinterLobby"

# View all scopes
Get-DhcpServerv4Scope

# View active leases
Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId 172.16.1.0
Get-DhcpServerv4Lease -ScopeId 172.16.1.0 | Select-Object IPAddress, HostName, ClientId

# View DHCP statistics
Get-DhcpServerv4Statistics

# Activate / deactivate a scope

# Allow RDP without Network Level Authentication
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\WinStations\RDP-Tcp" -Name UserAuthentication -Value 0

content:
# Enable WinRM (needed for PowerShell remoting and remote MMC)
Enable-PSRemoting -Force
winrm quickconfig

# Check WinRM service
Get-Service WinRM

# Add trusted hosts (required when NOT on same domain)
Set-Item WSMan:\localhost\Client\TrustedHosts -Value "192.168.1.10" -Force
Set-Item WSMan:\localhost\Client\TrustedHosts -Value "*" -Force         # trust all (lab only)
Get-Item WSMan:\localhost\Client\TrustedHosts                           # view

# Open interactive remote session
Enter-PSSession -ComputerName srv1.school.test -Credential school\administrator

# Run single command remotely
Invoke-Command -ComputerName srv1.school.test -ScriptBlock { Get-Service DHCP }
Invoke-Command -ComputerName srv1.school.test -ScriptBlock { Restart-Service DNS } -Credential school\administrator

# Run command on multiple servers at once
$servers = @("dc1", "srv1", "rras1")
Invoke-Command -ComputerName $servers -ScriptBlock { hostname }

# Copy file to remote computer
$s = New-PSSession -ComputerName srv1.school.test -Credential school\administrator
Copy-Item C:\setup.ps1 -Destination C:\temp\ -ToSession $s
Remove-PSSession $s

# Exit remote session
Exit-PSSession

content:
# Force GPO refresh on local machine
gpupdate /force
gpupdate /force /boot         # reboot if computer policy requires it
gpupdate /force /logoff       # log off if user policy requires it

# View which GPOs are applied and their status
gpresult /R                   # summary to console
gpresult /H C:\gp-report.html # full HTML report
gpresult /Scope Computer      # computer GPOs only
gpresult /Scope User          # user GPOs only

# Run gpresult on a remote computer for a specific user
gpresult /S srv1 /U school\jjansen /R

# Backup all GPOs
Backup-GPO -All -Path C:\GPOBackups

# Backup a single GPO
Backup-GPO -Name "Baseline Security" -Path C:\GPOBackups

# Restore GPO
Restore-GPO -Name "Baseline Security" -Path C:\GPOBackups

# Import GPO settings
Import-GPO -BackupGpoName "Baseline Security" -Path C:\GPOBackups -TargetName "Baseline Security"

# Create and link GPO
New-GPO -Name "Restrict Control Panel"
New-GPLink -Name "Restrict Control Panel" -Target "OU=kiosk-computers,DC=school,DC=test"

# List all GPOs
Get-GPO -All | Select-Object DisplayName, GpoStatus, CreationTime

# Block inheritance on OU
Set-GPInheritance -Target "OU=admin,DC=school,DC=test" -IsBlocked Yes

# Enforce a GPO link
Set-GPLink -Name "Baseline Security" -Target "DC=school,DC=test" -Enforced Yes

# View disks

Get-Disk | Format-Table Number, FriendlyName, OperationalStatus, Size

Initialize-Disk -Number 1 -PartitionStyle GPT
Initialize-Disk -Number 1 -PartitionStyle MBR

# Initialize all uninitialized disks

# Create a partition and assign drive letter
New-Partition -DiskNumber 1 -UseMaximumSize -AssignDriveLetter
New-Partition -DiskNumber 1 -Size 5GB -DriveLetter E

# Format the volume
Format-Volume -DriveLetter E -FileSystem NTFS -NewFileSystemLabel "AppData" -Confirm:$false
Format-Volume -DriveLetter E -FileSystem ReFS -NewFileSystemLabel "StoragePool" -Confirm:$false

# View volumes and partitions
Get-Volume
Get-Partition
Get-PSDrive -PSProvider FileSystem      # disk usage summary per drive

Remove-Partition -DiskNumber 1 -PartitionNumber 2 -Confirm:$false

# diskpart (CMD alternative)

# select disk 1      -> work on disk 1

# create partition primary size=5120  -> 5 GB partition
# format fs=ntfs quick label=Data
# assign letter=E
# exit

content:
# View available physical disks for pooling
Get-PhysicalDisk
Get-PhysicalDisk -CanPool $true

# Create a storage pool from multiple physical disks
$physdisks = Get-PhysicalDisk -CanPool $true
New-StoragePool -FriendlyName Webservers -StorageSubSystemFriendlyName "*Windows*" -PhysicalDisks $physdisks

# View pool
Get-StoragePool

# Create virtual disk (Simple=RAID0, Mirror=RAID1, Parity=RAID5)
New-VirtualDisk -StoragePoolFriendlyName Webservers -FriendlyName storagepool1 -Size 2GB -ResiliencySettingName Simple -ProvisioningType Thin

# Initialize and format the new virtual disk
$vd = Get-VirtualDisk -FriendlyName storagepool1
$vd | Get-Disk | Initialize-Disk -PartitionStyle GPT -PassThru |
    New-Partition -UseMaximumSize -DriveLetter E |
    Format-Volume -FileSystem ReFS -NewFileSystemLabel Applicatie_data -Confirm:$false

# Verify drive appears
Get-Volume -DriveLetter E

content:
# View available logs
Get-EventLog -List
Get-WinEvent -ListLog * | Where-Object {$_.RecordCount -gt 0} | Select-Object LogName, RecordCount

# Read recent events from common logs
Get-EventLog -LogName System      -Newest 20
Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Newest 20
Get-EventLog -LogName Security    -Newest 20

# Filter by type
Get-EventLog -LogName System -EntryType Error   -Newest 20
Get-EventLog -LogName System -EntryType Warning -Newest 20

# Filter by specific Event ID
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4624}        # successful logon
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4625}        # failed logon
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security'; Id=4740}        # account lockout
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System';   Id=6006}        # event log service stopped
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System';   Level=1}        # critical errors
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System';   Level=2}        # errors

# Filter by time range
$start = (Get-Date).AddHours(-2)
Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='System'; StartTime=$start}

# Search event messages for text
Get-EventLog -LogName System -Newest 100 | Where-Object {$_.Message -like "*DHCP*"}

# Export to CSV
Get-EventLog -LogName System -Newest 200 | Export-Csv C:\system-log.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Clear a log
Clear-EventLog -LogName Application

content:
# Navigate registry like a file system
Set-Location HKLM:
Get-ChildItem HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft
Get-Item HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion

# Read a single value
Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion" -Name ProductName
(Get-ItemProperty "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion").CurrentBuild

# Read all values in a key
Get-ItemProperty "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion"

# Set/update a registry value
Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server" -Name fDenyTSConnections -Value 0

# Create a new registry key
New-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp"

# Create a new registry value
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" -Name Version -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" -Name Name    -Value "MyApp" -PropertyType String

# Delete a value
Remove-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" -Name Version

# Delete a key and all subkeys
Remove-Item -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\MyApp" -Recurse

# Export/import via CMD
reg export "HKLM\SOFTWARE\MyApp" C:\backup\myapp.reg
reg import C:\backup\myapp.reg

# Useful registry paths
# HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<ServiceName>     = service config
# HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion        = OS version
# HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server    = RDP settings
# HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\WebManagement\Server             = IIS remote mgmt
# HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SstpSvc\Parameters = SSTP VPN

content:
# Check current time source and status
w32tm /query /source
w32tm /query /status
w32tm /query /peers

# Force immediate sync
w32tm /resync /force

# Set NTP server manually (on standalone server or PDC emulator)
w32tm /config /manualpeerlist:"pool.ntp.org" /syncfromflags:manual /update
Restart-Service w32tm

# Configure the PDC Emulator DC as authoritative time source
w32tm /config /manualpeerlist:"0.pool.ntp.org 1.pool.ntp.org" /syncfromflags:manual /reliable:yes /update
Restart-Service w32tm

# Domain-joined machines automatically use the DC as time source
# Test offset between client and DC:
w32tm /stripchart /computer:dc1.school.test /samples:5

content:
# Real-time performance counters
Get-Counter -Counter "\Processor(_Total)\% Processor Time"
Get-Counter -Counter "\Memory\Available MBytes"
Get-Counter -Counter "\PhysicalDisk(_Total)\Disk Reads/sec"
Get-Counter -Counter "\PhysicalDisk(_Total)\Disk Writes/sec"
Get-Counter -Counter "\Network Interface(*)\Bytes Total/sec"

# Sample every 2 seconds, 5 times
Get-Counter -Counter "\Processor(_Total)\% Processor Time" -SampleInterval 2 -MaxSamples 5

# Top 10 processes by CPU
Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending | Select-Object -First 10 | Format-Table Name, Id, CPU

# Top 10 processes by memory (in MB)
Get-Process | Sort-Object WorkingSet -Descending | Select-Object -First 10 | Select-Object Name, Id, @{N='MB';E={[math]::Round($_.WorkingSet/1MB,1)}}

# Disk usage per drive
Get-PSDrive -PSProvider FileSystem | Select-Object Name, @{N='Used(GB)';E={[math]::Round($_.Used/1GB,1)}}, @{N='Free(GB)';E={[math]::Round($_.Free/1GB,1)}}

# Quick resource snapshot via WMI
Get-WmiObject Win32_OperatingSystem | Select-Object @{N='FreeRAM_MB';E={[math]::Round($_.FreePhysicalMemory/1024,1)}}, TotalVisibleMemorySize

content:
# Install PSWindowsUpdate module (not present by default)
Install-Module PSWindowsUpdate -Force
Import-Module PSWindowsUpdate

# Check for available updates
Get-WindowsUpdate

# Install all updates
Install-WindowsUpdate -AcceptAll -AutoReboot

# Install without automatic reboot
Install-WindowsUpdate -AcceptAll -IgnoreReboot

# View update history
Get-WUHistory
Get-WUHistory | Select-Object -First 20 | Format-Table Date, Title

# View installed hotfixes
Get-HotFix
Get-HotFix | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending
Get-HotFix -Id KB5005033          # check specific hotfix

# Trigger update detection via CMD (older method)
wuauclt /detectnow
wuauclt /updatenow

# Via sconfig menu: Option 6 → Download and Install Updates

content:
# View certificates in machine store
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\Root
Get-ChildItem Cert:\CurrentUser\My

# Find certificate by subject
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Subject -like "*sstp*"}
Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.NotAfter -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(30)}  # expiring soon

# Export certificate with private key (PFX)
$cert = Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.Subject -like "*sstp*"}
Export-PfxCertificate -Cert $cert -FilePath C:\sstp.pfx -Password (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ss" -AsPlainText -Force)

# Export certificate without private key (CER)
Export-Certificate -Cert $cert -FilePath C:\sstp.cer

# Import PFX
Import-PfxCertificate -FilePath C:\sstp.pfx -CertStoreLocation Cert:\LocalMachine\My -Password (ConvertTo-SecureString "P@ss" -AsPlainText -Force)

# Pulse auto-enrollment (requests certs from CA)
certutil -pulse

# CA info commands
certutil -ping
certutil -cainfo
certutil -adca
certutil -verifykeys

# PowerShell CA cmdlets
Get-CATemplate
Get-CAAuthorityInformationAccess
Get-CACrlDistributionPoint

content:
# Install RRAS role
Install-WindowsFeature RemoteAccess -IncludeManagementTools
Install-WindowsFeature RSAT-RemoteAccess

# Register RRAS in AD (fixes error: 'unable to add to valid remote access servers')
netsh ras add registeredserver

# View active VPN connections (from server side)
Get-RemoteAccessConnectionStatistics

# Add a PPTP VPN connection profile (from client side)
Add-VpnConnection -Name "PPTP-VPN" -ServerAddress 172.16.2.251 -TunnelType PPTP -AuthenticationMethod EAP -AllUserConnection

# Add an SSTP VPN connection profile
Add-VpnConnection -Name "SSTP-VPN" -ServerAddress sstp.school.test -TunnelType SSTP -AuthenticationMethod Eap -AllUserConnection

# Connect via command line
rasdial "PPTP-VPN" administrator P@ssw0rd

# Disconnect
rasdial "PPTP-VPN" /disconnect

# Disable CRL check for SSTP (registry workaround)
New-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SstpSvc\Parameters" -Name NoCertRevocationCheck -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force

# View routing table
Get-NetRoute
netsh routing ip show rtmroutes

content:
# Immediate reboot
shutdown /r /t 0
Restart-Computer -Force

# Immediate shutdown
shutdown /s /t 0
Stop-Computer -Force

# Reboot with message and countdown (60 seconds)
shutdown /r /t 60 /c "Server rebooting for maintenance"

# Abort a pending shutdown (within the countdown)
shutdown /a

# Restart a remote computer
shutdown /m \\srv1 /r /t 0
Restart-Computer -ComputerName srv1 -Credential school\administrator -Force

# Logoff current user
logoff
shutdown /l

# Force restart (also via sconfig)
# sconfig → Option 13 = Restart
# sconfig → Option 14 = Shutdown

content:
# Check disk for errors (must schedule on next reboot for OS drive)
chkdsk C: /f /r

# Scan and repair Windows system files
sfc /scannow

# DISM — repair Windows image
Dism /Online /Cleanup-Image /CheckHealth
Dism /Online /Cleanup-Image /ScanHealth
Dism /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

# Check scheduled tasks
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v
Get-ScheduledTask
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object {$_.State -eq 'Running'}

# Create a scheduled task
$action  = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "powershell.exe" -Argument "-File C:\scripts\backup.ps1"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Daily -At 3am
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup" -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -RunLevel Highest

# Run a task immediately
Start-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup"

# Delete a task
Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "NightlyBackup" -Confirm:$false

# Run as a different user
runas /user:school\administrator cmd

# View hardware devices
Get-PnpDevice
Get-PnpDevice | Where-Object {$_.Status -ne 'OK'}

# Wait in scripts
Start-Sleep -Seconds 30
Start-Sleep -Milliseconds 500

content:
# Force AD replication
repadmin /syncall
repadmin /syncall /Ade         # sync all + verbose

# View replication status
repadmin /showrepl
repadmin /replsummary
repadmin /queue                # objects waiting to replicate
repadmin /kcc                  # trigger KCC topology recalculation

# DC diagnostics
dcdiag /test:connectivity
dcdiag /test:replications
dcdiag /test:kccevent
dcdiag /a                      # all tests on local DC
dcdiag /e                      # all tests on all DCs in enterprise

# View FSMO role holders
netdom query fsmo
Get-ADDomain | Select-Object InfrastructureMaster, PDCEmulator, RIDMaster
Get-ADForest | Select-Object DomainNamingMaster, SchemaMaster

# Transfer FSMO role
Move-ADDirectoryServerOperationMasterRole -Identity dc1.school.test -OperationMasterRole PDCEmulator -Force
